Name the capital cities of Europe.
St. Petersburg, Stockholm, Copenhagen, Berlin, The Hague, Brussels, Paris, Madrid, Lisbon, Rome, Vienna, Athens, Constantinople, Berne, London, Edinburgh.
With what country is England most extensively engaged in trade?
The United States.
Which is the most northern town in Europe, yet why is this port never frozen?
Hammerfest. Its immunity from freezing is due to the warm waters of the Gulf Stream, which flow by the coast of Norway, and the warm southwest return trade-winds.
Name the four most important rivers of Europe.
The Po, the Rhone, the Rhine and the Danube.
What countries are termed Spanish America?
Mexico, Central America, West Indies, and the greater part of South America.
What is the surface of Mexico?

The interior consists of tablelands from 4,000 to 9,000 feet high. The Pacific coast is abrupt and abounds in fine harbors; but on the Gulf of Mexico it is flat and sandy, and deficient in good harbors.
Are the tablelands accessible from the Gulf?
Only by two carriage-roads: one by Jalapa, from Vera Cruz; the other by Saltillo, from Matamoros and Monterey.
What is the climate?

As Mexico contains high and low lands, it exhibits a great variety of climate. In ascending from the coast to the surface of the plateau, there are three regions: the hot, swampy and pestilential lowlands; the temperate regions; and the cold, desolate plains above.
What are the inhabitants?
Whites, Indians, and Spanish Creoles. The Indians and mixed-bloods form the greater part of the population
How are the seasons divided?
Into the wet and the dry. The rainy season continues from May to October, and the dry from October to May.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are the agricultural products of Mexico?
They vary with its climate. Wheat and barley are produced in the cooler regions, and cotton, tobacco, sugar-cane, tropical fruits and spices in the plains and valleys below.
What is its commerce?
The commerce of Mexico is limited, and is mostly with England and the United States.
What are the principal minerals?
Gold and silver, iron and copper.
How is the City of Mexico situated?

On a plain 7,000 feet above the level of the sea and enclosed by lofty mountains. The city is about two miles from Lake Tezcuco, is in the form of a square, and is noted for its many churches, convents and plazas.
What two volcanoes are visible from the city?
Popocatapetl and Iztaccihuatl. The former is 17,717 feet high, and its name means Smoking Mountain. The latter is 15,600 feet high, and its name means White Lady.
What is cochineal?
The cochineal is an insect which feeds on a species of cactus. When killed and dried on the sun it yields a brilliant crimson dye.
What is pulque?
Pulque is a favorite Mexican beverage, manufactured from the Maguey plant. It is intoxicating.
What is briefly the history of Mexico?

In 1521 Fernando Cortez, a Spanish adventurer, conquered the country. He found there an extensive kingdom, with regular laws, and with cities, temples, public roads and many of the arts of civilization. After the conquest the cruelty of the Spaniards drove the Indians back into a state of barbarism. In 1821 Mexico became independent of Spain, and established first an empire and then a republic. The people are restless, and revolutions have been common. By the war with the United States, Mexico lost much valuable territory.
What peninsulas belong to this republic?
Yucatan and Lower California. They are little known. Yucatan is noted for its ruins of ancient cities and temples.
What is the government of Mexico?
It is a federal republic of twenty-seven states, one territory, and a small federal district which contains the capital. It was for three centuries the most important colony of Spain.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What does Central America comprise?
The five independent republics: Guatemala, Honduras, Salvador; Nicaragua and Costa Rica, and British Honduras. Central America bears a general resemblance to Mexico.
What are the capital cities?
San Salvador, Salvador; Managua, Nicaragua; Tegueigalpa, Honduras; Guatemala, Guatemala; San José, Costa Rica; and Belize, British Honduras.
Where is British Honduras?
South of the eastern part of Yucatan.
What are the exports of British Honduras?
Mahogany and other hard cabinet woods, cochineal, tortoise shells, sarsaparilla and cocoa-nuts.
What three routes have been projected for a canal from the Atlantic to the Pacific?
Across the Isthmus of Panama; by way of the San Juan River and Lake Nicaragua; and by the Isthmus of Tehuantepec.
How does Mexico compare in size to the United States?
It is about one-fourth as large.
In what zones is Mexico?
The north temperate and the torrid; the Tropic of Cancer crosses the central part.
Where are the greatest copper regions in the United States?
In northern Michigan and Wisconsin.
Where is lead found most abundantly?
In southwestern Wisconsin and those parts of Illinois and Iowa which adjoin, in Nevada, and in Colorado.
What are the great staple productions of the Southern United States?
Cotton, tobacco, rice, sugar, corn and sweet potatoes.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Which State produces the most (a) cotton, (b) rice, (c) sugar, (d) tobacco, (e) potatoes, (f) corn, (g) wheat?
(a) Texas; (b) South Carolina; (c) Louisiana; (d) Kentucky; (e) New York; (f) Iowa; (g) California.
Which State has the greatest number of (a) swine, (b) cattle, (c) horses, (d) mules, (e) sheep?
(a) Iowa; (b) Texas; (c) Illinois; (d) Missouri; (e) Texas.
Which State produces the greatest quantity of gold? Silver? Iron? Copper? Lead? Coal? Pine lumber? Turpentine, resin and tar?
Gold, California; silver, Colorado; iron, Pennsylvania; copper, Michigan; lead, Illinois; coal, Pennsylvania; lumber, Michigan; turpentine, etc., North Carolina.
How are turpentine, resin and tar manufactured?

Turpentine and resin are made of the sap of the pitch pine, by boiling; turpentine is the vapor, while resin is what remains. Tar is also made from the pitch, but by burning; the timber having been placed in nearly a perpendicular position, and covered to prevent a flame, the heat causes the pitch to ooze out and run below, where it can be saved.
From what does North Carolina derive her chief wealth?
From her pine forests.
What is South Carolina often called?
The "Palmetto State."
What article can be raised extensively in almost all the States?
Corn.
Into what physical divisions may we separate the United States?
Into the Atlantic Slope, the Pacific Slope, and the Central Plain.
What does the Atlantic Slope embrace?
The country which extends from the Alleghany Mountains to the Atlantic Ocean and Gulf of Mexico.
What does the Pacific Slope embrace?
The country between the Rocky Mountains and the Pacific Ocean.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is included in the Central Plain?
The Mississippi Valley, the Texas Slope, the basin of the St. Lawrence, and the valley of the Red River of the North.
How may we divide the United States with reference to drainage?
Into the St. Lawrence Basin, the Atlantic Slope, the Mississippi Valley, the Texas Slope, the Pacific Slope, the Inland Basin of Utah, and the Red River Basin.
What is the surface of Asia?
It is crossed by a system of mountains, tablelands and plains.
What division may we make in this country?
Arctic Asia, Desert Asia, Peninsular Asia and Insular Asia.
What part is included in each?

Arctic Asia takes in the Great Siberian Plain. Desert Asia is the belt of high and dry plateaus extending through the central sections. Peninsular Asia is the most important division of the continent, and includes Hindoostan, Indo-China and Corea, with the projecting part of China. Insular Asia includes the richest and most important archipelagoes in the world.
What portion of the human race is in Peninsular Asia?
One-half.
What can be said of the productions of Asia?
As Asia embraces all varieties of climates, its productions range from arctic to tropical.
What is the longest inland commercial route on the globe?
The caravan trade from Pekin through Irkoutsk, Tobolsk, to Moscow and St. Petersburg.
How are the tablelands divided?
Into those of Central and those of Southern Asia. They are the loftiest and most extensive in the world.
What countries constitute the Great Northern Plain of Asia?
Turkestan and Siberia. This is a continuation of the great plain of Europe.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Describe the mountain system of Asia.

It begins with the Taurus Mountains, and is continued by the Elburz and Hindoo Koosh to the Balor Mountains. Thence it branches off into four great chains—the Himalaya, Kuen Lun, Thian Shan, and Alti—which are the most stupendous mountains on the globe.
Where is the Great Desert of Gobi?
Near the central part of Asia, south of the Thian Shan Mountains. This is only a part of an immense belt of desert land which extends almost across the continent from west to east.
What are the climatic divisions?
The three of Northern, Central, and Southern Asia.
What is the climate of Northern Asia?
It is characterized by intense cold, with short intervals of great heat during the summer.
Describe Siberia.

It is a great plain, comprising more than one-third of Asia. Near the Arctic Ocean it is a barren and desolate region, and the cold is so intense that the spongy soil is frozen to the depth of several hundred feet. Near the Irtysh River the soil is rich and the pasturage good, but there are few inhabitants.
What is the climate of Central and Southern Asia?

Central Asia has the climate of the temperate zone. It is subject to great extremes of heat and cold, and, except near the shores of the Pacific, is remarkably dry, for the mountain system intercepts the warm and moist winds of the Indian Ocean. Southern Asia has the climate of the torrid zone.
To what three races do the inhabitants of Asia belong?
Mongolian, Caucasian and Malay.
What people belong to the Mongolian race?
The nations living north of the Himalaya Mountains — those of Farther India, the Japanese and the Chinese.
What nations, or tribes, living in Asia belong to the Caucasian race?
The Georgians, Armenians, Arabs, Persians, Afghans and Hindoos.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What countries are occupied by the Malay race?
The peninsula of Malacca, and many of the islands of Oceanica.
For what has Siberia long been used?
For a place of banishment for exiles and criminals. A large part of the population consists of them and their descendants.
To what country does Georgia belong?
It is a Russian province.
What is noticeable of the inhabitants?
The people are a vigorous and handsome race.
How is Turkestan divided?

Into Russian and Independent Turkestan. Russian Turkestan contains a number of districts called khanates, which are ruled by native rulers, although only as vassals of Russia. Independent Turkestan includes those Tartar khanates which are not yet annexed by Russia, representing only a small area of what was formerly Turkestan.
What is the Khan?
The name given to the rulers of the several independent states of Turkestan.
What does the Chinese Empire include?
China, the dependent provinces of Mantchooria, Mongolia, Kashgaria and Thibet.
What is said of the population of China?

It is so dense in some districts that great numbers of the inhabitants dwell constantly in boats on the rivers, or on rafts on the lakes, which they convert into gardens by covering them with earth. They are a very industrious people, and jealous of European aggression.
What are the productions of China?
The most important are rice, tea and silk. Rice is the chief article of food for the entire population.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the government?
A despotic empire. The laws are severe; for trifling offenses punishment is inflicted with the bamboo whip, while serious crimes almost always meet with death.
What about education in China?
Education is compulsory. All adult males are able to read and write and possess a knowledge of the elements of arithmetic.
How are all government officers below the emperor selected?
By means of public competitive examinations.
What can be said of these people in regard to trade, the arts and sciences?

Their domestic commerce is immense, but their foreign commerce is in the hands of foreigners. Their skill in various occupations cannot be excelled, and many of our most useful inventions were known to them hundreds of years before they were known to the Europeans, but were not put to a practical use.
Describe the great wall of China. For what purpose was it built?
The wall is 1,200 miles long, from thirty to forty feet high, and twenty feet thick, with many towers and equipments for defense. It was built about 211 B.C. to keep the Mongol Tartars out of China.
Which are the most important seaports for foreign trade?
Canton, Shanghai, Ningpo, Fuchow, Amoy.
Name the principal islands of Japan.
Nipon, Sikoke, Kiusiu and Jesso.
What are the chief productions of these islands?
Rice, wheat, barley, tea, tobacco and camphor.
What is the government of Japan?
A constitutional monarchy.
What is the emperor called?
The Mikado.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How do the Japanese compare in enlightenment with the Chinese?

They surpass the Chinese in intelligence and equal them in mechanical skill. Although of related races, they differ greatly in their language and political institutions. They are quick to adopt the great inventions of the white civilized peoples, such as their military systems, lighthouses, steam-power and electricity.
What two peninsulas comprise India?
Hindoostan and Indo-China.
What does British India include?
Nearly all of Hindoostan and the western coast of Indo China. It is the richest and most important dependency ever possessed by any nation.
How is Indo-China divided?
Between Burmah, Siam, Anam and many smaller independent states. All have despotic governments.
What peculiarity is found in the literature of the Hindoos?
It is written in a peculiar poetical and religious phraseology which has been in use since long before the Christian era. Besides being poetical, the Hindoos are almost universally musical.
What is the government of British India?
A Governor-General and a Supreme Council are appointed by the British government. The Queen of England is Empress of India.
What and where is Ceylon?
It is a rich and populous island south of Hindoostan, and a separate colony of Great Britain.
Where is Cochin-China?
The southeastern coast of Indo-China is called Cochin-China.
Where do the French hold possessions?
They possess an important province in the southwestern part, with Saigon for its capital.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are the productions of India?
Cotton, rice, wheat, opium, sugar, indigo, jute, fine silk fabrics and costly cashmere shawls.
For what is Ceylon especially noted?
For its coffee and cinnamon.
Describe Afghanistan.

Persia, Afghanistan and Beloochistan occupy the desert plateau of Iran, between the valleys of the Tigris and the Indus. The people of Afghanistan are hardy, warlike, and divided among many tribes. As this country is the chief route from India to Western Asia, it is of great commercial and military importance.
What can be said of Beloochistan?
This country is mostly a barren wilderness, and consists of a number of small states under the headship of the Khan of Kelat.
What is the government of Persia?
A despotic monarchy, with a sovereign called a Shah.
What is the surface of Arabia?
The greater part is a desert plateau. The principal fertile tracts are Oman and Yemen, and the mountain valleys.
Describe the inhabitants.

The greater part of them are Bedouins, a wandering people who live in the deserts, devoting themselves to the care of their camels, horses, goats and sheep. They are generous and hospitable, but vengeful and addicted to plunder.
For what are Mecca and Medina noted?
Mecca is regarded by Mohammedans as a holy city, the birthplace of Mohammed, and is annually visited by vast numbers of pilgrims. Medina is venerated as the burial-place of Mohammed.
What is the general appearance of the Persians?
They are polite in their manners, and, having a comparatively high civilization, are often called the French of Asia.
What can be said of the past history of Persia?
It was once one of the grandest and most formidable governments of the world.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Describe Arabia.

Arabia is about one-third as large as the United States. A great plateau, having many large and fertile valleys, occupies the interior; surrounding this is a broad belt of deserts, covering more than one-third of the peninsula; while along the coasts is a low and narrow sandy plain, containing a few fertile districts.
What is its government?
There are several independent states in Arabia, governed by despots called Sultans.
Name the peninsulas of Asia.
Arabia, Hindoostan, Malacca, Korea, and Kamtschatka.
Where is the Isthmus of Kraw?
It connects Malacca with the mainland.
Which is the most elevated lake in the world?
Sirikol, the source of the Amoo River, on the tableland of Pamer. It is 15,600 feet above the level of the sea.
Where is Palestine?
Palestine is in the northeastern part of Arabia, on the Mediterranean Sea.
Where is Jerusalem?
Jerusalem is the chief city of Palestine.
How wide is Bering Strait? Dover? Gibraltar?
Bering forty miles, Dover thirty, Gibraltar twelve.
How wide is the Isthmus of Panama? Suez? Tehuantepec?
Panama twenty-seven miles, Suez sixty-five, Tehuantepec one hundred and thirty.
Which are the five largest islands in the world?
Australia, Greenland, Borneo, New Guinea and Madagascar.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are the sizes of the oceans?
The Pacific Ocean contains about 82,000,000 square miles; the Atlantic, 30,000,000; the Indian, 22,000,000; the Antarctic, 12,000,000; and the Arctic, 4,000,000.
In the production of what minerals does Great Britain exceed every other country?
Coal, iron, salt, lead, and tin.
Name the largest seven rivers in the United States.
Describe them. Mississippi, Missouri, Arkansas, Rio Grande, Columbia, Nebraska, and Red.
What are the principal uses of rivers?
For drainage, commerce and milling.
What important places of the globe are situated on or near the 40th parallel north latitude?
Philadelphia, Columbus, Indianapolis, Springfield, Denver, Pekin, Constantinople, and Madrid.
What part of the United States is in the same latitude north of the equator, that the southern part of Africa is south of it?
The boundary line which divides the States of Mississippi, Alabama and Georgia from Tennessee.
What are the latitudes of Cape Horn and the Cape of Good Hope?
Cape Horn is fifty-three degrees south, and Good Hope about thirty-two degrees.
What is the difference between the natural location of commercial and manufacturing cities?
Commercial cities require good harbors and water communication to facilitate commerce, while manufacturing cities depend very much upon water power to run their machinery.
Why is the water of Great Salt Lake not fresh?
Lakes of this class lose water by evaporation only, and retain the salt and other materials carried into them by their affluent rivers.
Name five of the principal articles exported by the people of the United States.
Cotton, wheat, pork, cheese, machinery.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Name some of the principal articles imported?
Wool, tea, coffee and spices, dry goods, sugar and liquors.
What are the great waterways for the internal commerce of the United States?
The Atlantic system, the Mississippi system, the Great Lakes with the St. Lawrence River, and the canals.
Describe the Mississippi system.

This system contains fifty-six great tributaries, containing 17,000 miles navigable to steamboats and more than 20,000 by barge. It carries an immense commerce, but in some localities the annual floods and droughts prevent a continuous shipment. Below the mouth of the Ohio the Mississippi sometimes rises more than fifty feet.
What does the St. Lawrence system comprise?

The Great Lakes and the navigable streams which flow into and through the St. Lawrence River. This system is closed for several months in the year on account of ice, but floods or droughts never affect it.
Where are the chief canals?
They lie at the entrance of Lake Superior, the Niagara Falls, the Rapids of the St. Lawrence and the Erie Canal.
What change in the political status of the peninsula of Korea occurred in 1910?
It was annexed to the Japanese Empire.
Give its area and population.
It is about 600 miles long and 135 miles wide, and contains 10,000,000 inhabitants.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What country was absorbed by the British Empire in 1900, by war and conquest?
The Transvaal Republic, in South Africa, a country about three times the size of Ohio; rich in gold and diamond mines, but only having about 1,500,000 population.
In what parts of the world do they raise the most corn?
The United States, south central Europe and southeastern part of South America.
In what parts the most wheat?
The United States, Russia, France, India and Austria-Hungary.
In what parts do they produce the most sugar?
Cuba first and Java second.
In what parts the most coffee?
Brazil, Venezuela, Guatemala, Dutch East Indies.
What country produces the most coal, iron, petroleum, wheat, cotton, and manufactured goods?
The United States. No wonder we are a world power.
The area and population of the five principal nations, together with their production of wheat, cotton, coal and manufactured goods.

Area British Empire 11,444,000 Russian Empire 8,800,000 France & Poss. 4,300,000 United States & Poss. 3,800,000 Germany & Poss. 1,200,000 Population British Empire 392,800,000 Russian Empire 143,800,000 United States & Poss. 101,000,000 France & Poss. 91,000,000 Germany & Poss. 72,000,000 Wheat United States 737,000,000 Bushels Russia 711,000,000 Bushels France 365,000,000 Bushels India 300,000,000 Bushels Austria-Hungary 224,000,000 Bushels Cotton United States 10,600,000 Bales India 2,600,000 Bales China 1,200,000 Bales Egypt 1,150,000 Bales Korea 300,000 Bales Coal United States 414,000,000 Tons Great Britain 281,000,000 Tons Germany 222,000,000 Tons Austria-Hungary 45,000,000 Tons France 37,000,000 Tons Manufactured Goods United States 13,000 Million Dollars United Kingdom 5,000 Million Dollars Germany 4,500 Million Dollars France 3,500 Million Dollars

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is mathematics?
The science of quantity.
What is quantity?
Anything that can be increased, diminished or measured.
Define arithmetic as a science and as an art.
As a science, it logically investigates and philosophically classifies and arranges the principles and rules of the subject; as an art, it applies these principles.
Upon what is arithmetic founded, and how are its operations carried on?
It is founded on notation, and its operations are carried on by means of addition, subtraction, multiplication and division.
What is arithmetical language?
The method of expressing numbers. It is of two kinds, oral and written.
What is a unit?
One, or a single thing.
What is a number? The power of a number? A root?
A number is a unit, or a collection of units. The power of a number is the product produced by repeating a number so many times as a factor. A root is a factor repeated to produce power.
What is an integer?
A whole number.
What is the difference between an abstract number and a concrete number?

An abstract number is one used without reference to any particular thing; as, 1, 6, 15, etc. A concrete number is one that has reference to a particular thing; as, one tree, five boys, fifteen houses, etc.
What is a sign?
A sign is a character indicating an operation to be performed; as, ÷, X, —, +, √.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the sign of aggregation?
The sign of aggregation is a parenthesis, or bar, which shows that the numbers enclosed by it are to be considered together, subject to the same operation; as, (5 × 6 ÷ 2) or 5 × 6 ÷ 2.
What is a rule? A problem? An axiom?
A rule is a prescribed method of performing an operation. A problem is a question or proposition to be solved. An axiom is any truth which is self-evident.
What is analysis in arithmetic?
The process of investigating principles, and solving problems, independently of set rules.
What is notation?
Notation is a method of writing or expressing numbers by characters.
What is numeration?
Numeration is a method of reading numbers expressed by characters.
How many systems of notation are in general use?
Two: the Roman and the Arabic. Roman notation: V, IX, XC, DC, MDCVI, etc. Arabic notation: I, 35, 156, 1701, etc.
Upon what is the Roman notation founded?

Upon five principles: first, repeating a letter repeats its value; second, if a letter of any value is placed after one of greater value, it is added to the greater; third, if a letter of any value is placed before a greater, it is to be taken from the greater; fourth, if a letter of any value is placed between letters of higher value, it is to be taken from their united values; fifth, if a bar or dash is placed over a letter, it increases its value a thousandfold.
In how many ways may numbers be written?
In words, letters, and figures; as, five, V. 5.
In the Arabic system of notation numbers are expressed according to what principle?
According to the positions of the nine characters.
Why is the Arabic system of notation also called the decimal system?
Because of its increase and decrease in a tenfold ratio, from decem, a Latin word meaning ten.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the difference between the simple and local value of a figure?
The simple value of a figure is its value when taken alone, while the local value depends upon the place it occupies with other figures.
What is the fundamental synthetic process of arithmetic?
Addition, for by it we pass from unity to plurality; from the one to the many.
Give the principles of addition.
Only similar numbers can be added. The sum is a number similar to the numbers added. The sum is the same in whatever order the numbers are added.
What are the cases in, or modes of addition?
Mental addition and written addition.
Why do we write the numbers as suggested by the arith-methics, and why do we begin at the right hand to add?
Merely for convenience.
What is the fundamental analytical process of arith-metic?
Subtraction.
Give the principles of subtraction?

Similar numbers, only, can be subtracted. The differ-ence is a number similar to the numbers subtracted. If the two numbers are equally increased or diminished the re-mainder will be the same. If the first number is equal to the sum of the second and the remainder, then the second must equal the difference between the first and the remainder.
Name and define the cases in subtraction?
One is expressed in the Arabic system, the other by some mental calculation.
Name the principles of multiplication.

The multiplier is always an abstract number. The product of two numbers is the same, whichever is made the multiplier. If the multiplicand be multiplied by all of the parts of the multiplier, the sum of all the partial products will be the true product. The multiplicand equals the quo-tient of the product divided by the multiplier; the multi-plier equals the quotient of the product divided by the multiplicand.
Why are the multiplicand and multiplier taken together called factors?
Because they make the product.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are the terms of division?
The dividend, divisor and quotient.
Upon what does the quotient depend?
Upon both dividend and divisor.
Show how the value of the quotient depends on the relation of dividend and divisor.
If one of these terms is changed it changes the quotient, but if both are changed alike the changes neutralize each other and the quotient remains the same.
What principles of division are deduced from these relations?

Multiplying the dividend or dividing the divisor by any number multiplies the quotient by that number. Dividing the dividend or multiplying the divisor by any number divides the quotient by that number. Multiplying or dividing both dividend and divisor by the same number does not change the quotient.
In division, what relation exists in regard to the quality of abstractness and concreteness?

The quotient may be either abstract or concrete. It will be abstract when the dividend and divisor are both abstract, or both concrete. It will be concrete when the dividend is concrete and the divisor abstract. Either the divisor or quotient must always be an abstract number. An abstract number cannot be divided by a concrete number.
How are numbers classified?
As even and odd; prime and composite; integral and fractional; abstract and concrete; simple and compound; like and unlike.
What are the minuend and subtrahend?
The minuend is the number subtracted from; the subtrahend is the number subtracted.
What is a composite number?
One that can be produced by multiplying two or more numbers together. Thus, 36 is a composite number because 3 X 12 will produce it, and 4 X 9 will, also.
What is factoring?
The process of separating numbers into factors.
What are the component factors of a number?
The several numbers which, being multiplied together, produce the number.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are the powers of a number?

The first power is the number itself, or the root. The second power, or square, is the number multiplied by itself. The third power, or cube, is the product arising from using the number three times as a factor.
What is the reciprocal of a number?
It is 1 divided by that number.
How do we find the true remainder by dividing by factors?

Multiply each partial remainder, except the first, by all the preceding divisors, and add these products to the first remainder. It will be noticed that after the first division the remainder cannot be of the same denomination as the first number divided; and as each succeeding division increases the value of the remainders, each remainder must be multiplied by all the preceding divisors to reduce it to its former denomination.
What is a prime number? When are numbers prime to each other?
One which cannot be resolved into factors. Numbers are prime to each other when they have no common divisor. 17 is a prime number and so is 29.
What are a common divisor, and the greatest common divisor?
One which will divide two or more numbers without a remainder. The greatest common divisor is the greatest number that will divide two or more numbers without a remainder.
What are a multiple, a common multiple, and the least common multiple?

A multiple is a number exactly divisible by a given number. A common multiple is one exactly divisible by two or more numbers. The least common multiple is the least number that is exactly divisible by two or more given numbers. Thus, 24 is a multiple of 4.
What are fractions?
Parts of a unit.
What is the value of a fraction?
The quotient of the numerator divided by the denomi-nator.
What do the numerator and the denominator respect-ively show?
The denominator shows into how many parts a number is divided; the numerator shows how many parts are taken.
What are a proper fraction, an improper fraction, a mixed fraction and a complex fraction?

A proper fraction is one whose numerator is smaller than its denominator. An improper fraction is one whose numerator is equal to or greater than its denominator. A mixed number is one expressed by an integer and fraction written together. A complex fraction is one that has a fraction in its numerator, or in its denominator, or in both.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Is a fraction a number?

A fraction is a number of equal parts of unity, and consequently must represent a subdivision of numbers: thus, 5 feet is a number, and the fraction ⁵⁄₁₂ of a foot represents equal numbers of a lower order, as 5 inches.
Is a fraction a denominate number?
No.
Why do we invert the divisor in division of fractions?
For convenience; if it is not inverted the fractions must be reduced to a common denominator, and one numerator divided by the other.
How do we find the greatest common divisor of fractions?
We find the greatest common divisor of the given numerators, and the least common multiple of the denominators.
How do we find the least common multiple of fractions?
By finding the least common multiple of the numerators, and the greatest common divisor of the denominators.
What are decimal fractions?
Fractions whose denominators are increased or decreased in tenfold ratio.
What is the first thing to be considered in the treatment of decimals?
The method of reading and writing them, or their numeration and notation.
What will the denominator of a decimal always be?
The unit 1 with as many ciphers annexed as are equal to the number of figures in the decimal or numerator.
Upon what does the value of a decimal depend?
Its distance from the decimal point.
How do decimals increase and decrease?
They increase from right to left, and decrease from left to right, in a tenfold ratio.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is a pure decimal?
A pure decimal consists of a decimal only; for example, .35, which is read, as if written, 35/100.
What is a mixed decimal?
One with an integer and a decimal; thus, 15.7.
What is a complex decimal?
One with a fraction annexed; thus, .005¾.
From what are the names of decimal orders derived?
From the names of the orders of integers.
What is the rule for the multiplication of decimals?
Multiply as in simple numbers, and point off as many decimals in the product as the sum of the decimals in both multiplier and multiplicand. Examples: .12 3.02 .08 .04 .0096 .1208
What is the rule for the division of decimals?
Divide as in simple numbers, and point off as many decimals in the quotient as the decimal places of the dividend exceed those of the divisor. Examples: .12).132 .001).3482 1.1 348.2
What is a circulating decimal?
A decimal in which a figure or set of figures are continually repeating.
What is a repetend?
The figure or set of figures continually repeated.
What is a continued fraction?
A fraction whose numerator is 1, and whose denominatoris a whole number, plus a fraction whose numerator is also I, and whose denominator is a similar fraction, etc.
What is the difference between decimal fractions and common fractions?

The decimal fraction has a denominator understood, showing that a unit is divided into ten equal parts, or subdivided in a tenfold ratio; the second has a denominator expressed, showing that a unit is divided into any number of equal parts.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is currency?
The medium of circulation.
From what was the sign $ derived?
From the initial letters of the United States, U.S. joined together as a monogram.
What is an aliquot part of a number?
Such a part as will exactly divide that number.
What is a bill?
A written statement of articles bought or sold, together with the price of each and the whole cost.
What is a compound number?
A concrete number, whose value is expressed in two or more different denominations.
What is a denominate number?
A concrete number in which the unit of measure is established by law or custom.
What is a simple denominate number?
A denominate number composed of units of the same denomination; as, 3 feet.
What is a compound denominate number?

One composed of two or more denominations which are related to each other; as, 3 feet 4 inches. Example: Reduce 5 yds., 2 ft., 3 in. to inches. --- --- --- --- Ans. 207 or 5 X 3 + 2 X 12 + 3 = 207 in., Ans.
What is a standard unit?
A unit of measure from which the other units of the same kind may be derived.
Name the quantities of magnitude which give rise to denominate numbers?
They are value, weight, length, surface, volume, capacity, angles, time.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the standard unit of value?
Money is the standard measure of value, and in the United States the standard unit is the dollar. In English money the pound sterling is the unit.
What is the standard unit of weight?
The Troy pound.
What is the standard unit of length?
The yard.
What is the standard unit of surface?
The square yard for ordinary measurement, and the acre for land.
What is the standard unit of volume?

The cubic yard for ordinary measurement, and the cord for wood. Example: Divide 45 A. of land among seven children so that each child will have an equal portion. Solution: 45 A. ÷ 7 = 6 A. and 3 A. remaining. 3 A. × 160 = 480 ÷ 7 = 68 sq. rds. and 4 sq. rds. remaining. 4 sq. rds. × 30¼ = 121 ÷ 7 = 17 sq. yds. and 2 sq. yds. remaining. 2 sq. yds. × 9 = 18 ÷ 7 = 2 sq. ft. and 4 sq. ft. remaining. 4 sq. ft. × 144 = 576 ÷ 7 = 82 sq. in. and 2 sq. in. remaining. Ans. 6 A., 68 sq. rds., 17 sq. yds., 2 sq. ft., 82⅔ sq. in. to each child.
What is the standard unit of capacity?
The gallon for fluids, and the bushel for dry measure.
What is the standard unit of angles?
The right angle.
What is the standard unit of time?
The day. This is determined by the revolution of the earth.
What is a measure?
That by which extent, dimension, capacity, or amount is ascertained. Measures are of eight kinds: length, surface or area, solidity, weight, time, angles, capacity and money.
How many dimensions has extension?
Extension has three: length, breadth, and thickness.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Explain how distance is measured by time?

Every circle is supposed to be divided into 360 equal parts called degrees. As the earth makes its revolution once in 24 hours, the 24 hours must equal 360°, or 1 hour, 15° of distance. By subdividing, we find that 1° would equal 4 minutes, and 1/15 of a degree would equal 4 seconds.
How do you find the difference in time corresponding to any given difference in longitude?
Divide the difference in longitude, expressed in degrees, minutes and seconds, by 15, and the respective quotients will be hours, minutes, and seconds.
How do you find the difference in longitude corresponding to any given difference in time?
Multiply the difference in time, expressed in hours, minutes and seconds, by 15, and its respective products will be degrees, minutes and seconds of longitude.
How do you find the time at one place, when the time at another place and the difference of these places in time are known?
When the second place is east of the first, add their difference of time; when it is west of the first, subtract their difference of time.
How do navigators determine their longitude at sea?

Taking with them a chronometer set to mark the time at a given place — as Greenwich or Washington — they ascertain by observation of the sun with the sextant the time at the place they are in, reduce the difference of time to the difference of longitude, and thus ascertain that they are so many degrees east or west of the meridian of the place for which the chronometer is set.
What is a scale?
A series of numbers, descending or ascending, used in operations upon compound numbers.
What is weight?
A measure of the quantity of matter a body contains, according to some fixed standard.
Describe Gunter's chain.
It consists of 100 links, each link containing 7.92 inches, or the whole length 66 feet, or 4 rods.
What is the difference between a pound Troy and a pound avoirdupois?
The Troy pound has 5,760 grains: the avoirdupois, 7,000.
What is linear measure?
That which is used in measuring lines or distances.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are a square, and a cube?
A square is a superficial figure having four equal sides and four equal angles; a cube is a solid figure having six equal sides.
How many inches in a wine gallon, in a beer gallon, and in a bushel?
In a wine gallon, 231 cubic inches; in a beer gallon, 282; in a bushel, 2,150.4 cubic inches.
What are duodecimals?
The division and subdivision of a unit, resulting by continually dividing by 12.
What is percentage? What is its base?
Such part of a number as is represented by the per cent. Its base is the number on which the percentage is reckoned.
What do the words per cent, mean?
By the hundred.
Name and define the elements involved in percentage?

The elements are: the base, or number of which the per cent. is taken; the rate, or number of hundredths taken; the percentage, or quantity taken; the amount, or sum of the base and the percentage; and the difference, or base less the percentage.
What is the difference between rate and rate per cent?
The rate is the number of hundredths, and the rate per cent is the fraction which denotes how many hundredths are taken. Thus, in 3%, or 3/100, 3 is the rate and 3/100 the rate per cent.
Give the applications of percentage.
The rules of percentage are applied to the computing of interest, discount, commission, taxes, insurance, duties, and especially profit and loss.
What is commission? What is brokerage?

The percentage allowed an agent, factor or commission merchant, is commission. Brokerage is the fee paid to a dealer in money, stocks, etc., for the transaction of business. The reader should always bear in mind that 50% of a number would be ½ of it; 33⅓% of a number is ⅓ of it; and 20% of anything is ⅕ of it; 5% of a number is ¹⁄₂₀ of it; while 2% of anything is ¹⁄₅₀ of it. Example 1. Find 9% of $284. Solution: $284 × .09 = $25.56, Ans. Example 2. $21.96 is 6% of what number? Solution: $21.96 ÷ .06 = $366, Ans. Example 3. $430 is 7½% more than what number? Solution: That number must be 100% of itself. Then 100% + 7½% must = $430. $430 ÷ 107½ = $4, which is 1% of the number, and 100% = 100 × $4, or $400, Ans. Example 4. A man bought 50 shares of stock at 96½ and sold it at par. Allowing ½c. brokerage on each transaction, how much did he gain on the 50 shares, provided the shares were $10 each? Solution: One dollar's worth of stock would cost him 96½c. + ½c. or 97c.; and a share would cost him 97c. X 10 or $9.70; and 50 shares would cost him $9.70 X 50 or $485. Now he sells one dollar's worth for 100c. — ¼c. brokerage, or 99¾c., and a ten-dollar share would bring him 99¾c. X 10, or $9.98¾; and 50 shares would bring him $9.98¾ X 50, or $499.37½. And $499.37½ — $485 = $14.37½, Ans. Example 5. A man had $3875 to invest in stock, which was quoted in the market at 96½; if he had to pay also ¼c. commission, how many one-hundred-dollar shares could he buy? Solution: One dollar's worth of stock would cost him 96½c. + ¼c., or 96¾c.; and with his $3875 he could purchase as many dollars' worth of stock as .96¾ is contained in $3875, or $4015.54; and there would be as many shares as $100 is contained in $4015.54, or 40 shares, with $15.54 still uninvested. Example 6. If a company declares a dividend on its stock of 4½ per cent. and this dividend amounts in cash to $4050, what is the amount of the stock of that company? Solution: If one dollar of stock yields 4½c., then to yield $4050 it would take as many dollars of stock as 4½c. is contained in $4050, or $90,000 worth of stock. $4050 ÷ .04½ = $90,000, Ans. Example 7. A man bought 250 one-hundred-dollar shares of stock at 99 per cent., and sold 100 shares at 101 per cent., and 150 shares at 98 per cent. If he paid ⅛c. brokerage for the buying, and ⅛c. brokerage for the selling of all of it, did he gain or lose in the transaction, and how much? Solution: 99c. + ⅛c. = 99⅛c. X 100 = $99.12½, cost of one share. $99.12½ X 250 = $24,781.25, cost of entire stock. 1.01 — ⅛c. = 1.00⅞ X 100 = $100.87½, selling price of one share. $100.87½ X 100 = $10,087.50, selling price of one hundred shares. .98 - ¼c. = .97¾ X 100 = $97.87½, selling price of one share. $97.87½ X 150 = $14,681.25, selling price of one hundred and fifty shares. $24,781.25 cost — ($10,087.50 + $14,681.25) = $12.50 loss, Ans. Example 8. A company has a capital stock of $75,000, and loses $1,875 the first year in business. What per cent. of assessment must be made on the stockholders to make up this deficit? Solution: If $75,000 must make up a loss of $1,875, then one dollar of the stock would be assessed ⁷⁵⁰⁰⁰ of $1,875, or 2½ per cent., Ans. 75000)1875.000(.025 150000 375000 375000
What is a commission merchant?
An agent who buys and sells goods for another on commission.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are a consignee, and a consignor?
A person who receives goods to sell for another is a consignee; a consignor is a person who sends goods to another to be sold.
What is a company? What is a corporation?
A company is a group of individuals united for the purpose of performing some business or undertaking. A corporation is a company formed and authorized by law to act as one person.
What is a share of stock, and what is a stockholder?
Stock is the amount of capital invested, and a share is one of the equal parts into which capital stock is divided. A stockholder is the owner of stock.
What is the difference between a charter and a firm?
A charter defines the powers of an incorporated body, while a firm is the name under which an unincorporated body transacts business.
What is stock at par?
Stock is at par when it sells for its face value; it is said to be above par when it sells for more, and below par when it sells for less.
What is profit and loss?
A commercial term, used to express the gain or loss in business transactions.
How do we find the loss or gain per cent. when the cost and selling price are given?
Divide the gain or loss by the purchase price, or cost.
How do we find the selling price when the cost and gain or loss per cent. are given?
Multiply the purchase price by $1, increased or diminished, as the case may be, by the gain or loss per cent.
How do we find the cost when the selling price and the gain or loss per cent. are given?

Divide the selling price by $1, increased or diminished, as the case may be, by the gain or loss per cent. Example 1. How should goods be marked, that cost $25, so there will be a gain of 12% when sold? Solution: 12% of $25 is $3, and $25 + $3 = $28, Ans. Example 2. Goods were marked to sell for $59, which was at a profit of 18%. What was the cost of the goods? Solution: The goods cost 100%, and if they sold at a profit of 18%, then $59 is 118% of the cost; and $59 ÷ 118 = .50 or 1%; and 100% = .50 × 100, or $50, cost of goods, Ans. Example 3. A house was sold for $2,600 that cost the owner $2,925. What was his loss per cent.? Solution: $2925 - $2600 = $325, the loss in money; and if $2925, the cost, lost $325, then one dollar of the cost would lose 325/2925 of $325, or 11 1/9%, Ans.
What is an instalment? An assessment? A dividend?

An instalment is an advance payment required of the stockholders on their subscriptions to the capital stock. An assessment is a sum required of the stockholders to meet the losses of the business expenses of the company. A dividend is a sum paid to the stockholders from the profits of the business.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is insurance? A policy? A premium?

Insurance is security on property guaranteed by one party to another for a stipulated sum, against the loss of property by any casualty. A policy is a written contract between the parties. A premium is the sum periodically paid for insurance.
Define an underwriter.
An underwriter is an insurer—the person who takes the risk.
Name the kinds of insurance.
There are six different kinds of insurance: Fire insurance, Marine insurance, Cyclone insurance, Accident insurance, Health insurance, Life insurance.
What is a tax? A poll tax? An assessor?

A tax is a money payment assessed on the person or on property of the individual for public purposes. A poll tax is a head-tax—a sum required of each male citizen liable to Taxation, without regard to his property. An assessor is the person appointed to prepare the assessment-roll and apportion the taxes.
What is an inventory?
A written list of articles of property, with their values.
What are general average and jetsam?

General average is a method of ascertaining the loss to be sustained by the proprietors of ships, freight and cargo respectively where a part of the property has been sacrificed or damaged for the common safety. Jetsam is the part of the goods thrown overboard.
What are duties or customs, and what is a custom-house?
Duties or customs are taxes levied on imported goods. A custom-house is an office established by the government for the transaction of business relating to duties.
What is smuggling?
Carrying on foreign commerce secretly, without paying the duties imposed by law.
How many kinds of duties are there?

Define them. There are two kinds of duties: ad valorem and specific. An ad valorem duty is the sum computed on the cost of the goods in the country from which they were imported. A specific duty is a sum computed on the weight or measure of the goods, without regard to their cost.
What is interest, and what is usury?
A sum paid for the use of money. Usury is interest above the legal rate.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is a custom tariff?
A list of the rates of duties to be collected.
What is the difference between simple and compound interest?
Simple interest is the interest on the principal only; compound interest is the interest on the principal and the previous interest added.
The time, rate per cent, and interest being given, how do we find the principal?

Divide the given interest by the interest on $1 for the given time at the given rate. Example: What is the face of a note that in 1 yr. 6 mo. 15 da. at 6% would yield $12.95 interest? Solution: One dollar at 6% for 1 yr. 6 mo. 15 da. would yield 9¼ cents, and to yield $12.95 it would take as many dollars as 9¼ cents is contained in $12.95, or $140, Ans.
The time, rate per cent, and amount being given, how do we find the principal?

Divide the given amount by the amount of $1 for the given time at the given rate. Example: A certain note in 2 yr. 3 mo. and 10 da. at 8% amounted (principal and interest) to $127.68. What was the face of the note? Solution: One dollar at 8% for 2 yr. 3 mo. and 10 da. would yield 18.2⅔ cents, and the amount of the one dollar would be $1.182⅔, and it would take as many dollars to amount to $127.68 as $1.182⅔ is contained in $127.68, or $108, face of note, Ans.
The principal, time and interest being given, how do we find the rate per cent.?

Divide the given interest by the interest on the principal at 1 per cent, for the time. Example: A note of $246 in 2 yr. 6 mo. yielded $27.67¾ interest. What was the rate per cent.? Solution: At one per cent, the $246 for 2 yr. 6 mo. would yield $6.15; and for the note to yield $27.675 it would take as many times one per cent. as $6.15 is contained in $27,675, or 4½%, Ans.
The principal, interest, and rate per cent. being given, how do we find the time?
Divide the given interest by the interest on the principal for one year at the rate per cent.
What is a partial payment? An indorsement?
Partial payment of a note, bond, or other obligation is the payment of a certain amount of the money due on the obligation. An indorsement is anything written on the back.
What is discount? What is the present worth of a debt, and how do we find it?

Discount is an allowance made for the payment of a debt before it is due. The present worth of a debt is such a sum as, being put at legal interest, would amount to the debt when due. It is found by dividing the given debt by the amount of $1 at interest for the given time and rate.
What is a bank? What is bank discount? What are days of grace?

A bank is a corporation chartered by law for the purpose of receiving and loaning money, and perhaps also of issuing a paper circulation. Bank discount is an allowance made to a bank for the payment of a debt before it is due. Days of grace are three days allowed a debtor to pay his obligations after they become due. This practice has been discontinued in many of the States. Example I. A man desiring some ready money made out and signed a note for $275, due in 6 mo. He took it to a bank, and the bank discounted it at 8% and handed him the proceeds in cash. How much money did he get? Solution: 8% on $275 for 6 mo. is $11, which the bank demanded; and they gave the man $275 — $11, or $264, Ans. (At the end of the 6 mo. the man would have to return to the bank and pay the bank $275 cash, and take up his note.) Example 2. A party took a note to a bank and had it discounted at 6% for 3 mo. The banker handed the party in cash $65.01. How large was the note? Solution: One dollar discounted at 6% for 3 mo. would yield 98½c.; and to yield $65.01 it would take as great a note as 98½c. is contained in $65.01, or $66, the size of the note, Ans.
What is a promissory note? What is a bank-note? What is a negotiable note?

A promissory note is a written agreement to pay a certain sum either on demand or at a specified time. Bank-notes are paper currency issued by banks as money. A negotiable note is one which may be bought and sold, or negotiated, and is made payable to the bearer or to the order of the payee.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Name and define the kinds of notes.
They are classified as time notes, negotiable notes, joint notes, bank-notes, drafts, stock, and bonds.
What is a notary public? What is a protest?

An officer authorized by law to attest the validity of documents or undertakings of any kind. A protest is a formal declaration in writing, made by a notary public, at the request of the holder of a note, notifying the makers and the indorsers of its non-payment.
What is a draft? A check?

A draft is a note of exchange, issued by one party, calling upon a second party to pay over to a third party so much money, and charge therewith the account of the third party. A check is also a note of exchange, but made payable at some bank where the person issuing it has de-posits or credit.
What is a bond? What is a coupon?

Bonds are interest-bearing notes issued by nations, states, cities, railroad companies, and other corporations as a means of borrowing money. The coupons are the due-bills for the interest, which, as they become due, are cut off and presented for payment.
What do the terms "five-twenties" and "ten-forties" mean?

They are applied to government bonds and indicate the time when due. The five-twenties are due twenty years after issue, although the government can pay them any time after five if they choose to do so. The same rule of interpretation applies to the ten-forties. These war-bonds are now out of date.
What is exchange, and how many kinds are there?
Exchange is a method of remitting money from one per-son to another, or making payments by written orders. There are two kinds, domestic and foreign.
Define these.
Domestic or inland exchange is the exchange between different places in the same country. Foreign exchange is the remittance made between different countries.
What is a bill of exchange?
It is a written request or order upon one person to pay a certain sum to another person, or to his order, at a speci-fied time. A draft or check is one kind of bill of exchange.
How many parties are there to a transaction in ex-change?
There are always three, and usually four.
What is a set of exchange? What is a course of exchange?

It consists of three copies of the same bill sent by different conveyances to provide against a loss; when one has been paid the others are void. A course of exchange is the current price paid in one place for bills of exchange on another place. Example 1. A drover went to a bank in New York with $2450, and desired a draft to mail to his son in Kansas City. How large a draft could he get if the bank charged ⅓ of one per cent. for writing up and giving the draft to him? Solution: A dollar draft would cost the drover $1 plus the ⅓c. that the bank charged. Hence a dollar draft cost $1.00⅓, and he could get as large a draft for his money as $1.00⅓ is contained in the $2450, or $2437.81, Ans. Example 2. A lady went to a bank in Pittsburg one day and purchased a draft to send to her daughter in Atlanta. The bank charged her 1¼ per cent. exchange. She paid for the draft but did not have money enough to pay the exchange. So the bank waited until the following Saturday, when she paid them $3.75 in full. How large a draft did the lady send to her daughter the day previous? Solution: If the bank charged .01¼c. for writing up a draft for one dollar, then if the lady paid exchange to the amount of $3.75 her draft must have been as large as .01¼c. is contained in $3.75, or $300, Ans.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is equation of payments?
The process of finding the mean time of the payment of several sums, due at different times, without interest.
What is equated time?
The date at which several debts may be paid at one time.
What is the focal date?
The date by which all others are compared in averaging an account.
What is partnership?
A relation established by two or more persons in trade. The individuals thus associated are called partners.
What is the difference between simple and compound partnership?

A simple partnership is one in which each of the partners has his capital invested for the same length of time. A compound partnership is that in which the capital of the partners is employed for different periods of time.
What is an indorsement?
The indorsement of a bill of exchange is the writing upon its back by which the payee relinquishes his title, and transfers the payment to another.
What is an acceptance?
The acceptance of a bill is the promise which the drawee makes to pay it at maturity.
How is this obligation acknowledged?
By writing the word "accepted," with his signature, across the face of the bill.
What is a bankrupt?
A bankrupt is a person who fails in business and has not property enough to pay his bills.
What is the difference between a bankrupt and an insolvent?
"Bankrupt" is strictly a term applicable only to a trader, while the term "insolvent" applies to any person who is unable to pay his debts.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Define bankruptcy.
It is the condition of the business of a bankrupt.
What is analysis?
It is the process of arriving at a required result by following a course of reasoning, and not by formal rules.
What is ratio?
Define each kind. Ratio is the comparison of two numbers with each other. Arithmetical ratio is the difference between two numbers. Geometrical ratio is one divided by the other.
What are the terms of a ratio?
The two numbers compared.
What are the antecedent and consequent?
The antecedent is the first term of a ratio; the consequent is the second term.
What are direct and inverse ratio?
Direct ratio is dividing the consequent by the antecedent. Inverse ratio is dividing the antecedent by the consequent.
In how many ways may the ratio of two numbers be expressed?

In two ways: By placing a colon between the terms, or by the form of a fraction, taking the antecedent for the numerator and the consequent for the denominator. Example: What is the ratio between 6 and 10? 6 : 10 or 6/10, Ans.
What is the difference between a simple ratio and a compound ratio?

A simple ratio is the ratio of two numbers. A compound ratio is the product of two or more simple ratios. Example 1. Is the ratio the same between 7 and 21 as between 15 and 45? Solution: 7 : 21 :: 15 : 45 7 15 — = — 21 45 1 1 — = — Ans., Yes. 3 3 Example 2. What number has the same ratio to 65 that 5 has to 15? Solution: 5 : 15 :: what : 65 5 what — = — 15 65 1 21⅔ — = —, Ans. 3 65
What is proportion?
An equality of ratios.
Which are the extremes and the means of a stated proportion?
The extremes are the first and fourth terms of a stated proportion. The means are the other two.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is a continued proportion?
A proportion with more than two equal ratios; as, 3:5::6:10::9:15.
How many antecedents are there in a proportion, and how many consequents?
As each ratio has an antecedent and consequent, the first and third terms of a proportion are the antecedents, and the second and fourth the consequents.
How are ratio and proportion distinguished?
Ratio is the relation between two numbers, while proportion is the relation between two ratios?
What is a simple proportion?

An equality between two simple ratios. Example: In a sack of 162 lb. of rough rice the ratio of hulls to clean rice is as 1 to 4. How many pounds of clean rice in the sack? Solution: 1 : 5 :: what : 162 1/5 = what/162 1/5 = 32⅖/162 hulls hulls 1 : 5 :: 32⅖ : 162 162 — 32⅖ = 129⅗, Ans.
What is a direct proportion?
One in which each term increases or diminishes, as the one on which it depends increases or diminishes.
What is an inverse proportion?
One in which each term increases as the term upon which it depends diminishes, or diminishes as it increases.
What is a compound proportion?

A proportion in which either ratio is compound. Example: Two planters expended $2240 on an irrigating plant. One owned 80 A. and the other 120 A. If they paid in the ratio of their acreage, how much did each pay? Solution: 80 : 200 :: what : 2240 80   what --- = ---- 200  2240 2   what --- = ---- 5   2240 2   896 --- = ---- 5   2240 80 : 200 :: 896 : 2240 Hence the 80 A. pays $896. And also 120 : 200 :: what : 2240 120  what ---- = ---- 200  2240 3   what --- = ---- 5   2240 3   1344 --- = ---- 5   2240 120 : 200 :: 1344 : 2240 Hence the 120 A. pays $1344.
Of what does alligation treat?
Of mixing or compounding two or more ingredients of different values.
How many kinds of alligation are there?

Define each. Two: medial and alternate. Medial is the process of finding the average price or quality of several ingredients whose prices or qualities are known. Alternate is the process of finding the proportional quantity to be taken of several ingredients whose prices are known.
Give the analysis of alligation.

Alligation is but a short way of performing an analysis. To show the process we will unite 3, 4, 7, 10, to form a value worth 6. It will be noticed that the difference between 10 and 6 is a loss of 4, and to make a loss of 1 will require ¼, also there will be a gain of ⅓ in the opposite connection. The proportion, then, if the two terms 3 and 10, expressed in fractions, is ⅓ and ¼, which, reduced to a common denominator, is ⁴⁄₁₂ and ³⁄₁₂, or expressed in whole numbers, 4 and 3, which, in alligation, is understood as an exchange of difference without the use of fractions and their reduction to whole numbers.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is an annuity?
A sum of money payable at certain intervals of time, usually one year.
Name and define the kinds of annuity.

A certain annuity, which continues for a fixed period of time; a perpetual annuity, continuing forever; a contingent annuity, depending upon certain occurrences, as on the death of a person; an annuity in reversion, one that begins at a specified future time or event; and an annuity in arrears, one the payments of which have been allowed to accumulate instead of being paid. Examples: A man by will left to his son a stipend of $10 per month until the son reached the age of 30 years. This would be a certain annuity. Where a person settles upon another a sum of money, say $50 per year to continue till the said person contracts marriage, this would be a contingent annuity. If a person contracts to pay to another person a sum of money, say $5 per month not to commence till the person reaches the age of 50 years, and then continue the rest of his life, this would be an annuity in reversion. Where a person grants to another a sum of money, say $8 per month to commence at birth and none of the money to be paid over till the beneficiary reaches the age of 21 years, this would be an annuity in arrears.
What is involution?
The process of raising a number to a given power.
What is a surd?
The indicated root of an imperfect power.
What is the power of a number?
A power of a number is the result obtained by using it a certain number of times as a factor.
What is a perfect power? An imperfect power?
A perfect power is a number whose root can be found. An imperfect power is one whose root cannot be exactly found.
What is the exponent of a power?
The exponent of a power is a number placed at the right of the root and just above it, to show the number of times the root is to be used, as 3⁴ = 3 × 3 × 3 × 3.
Define evolution.
Evolution is the process of extracting the root of any number considered a power. It is the opposite of involution.
What is the root of a number?
The root of a number is one of the equal factors of that number.
How are the roots of numbers indicated?
By the sign called a radical √. The figure on the sign indicates the root. Examples: √64 = 8 ³√27 = 3 ⁴√16 = 2 ⁵√243 = 3

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the square root of a number?
One of two equal factors that produce that number.
What is the cube root of a number?
One of three equal factors that produce that number.
What is an arithmetical progression?
A series of numbers increased or diminished by a common difference. Examples: Common difference, 3. Then 4, 7, 10, 13, 16, etc., would be an arithmetical progression.
What is a geometrical progression?
A series of numbers increased or diminished by a constant multiplier. Common multiplier, 4. Then 2, 8, 32, 128, 512, etc., would be a geometrical progression.
What five things are to be considered in an arithmetical progression?
The first term, the last term, the number of terms, the common difference and the sum of the series. Three of these being given, the other two can be found.
What things are to be considered in a geometrical progression?
They are the first term, the last term, the number of terms, the constant multiplier, and the sum of the series. Three of these being given, the other two can be found.
What is a duodecimal?
A denominate number in which twelve units of any denomination make a unit of the next higher denomination.
What is mensuration?
The art of measuring magnitudes.
What is magnitude?
Anything which has length, breadth and thickness.
What is the measuring unit of surfaces?
It is customary to use the square as the measuring unit, as a square inch, foot, rod, etc.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How do you find the area of a square?
By multiplying the length by the breadth.
How do you find the area of (a) a rhombus, (b) of a trapezium, (c) of a triangle?
(a) By multiplying the length by the altitude; (b) by multiplying half the sum of the parallel sides by the altitude; (c) by multiplying the base by half the altitude.
How do you find the area of a triangle when the three sides are given?

From half the sum of the three sides subtract each side respectively; then multiply together half the sum and the three remainders, and extract the square root of the product. Example: The three sides of a triangle are 5 rods, 9 rods and 12 rods: to find the area. Solution: (5 + 9 + 12) ÷ 2 = 13 13 - 5 = 8 13 - 9 = 4 13 - 12 = 1 8 X 4 X 1 X 13 = 416 √416 = 20.+, Ans.
How do you find the circumference of a circle when the diameter is given?
To find the circumference multiply the diameter by 3.14159; to find the diameter, divide the circumference by the same number.
How do you find the area of a circle?
By multiplying half the circumference by half the diameter; or, by multiplying the circumference by a fourth of the diameter.
How do you find the side of the greatest square that can be inscribed in a circle of a given diameter?

Divide the square of the given diameter by 2, and extract the square root of the quotient. Example: What is the side of the greatest square that can be inscribed within a circle whose diameter is 16 ft.? 16² = 256 ÷ 2 = 128 √128 = 11.3+, Ans.
How do you find a mean proportional between two numbers?
By multiplying the given numbers together, and extracting the square root of the product.
How do you find the solidity of bodies whose sides are perpendicular to each other?
By multiplying the length, breadth and thickness.
How do you find the solidity of a prism?
By multiplying the area of the base by the height.
How do you find the lateral surface of a right prism?
By multiplying the length by the perimeter of the base.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How do you find the solidity of a pyramid or cone?
By multiplying the area of the base by ⅓ of the altitude.
How do you find the lateral or convex surface of a regular pyramid or cone?
By multiplying the perimeter of the base by ½ of the slant height.
How do you find the solidity of a cylinder?
By multiplying the area of the base by the height or length.
How do you find the convex surface of a cylinder?
By multiplying the circumference of the base by the height.
How do you find the surface of a sphere or globe?
By multiplying the circumference by the diameter.
How do you find the solidity of a sphere or globe?
By multiplying the surface by ⅓ of the diameter.
How do you find the side of a square equal in area to any given surface?
By extracting the square root of the given surface.
How do we measure lumber?
Multiply the length in feet by the product of the breadth and thickness, both in inches, and divide the result by 12.
How do we find the solid contents of a stick of round timber?
By multiplying the length by ¼ the mean circumference.
What is gauging?
The process of finding the contents or capacity of casks, barrels, etc.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How do we find the contents of casks?
Multiply the square of the mean diameter by the length, and this product by .0034 to obtain the wine gallons. Using the decimal .0028 will give us the beer gallons.
Define the metric system of measurement.
The metric system is a decimal system of weights and measures, having the metre for the base or unit.
What is the measure of the metre in inches?
It measures 39.3708 inches.
From what was the metre obtained?

It is one ten-millionth part of a quadrant of the earth's meridian. Illustration: A meridian passing around the earth and through each pole is about 24,750 miles long, and ¼ of it would be 6187.5 miles. Reduced to feet by multiplying by 5,280, this product divided by 10,000,000 would give 39.37 inches, the length of a metre.
One gentleman meeting another, and inquiring the time past 12 o'clock, received for an answer: ⅓ of the time from now to midnight; what o'clock in the afternoon was it?
Where shall a pole 60 feet high be broken, that the top may rest on the ground 20 feet from the stump?
Suppose a man owes $1,000. What sum shall he pay yearly so as to cancel the debt, principal and interest, at the end of three years, reckoning it at 6 per cent. simple interest?

A gentleman has a garden 400 feet long and 300 feet wide which he would raise 9 inches higher by means of the earth to be dug out of a ditch that goes around it. To what depth must the ditch be dug, supposing its breadth to be everywhere 6 feet?
10 1/78. 5. Divide $1,000 among A, B and C, so that A may have $156 more than B, and B $62 less than C. Ans. A, $416 2/3; B, $260 2/3; C, $322 2/3.
If 21 cows eat up 8 acres of grass in 6 weeks, and 18 cows eat up the same in 9 weeks, how many cows will it maintain for 18 weeks, if the grass grow uniformly during that time?

15 cows. 7. Required the thickness of the lead of a pipe 1 1/4 inch bore, which weighs 16 lbs., a yard in length, a cubic foot of lead weighing 11,325 oz. avoirdupois. Ans. 2.329+ in. 8. If a cubic foot of brass were drawn into wire 1/10 of an inch in diameter, required the length of the wire, supposing no loss in the metal. Ans. 55 m., 4 fur., 104 yds., 2 ft., 4 in.+. 9. Divide $25 between 2 boys in such a proportion that, after it is divided, A shall have 1/2 more than B. Ans. A, $15; B, $10.

A makes B a present of $100, on condition that he shall expend it in cows, sheep, and geese; cows at $10 each, sheep at $1, and geese at 16 2/3 cents, so as to have just 100 in the whole. How many must he purchase of each?
5 cows, 41 sheep, and 54 geese.
