Name and give the length of the longest four rivers in the world?
Mississippi, with the Missouri, 4,200; Nile, 4,000; Amazon, 3,750; Yenisei, 3,400.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Name and give the length of the longest four rivers of North America?
Mississippi proper, 2,800; Missouri, to the Mississippi, 2,900; Mackenzie, 2,300; Arkansas, 2,000; Saskatchawan, 1,900.
What would be the length of the St. Lawrence through the chain of lakes to the head of the St. Louis River?
Name and give the length of the longest four rivers of South America?
Amazon; La Plata, 2,300; Orinoco, 1,550; San Francisco, 1,550.
Of Europe?
Volga, 2,000; Danube, 1,600; Don, 1,000; Dnieper, 1,000.
Of Asia?
Yenisei; Yang-tse-Kiang, 3,320; Obi, 3,000; Lena, 2,700.
Of Africa?
Nile; Niger, 3,000; Zambesi, 1,800; Senegal, 1,200.
What is noticeable of the Amazon River?

It is a broad, deep river, and navigable for large vessels about 2,400 miles, and for steamboats to the foot of the Andes. The tide flows up 600 miles. Its waters teem with alligators, and its banks are lined with almost impenetrable forests, filled with huge serpents, ferocious beasts, troops of monkeys, swarms of insects, and flocks of gaudily-colored birds.
Of the Mississippi?

It rises in Lake Itasca, and is navigable to the Falls of St. Anthony, which are very heavy rapids. The upper part of the river, in many places, has very high and rugged banks, while in Louisiana levees, or banks, are built to prevent inundations. Its mouth is not favorable for the navigation of large vessels. Instead of being broad and clear it has many outlets, and sand-bars are continually forming.
Of the Missouri?
This river is very rapid. It is much larger than the Mississippi above, and is navigable to the Great Falls above Ft. Benton, where there are some of the grandest cataracts in the world.
Of the Nile?

Its source, until recently, was not definitely known, but through the explorations of Livingston and Stanley it has been ascertained, together with the mysterious overflow through Egypt. At Khartoom, in Soudan, the river divides and these divisions are called the Blue Nile and the White Nile. The Blue rises in Abyssinia. and the White, the real source of the Nile, rises in Lake Victoria Nyanza. The overflow of the Lower Nile is from June to December, and is caused by the tropical rains in Abyssinia and those of the interior. The river gradually rises for three months and as slowly subsides, leaving a narrow river for the remainder of the year. By this overflow the lands from ten to twenty miles become one of the most fertile valleys in the world. Above its mouth for nearly 1,500 miles there are no rivers or creeks which flow into it.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Describe the Rio de la Plata?

It is a great estuary 200 miles long, and formed by the union of the Parana and Uruguay. For 1,000 miles on the Parana there is a continuous and safe navigation for vessels of three hundred tons burden.
Describe Egypt?

This country is nearly as large as Texas; about three-fourths of its area consists of sandy deserts. The settled portions are principally in the valley of the Nile, which is from 10 to 20 miles in width. Egypt is noted for its wonderful ruins. The pyramids, temples, sphynxes, statues, and caves hewn in the rocks, are traces of its former civilization.
By what two great commercial routes can we travel around the world, starting from New York?

Cross the Atlantic, enter the Mediterranean Sea; pass through the Suez canal; down the Red Sea into the Indian Ocean; pass near the southern points of Asia to Canton; thence across the Pacific to San Francisco; then by rail across the continent to New York. 2d. Sail southwest on the California route until nearly opposite Rio Janeiro; cross the Atlantic; pass Cape of Good Hope; through the Indian Ocean to the East Indies; then up the China Sea to Canton; cross the Pacific to San Francisco; thence to Panama and across the Isthmus; thence north through the West Indies, to New York.
What country in the world has the longest and most numerous lines of railroads?
The United States.
Between what parallels does the United States lie?
Between the 26th and 49th. The distance from the southern point of Texas to the British possessions is about 1,600 miles.
What is the longitude of San Francisco, and about how far is it from New York City?
It is about 45° west from Washington, and about 3,000 miles from New York.
What State is the geographical center of the United States?
Kansas.
What country produces the most cotton? Sugar? Coffee? Rice? Tea?
Cotton—United States. Sugar—West Indies. Coffee—Brazil. Rice and Tea—China.
What countries produce largely of the same products?
Cotton—South America and Egypt. Sugar—United States and South America. Coffee—Java Islands, West Indies, and United States. Rice—United States and Egypt.
What country produces the most spices?
The Spice Islands are celebrated for the production of cloves, nutmegs, and other spices. Java produces more pepper than all other countries combined.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are cloves?
The unexpanded flower dried. The clove tree grows to the height of from twenty to thirty feet.
What are nutmegs? Mace?
The fruit of the nutmeg tree. This tree is about the size of the common pear.
What is a mountain system?
Several ranges near each other and connected.
Name the mountain systems of America?
The Rocky, Californian, Alleghanian, Andean, Parime, and Brazilian.
What is the length of the longest day at the equator? At the Tropic of Cancer? Arctic Circle? North Pole?

At the equator the length of the days is equal, being twelve hours each; at the tropics the longest day is about thirteen and one-half hours; at the Polar Circles twenty-four hours; at the Poles six months.
Why does so little rain fall on that part of South America west of the Andes Mountain range?
Rains from the Pacific are of rare occurrence, and the height of the Andes range prevents the rain-clouds from the east.
What large city in Europe is noted for the manufacture of cotton cloth? For silks? Linen? Cutlery? Laces?
Manchester. Lyons. Belfast. Sheffield. Brussels.
What are the leading pursuits of the people of the New England States?
Agriculture, manufactures, lumbering, and commerce.
What are the products of the New England States?
Grains, butter, cheese, wool, maple sugar, and live stock.
Where are the manufactures carried on to the greatest extent in these States? Name some of the most famous cities for the manufacture of cotton.
On the Merrimac and Pawtucket Rivers. Lowell, Lawrence, Fall River, Pawtucket and Woonsocket.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is remarkable about the Merrimac River?
It is said to be more extensively used for manufacturing purposes than any other river in the world.
What is said of the climate? Soil? Agricultural products? Commerce?

The winters are long and severe and the summers short but hot. The soil is generally stony and not very fertile, except in the valleys. The agricultural products are not sufficient to supply the wants of the population. The many excellent harbors, the comparative nearness to Europe, and the abundance of ship timber have contributed to establish an extensive foreign commerce.
How do the New England States compare in size with California?
They are about two-fifths that of California.
What noted colleges are located at Cambridge and New Haven?
Harvard at Cambridge and Yale at New Haven.
What important industries are carried on at Lynn? New Bedford? Springfield? Worcester? Portland?

Lynn is famous for its manufacture of boots and shoes. New Bedford for its whale fisheries. Springfield, the greatest arsenal in the United States. Worcester, a great railroad and manufacturing center. Portland, for the termination of the Grand Trunk Railway of Canada.
Where is Mount Washington? Its height?
It is the highest peak of the White Mountains in New Hampshire, and is 6,288 feet high.
Where is marble obtained?
From the extensive quarries in Vermont and Italy. It is also found in the States of Maine, Massachusetts, Rhode Island and Tennessee.
What is granite, and where found?
It is very hard rock, and much used for millstones. It is found in the Eastern States.
What State is called the "Granite State"? The "Pine-tree State"? The "Green Mountain State"? The "Bay State"?
New Hampshire, Maine, Vermont, Massachusetts.

For what are Lexington and Concord famous? (a) Where are the following mountains: White, Blue, Hoosick, Mt. Katahdin, Mt. Holyoke, Bunker Hill. (b) What are Nantucket and Martha's Vineyard, and to what State do they belong? (c) Where are Rhode and Mt. Desert Islands, and to what States do they belong? (d) Describe Lakes Memphremagog, Moosehead, Chesuncook, Grand, Winnipiseogee, Sebago, and Umbagog. (e) Describe the bay of Passamaquodda, Massachusetts, Cape Cod, Penobscot, Casco, Narragansett, and Long Island Sound.
For the opening engagements of the war of the Revolution, April 19th, 1775.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How many square miles in the area of Rhode Island, and how many States of the same size could be formed out of the State of Texas?
1,300 square miles. 183.

How does Maine compare in size to the other five New England States? 204. What is particularly noticeable about the northern boundary of Connecticut? (a) Locate Burlington, Belfast, Dover, Salem, Bath, Bennington, Bangor, Eastport, and Lowell. (b) Describe the following: Housatonic, Kennebec, Merrimac, Pawtucket, Connecticut, Thames, St. Croix, Taunton, Onion, White, and Androscoggin.
It is nearly as large.
What is the size of California ?
About 189,000 square miles.
What is the surface of this State ?
A large portion of it is traversed by mountain ranges. The great agricultural valley lies between the Sierra Nevada and coast range, and extends 500 miles.
What is the name of this valley, and from what did it derive its name ?

It is the Sacramento and San Joaquin valley, and derives its name from the Sacramento River, which rises in the northern part of the State, and the San Joaquin, which rises in the southern part. These rivers flow towards each other and empty into a branch of San Francisco Bay.
What is the Golden Gate ?
A narrow strait about four miles long, which is the entrance from the Pacific Ocean to San Francisco Bay.
What is the climate of the Pacific Coast ?

It is mild and equable in temperature, but has great extremes of moisture. The seasons are termed the wet and the dry. At Puget Sound there is a greater rainfall than any other part of the United States, except Alaska.
What natural curiosities do we find in California?

The Big Trees and the Yosemite Valley are two of the chief wonders of nature. The Big Trees are gigantic evergreens, some of which are more than 100 feet around, 400 feet high, and 3,000 years old. The Yosemite Valley is a remarkable chasm ten miles long and three in width, with perpendicular walls of granite from 3,000 to 5,000 feet high, over which the Merced River, in three leaps, falls more than 2,000 feet.
What are the productions of this section ?
Wheat, wool, lumber, salmon fisheries, gold, wine, and a great variety of fruits.
What can be said of the fisheries of the Columbia River?
They are an immense industry.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What can be said of the valley of Puget Sound ? Its climate ? Its latitude ?
It is very fertile, its climate mild, which is due to the warm ocean current which flows by its shores. The same latitude on the Atlantic is almost a cold, barren waste.
Describe Alaska.

It has an area of 577,000 square miles, or more than ten times that of Illinois. The climate is cold, and with the exception of the southern point, it is unfit for civilization. In the northern part the coast is low and marshy, while in the south it is mountainous. The Yukon and Pelly Rivers are remarkable for their size and length.
What is its form of government?
It is organized into a territory, but has no representation in Congress.
Is this possession valuable to the United States ?

It is already of considerable value, and may become of great interest, as the salmon fisheries are inexhaustible and mining is attracting much attention. The seal fisheries yield an annual revenue to the government of over $100,000.
How are the seal fisheries conducted ?
A species of license is granted a company, who are allowed to kill so many seals each year and pay certain amounts to the government for this exclusive privilege.
Which is the principal settlement, and where situated ?
Sitka, on the Island of Sitka. The rain-fall at this point is about seven and a half feet a year.
When was gold discovered in California ?
It was first announced at the close of the year 1848.
What is the climate?
The climate is noted for its periodical changes. There are two seasons, the wet and the dry. In the latitude of San Francisco the wet season lasts from the middle of November to the middle of May.
What is the difference in latitude between San Francisco and Richmond ?
Richmond is about twenty-five miles south.
How far north of Richmond is New York City ?
About 230 miles.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What part of South America is in the same latitude south of the equator, that Central United States is north of it ?
The northern part of Patagonia.
How does Brazil compare in size with the United States (not including Alaska)?
It is a little larger than the United States, containing about 3,230,000 square miles.
What city in the West Indies is in the same latitude north of the equator that Rio Janeiro is south of it?
Havana.
Describe South America.

It is a vast peninsula, occupying the southern half of the Western Continent. Its length from north to south is about 4,000 miles, and its greatest breadth is about 3,000. Three great mountain systems diversify the surface, viz., the Andean, which stretches along the western coast; the Parime, which consists of several parallel ranges extending from east to west, between the Orinoco and Amazon Rivers; and the Brazilian system, which consists of two ranges running parallel to the coast of Brazil, with several diverging chains. Between these mountain systems extends a vast plain. This plain may be divided into four parts—the region of the llanos, the selvas, the pampas, and wastes of Patagonia.
What are the llanos?

They are level grazing tracts, which occupy most of the region of the basin of the Orinoco. During the dry season they are parched and desolate, but with the return of rain rich pastures are abundant.
What are the selvas?
They are the forest plains of the Amazon, occupying the lower part of the basin, extending as far as the periodical inundation.
What are the pampas?
These tracts are in the Argentine Republic, and are covered with a heavy growth of grass, which affords sus-tenance to great herds of cattle and horses.
What are the wastes of Patagonia?
Sterile tracts covered with sand and gravel.
What is a plateau, or table-land?
An extensive level tract considerably elevated above the level of the sea.
How may the plateaus of South America be divided?
The Great Plateau of the Andes ; the elevated plains of Quito, Bogota, and Popayan ; and the tableland of Brazil.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Describe the plateau of the Andes.
It is an extensive tract of lofty tableland, stretch-ing along the tops of the Andes between parallels 3° and 15° south latitude, with an elevation of nearly 13,000 feet.
Describe Quito.

It is situated under the equator, on the side of a volcanic mountain, 9,500 feet above the sea. The climate is that of continued spring. Earthquakes are frequent. There are in sight eleven summits of the Andes covered with perpetual snow.
What are Chimborazo and Cotopaxi?

Volcanoes of Ecuador. Chimborazo is a peak of the Andes range, 21,440 feet high. Cotopaxi, 18,900 feet high, is the most tremendous volcano in the world. The flame sometimes rises 3,000 feet above the top.
What is the Peruvian Balsa?

A light raft, which is formed of inflated skins cov-ered with a light platform. These are used for unloading vessels through the breakers, which continually dash upon the shores and prevent the approach of any ordinary boat.
Describe the Cassiquiare River.

It is a connecting river between the Orinoco and Rio Negro Rivers. This tract of country is so level that a rise or fall of the Orinoco governs the course of that river, which flows during a portion of the year into the Amazon, and at other times in an opposite direction.
How far north does South America extend?
Twelve and a half degrees north of the equator.
Into what divisions can the countries of South America be divided?
Into three divisions : Portuguese South America, Spanish South America, and the Guianas.
What does Portuguese South America comprise?
The Empire of Brazil.
What does the Guianas comprise?
British, French and Dutch colonies.
What does Spanish South America comprise?
Nine independent republics, viz.: Venezuela, Co-lumbia, Ecuador, Peru, Bolivia, Chili, Argentine Confeder-ation, Uruguay and Paraguay.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Why are these republics called the Spanish division?
They were once colonies of Spain.
Describe Venezuela. The United States of Columbia. Ecuador. Peru. Bolivia. Chili.

Venezuela is the most northern country of South America, is tropical in climate, and exports coffee, cocoa and tropical fruits. United States of Columbia occupies the northwest por-tion, embracing the Isthmus of Panama, and is the most important of the five northern republics. By treaty it is under the protection of the United States. Ecuador lies south of Columbia, and its exports are cocoa, coffee, Indian-rubber and Peruvian bark. Peru lies south of Ecuador, and has more miles of railroad than any other country of South America. Its chief exports are guano and saltpeter. Silver mining is an important industry. Bolivia has but little commerce; its people are not so energetic and prosperous. The exports are Peruvian bark, saltpeter and silver ore. Chile occupies a narrow strip of sea-coast extending from Bolivia to Cape Horn. It is the most enterprising country of South America, and a large proportion of its inhabitants are Europeans. Its chief exports are copper-ore, silver and wheat. The climate is semi-tropical in the northern part and extends into the cold, barren regions on the south.
What is the Argentine Confederation? Describe Uruguay. Paraguay.

This Confederation stands next to Chile in enterprise. Its climate is semi-tropical, and its exports are hides, wool and flax. The country is almost an extensive plain with the finest grazing in the world. Large herds of cattle, horses and sheep are successfully handled here. Two-thirds of the hides are shipped to the United States. Education is rapidly advancing. The location of this country is to the southern hemisphere what the United States is to the northern. Uruguay and Paraguay formerly belonged to the Argentine Confederation, but are each now independent republics. Their exports and climate are similar to the Argentine Confederation. Paraguay is the weakest of the nine republics, and is the only country of South America having no sea-coast.
What are the inhabitants of South America? How divided in regard to numbers?

It contains about 27,000,000 inhabitants, of which one-third belong to the Caucasian race, one-third Indians, and the remainder consists of Negroes and persons of mixed blood called Mestizoes and Mulattoes.
What is the state of commerce? Civilization?

As South America is largely in the torrid zone the climate does not inspire activity, and commerce naturally drags, but as civilization advances the general prosperity increases. The country is fast feeling the influence of the enlightenment of the United States and Europe.
What are the natural resources? The industries?

There are no large gulfs or bays to indent the shores, and no great lakes for an inland commerce. The natural facilities for commerce and manufacturing are greatly inferior to those of North America. The great central plain supports millions of cattle, horses and sheep. The industries are mostly of the productions of tropical climates. Gold, silver, copper and diamonds are found and extensively mined.
Which is the most important country for the commerce of the United States?
Brazil.
Which is the most progressive of the republics?
Chili.
Describe the railroads of South America.
They are short. Chili has the best system and Brazil has about 2,000 miles.
What part of South America is in the same latitude as Cape Colony?
Uruguay.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What city in Australia is in nearly the same latitude as Buenos Ayres?
Sydney.
What large city in the United States is in nearly the same longitude as Quito?
Buffalo.
What part of the western coast of Africa is in the same latitude as Cape Gallinas?
Near Cape Verd.
Where is Cape Horn ?
It projects from Horn Island, which is southwest from Hermit Island. Cape Horn has usually been given as projecting from the Isle of Hermit.
What is Terra del Fuego?

The most southern part of the inhabited world, and is peopled by a race of miserable savages who live chiefly by fishing. These islands were discovered by Magellan, in 1520, and so named by him on account of the number of fires he saw along the coast, which he supposed to be eruptions of volcanoes. The meaning of Terra del Fuego is land of fire.
Why was Patagonia so named ?

It was taken from the Spanish word Patagon—a man with large feet. Magellan gave the inhabitants of this country the name of Patagonians on account of the apparent large size of their feet, which, being wrapped in skins, seemed much larger than they really were.
What cape in South America is the most northern, eastern, southern, western?
Cape Gallinas, St. Roque, Horn, Parina.
Where is Lake Reyes, and what river is its outlet ?
It is in Peru, and the Ucayle River is its outlet. This lake is the source of the Amazon.
What is the general character of the lakes of South America ?
With the exception of Lake Titicaca and Lake Maracaibo, they are more like vast morasses than lakes.
Describe Lakes Titicaca and Maracaibo.

Lake Titicaca is a saltish lake, about half as large as Lake Erie, and is situated on the Great Plateau nearly 13,000 feet above the level of the Pacific Ocean. Its waters are inland, having no outlet to the ocean. Lake Maracaibo is, more properly speaking, a bay, being connected by a strait with the Caribbean Sea.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Where are the Lobos and Chincha Islands ? For what are they valuable ?
They lie off the coast of Peru, and are of great value for the immense quantities of guano found upon them.
Which is the smallest division of South America, and what State is about the same size ?
French Guiana, which contains about 3,500 square miles, or about the size of Maine.
How does Brazil compare in size to the whole of South America ? Describe this country.

It contains nearly one-half of the peninsula. It was once a colony of Portugal, and Portuguese is the language of the civilized inhabitants. An ocean telegraph connects this country with Portugal and another with the United States by the way of the West Indies. Brazil furnishes the greater part of the coffee used in the world, more than one-half of which goes to the United States. Cotton, sugar, hides, dye-woods, Indian-rubber and diamonds are also produced in large quantities.
What important enterprise is being pushed on the Isthmus of Panama ?
A ship canal.
What political interest does the United States hold in this enterprise ?
That it shall not become a possession of any foreign government.
What political relations exist between this government and United States of Colombia?
A treaty of protection. The United States guarantees that no other power shall take possession of her lands or government.
Who is Count de Lesseps, and what notable works has he accomplished ?
A great French engineer who conceived the idea of building the Suez Canal, and is now pushing to completion the canal on the Isthmus of Panama.
Describe the Andes Mountains.

They form one of the grandest mountain ranges in the world. They commence in low hills on the Isthmus of Panama, and terminate in the Island of Cape Horn, which is a bleak and naked rock, rising 3,500 feet above the sea. In Patagonia they rise abruptly from the shore, but farther north they are from 60 to 100 miles from the coast. They consist generally of parallel ranges of lofty mountains, with high valleys and tablelands between. This system is from 30 to 400 miles wide.
What does British America comprise? What are its chief divisions ?

It comprises about one-third of the continent of North America, together with many adjacent islands. Its chief divisions are the Dominion of Canada, the Province of Newfoundland, the Bermuda Islands, the Bahamas, Jamaica, and numerous colonies in the West Indies.
What are the divisions of the Dominion of Canada ?
Quebec, Ontario, New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, Prince Edward Island, British Columbia, Manitoba, and the Northeast and Northwest Territories or Districts.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Describe the climate of the Dominion. The surface. The trade.

The climate is severe, except in the southern parts of British Columbia and Ontario. The surface as a whole is considered level. British Columbia and the provinces east of Manitoba are great forest regions. Between the Rocky Mountains and Manitoba are extensive prairies. The trade is principally with Great Britain and the United States.  The exports are fish, coal, grain, and lumber.
How does the Dominion compare in size to the United States ? What is its population ?
It is nearly the same size and its population is 4,375,000.  Its area is 3,500,000.
What island forms a part of the province of Nova Scotia?
Cape Breton.
Describe Newfoundland. For what is it noted ?
The surface is generally rocky and barren. The coast is indented by deep inlets, which form many excellent harbors.  It is noted for its fisheries.
Describe Nova Scotia. Prince Edward Island. New Brunswick. Quebec. Ontario.

Nova Scotia has many good harbors and valuable fisheries. It is favorably located for commerce and exports fish, sheep, coal and gypsum. Prince Edward Island has a fertile soil, and agriculture is the leading occupation. New Brunswick is noted for its timber forests, and exports great amounts of lumber and fish. Quebec has a severe climate, and is noted for its picturesque scenery. Montreal, the largest city of this province, has an extensive commerce and ships large amounts of grain. Ontario has a mild climate, compared with Quebec, and produces a great amount of grain and lumber.
Describe Manitoba. British Columbia.

Manitoba is in the basin of the great wheat growing region of the North and is rapidly increasing in population. The Red River of the North, flowing from the United States, and the Winnipeg Lake with the Saskatchawan River, form 2,000 miles of inland navigation. British Columbia abounds in salmon, timber, coal, gold and other metals. It is soon to be connected with the great Canadian Pacific Railroad.
Describe the Northeast and Northwest Territories, or Districts.
They are divided into Provisional Districts: Athabasca, Alberta, Saskatchawan and Assiniboia. It will eventually be the greatest wheat producing country in the world.
What is the government of the provinces of the Dominion ?

The chief executive officer is the Governor-General, who is appointed by the sovereign of Great Britain. The Legislature consists of two branches. The members of the lower branch are elected by the people, but the members of the other branch are appointed by the Governor-General. Each province has a similar government under a Lieutenant-Governor.
For what is the great Saskatchewan Valley noted? What is the extent of this valley ?
For the production of wheat, and contains over 300,000 square miles.
What is the Bras d'Or?
It is an arm of the sea, which almost divides the Island of Cape Breton.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are the French possessions near these provinces ?
The small islands of St. Pierre and Miquelon. These islands lie south of Newfoundland, and serve as fishing-stations.
What are the banks of Newfoundland ?
They are shallow places in the sea, the largest of which is Grand Bank, and lies to the eastward of the island. This bank is more than 600 miles long and 250 broad.
Where is Anticosta Island ? Is it valuable ?
It is situated at the mouth of the St. Lawrence River. It is a barren island and only occupied by keepers of light-houses upon the coast.
Which of the United States is about the size of New Brunswick ? Prince Edward Island ?
South Carolina. Delaware.
How is Montreal situated ?
On Montreal Island. This island is 32 miles in length, and is situated at the junction of the Ottawa with the St. Lawrence. The Isle of Jesus lies north of it.
For what is the basin of the St. Lawrence remark- able?

For the grandeur and beauty of its natural scenery. Niagara Falls, the Thousand Isles, and Rapids of St. Lawrence, the falls near Quebec, and the grand scenery of the Saguenay River, are the admiration of thousands of tourists.
For what is the Bay of Fundy noted?
For having the highest tides in the world, which rise to the height of seventy feet.
What are tides? Neap tides? Spring tides?

Tides are elevations and depressions of the ocean at regular intervals, occurring twice in twenty-four hours. The neap tides are the least ebb and flow of the water, and are at the close of the first quarter and first of the last quarter of the moon. Spring tides are the greatest ebb and flow of the water, which occur at the time of the new and the full moon.
What are the chief causes of tides ?

The attraction of the moon and sun. Probably not one teacher in a thousand can explain to the satisfaction of his scholars this attraction. The fact that the water rises on opposite sides of the earth at the same time is the difficult part of the question. How the attraction on one side produces a similar attraction on the other, we leave for your study, as we frankly confess we dare not attempt its explanation in this review.
What are the three distinct movements of the ocean?
Waves, tides and currents.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are waves ?
The action of the winds on the surface of a body of water. On the ocean, the height of waves produced by storms averages from 10 to 20 feet.
What are ocean currents, and how are they caused ? 295. How are ocean currents divided ?
They are like vast rivers, transporting its waters from one part to another, and are caused by the heat of the sun, rotation of the earth, the saltness of the sea, by winds, tides, and melting ice.
What is a counter current?
A stream which runs by the side of, or beneath another current, and in an opposite direction.
Why are the tides of Fundy Bay so much greater than at other ports ?
The position of the bay is such, that the Atlantic tide is forced into it, and the waters are raised by this pressure above the adjoining sea.
What are the chief productions of the Canadian provinces?
Grain, hemp, flax, potatoes, lumber, fish, coal, grindstones, and gypsum.
To what province does Labrador belong ?
Newfoundland.
What are the obstructions to navigation from the Gulf of St. Lawrence to Lake Superior?
The rapids of the St. Lawrence, Niagara Falls, and falls in the River St. Mary.
How are these obstructions avoided ?

In ascending the river, the rapids are avoided by canals on the left bank, but the downward passage, though perilous, is made by the river itself. The Welland Canal connects Lakes Ontario and Erie, thereby avoiding the Falls of Niagara. The falls of the St. Mary River are passed by a ship canal.
How is Nova Scotia joined to New Brunswick ?
By an isthmus, fourteen miles in width.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What islands are in the Niagara River ?
Grand, Navy, and Goat.
What difficulties of a political nature have lately arisen between Canada and the United States ?
The Canadians claim that American fishermen have gone into British waters. Several seizures were made, and the matter called the attention of the two governments.
What are the commercial routes of the Dominion ?

The great water route is the St. Lawrence River and the Great Lakes. By railroad, the Grand Trunk, which passes from Detroit, Michigan, through the principal cities of the Dominion to Quebec, with a branch to Portland, Maine.
How is Oceanica divided ?
Into three divisions: Malasia, Australasia, and Polynesia.
What does Malasia comprise ?
The East India Archipelago, which lies to the southeast of Asia. The principal divisions are the Sunda Isles, Spice Islands, Philippine, and Celebes.
Which are the principal Sunda Isles ?
Borneo, Sumatra, and Java.
What are the productions of these islands ?

They are varied, for no part of the world is richer in its vegetable productions. The minerals are also valuable. Nearly all of the spices that we use—such as cinnamon, cloves, nutmegs, and pepper—are raised on these islands. The bread-fruit tree, sago, cocoanut palm, bananas, yams, and various tropical fruits are grown extensively. Coffee is exported from Java; gold and diamonds from Borneo; tin from Banca; sugar, hemp and tobacco from the Philippine Isles.
What is said of the animals of Malasia ?
They are among the largest and fiercest upon the earth. The tiger, rhinoceros, elephant, and ourang-outang are found in Sumatra.
What are the inhabitants ?
They are principally Malays.
What nations have possessions here?

The Dutch, English, Spanish, and Portuguese. Java, the Spice Islands, Sumatra, Celebes, a part of Borneo, and most of Timor belong to the Dutch. The Philippine Isles to Spain. A part of Borneo and Papuan to the English, and a part of Timor belongs to the Portuguese.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.

Which are the most important cities of this archipelago? Where are they situated ? (a) Locate Acheen, Macassar, Bencoolen. (b) What strait separates Sumatra from Java? (c) What strait separates Sumatra from Asia? Borneo from Celebes? (d) Where is Mt. Ophir?
Batavia and Manilla.
How large is Borneo? Sumatra? Java? Celebes? Philippine Isles?
Borneo contains about 260,000 square miles, Sumatra 125,000, Java 52,000, Celebes 46,000, Philippine 66,000.
What State is about the size of Celebes?
Pennsylvania.
In which zone are these islands situated?
The torrid. The equator crosses Sumatra, Borneo, Celebes, and some of the Spice Islands.
What is the surface of these islands?
Most of them are mountainous, and many contain active volcanoes. Java alone contains forty-three.
Are these islands thickly inhabited?
With the exception of Java, they are not. Java, which is about the size of Arkansas, contains 18,000,000 people.
How is Australasia divided?
This is the largest division of Oceanica, and comprises Australia, Papua or New Guinea, Tasmania, New Zealand, New Caledonia, and many other islands.
What race is called Papuans? Where are they found and what is noticeable of them?
They are a cannibal negro race and inhabit a long group of islands extending from New Guinea to New Caledonia.
To what country does Australia, New Zealand, and Tasmania belong?
To Great Britain.
What possessions has France in these divisions?
New Caledonia.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How is Australia divided?
North Australia, South Australia, West Australia, Queen's Land, New South Wales, Victoria, and Alexandra Land.
How large is Australia?
About four-fifths as large as the United States.
What is the surface of this island?
Near the coast are mountain ranges. The central regions have never been explored, but are supposed to consist of a low, barren plain. The Murray is the only river of much importance.
What are the inhabitants?
The greater part of them are Europeans and their descendants. The natives are a race of negroes, called Papuans.
What are the chief pursuits of the people? The chief exports?
Mining and raising wool. They also produce large amounts of wheat, indigo, cotton, sugar, coffee, tin and copper.
What is the government of Australia?
The colonies are politically independent of one another, and are governed by representatives chosen by universal suffrage, and by executive officers appointed by the Crown.
What is the climate? Vegetation?
The northern part is tropical, while the southern part is subjected to sudden successions of floods and droughts.
How is Australia connected, by means of the telegraph, with the countries of Europe?

One line, from the principal cities, crosses the middle of the continent to the northwest coast, and is continued by ocean cable to British India, and thence through Asia and Europe. Other lines and cables connect direct with London.
What is said of the native animals of Australia?
The largest is the kangaroo. The most singular one is the platypus, which has the body of an otter, the bill of a duck, and lays eggs.
Which are the principal cities? Locate them.
Melbourne, Sydney, Adelaide, and Perth.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Is this country thickly settled?
It is not. The estimated number of inhabitants is two million.
Describe Tasmania.
It is about one-half as large as England, and is a fine agricultural island, inhabited solely by European col-onists.
Where is New Zealand? (a) Locate Auckland, Hobarton, Cook Strait. (b) Where is Bass Strait, Torres, Molucca Passage?
It is situated southeast of Asia. These islands are mountainous, and the inhabitants belong to the Malay race.
What is said of Papua and the other islands?
They are but little known. There are no white settlements in any of them except New Caledonia.
What does Polynesia include?
The great number of islands which are scattered throughout the wide expanse of the Pacific Ocean.
Name the principal groups.
The Sandwich, Society, Friendly, Marquesas, Caroline, and Ladrone.
What is the surface of the islands of Polynesia?
They are divided into two classes : The mountain-ous, which are principally of volcanic formation, and the low islands, which are the work of the coral insect.
What are the inhabitants?
Most of the natives belong to the Malay race.
What is said of the animals?
When first discovered, these islands contained neither insects nor reptiles, and no animal larger than a hog.
Which is the only important town in Polynesia?
Honolulu, on Oahu, one of the Sandwich Islands.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Name the Middle States. How do they compare in size to the New England States ?
New York, Pennsylvania, New Jersey and Dela-ware. They are about twice as large.
Which is the larger, New York or Pennsylvania ?
New York contains 47,000 square miles, and Penn-sylvania 46,000.
What is the surface of these States ?

New York is somewhat undulating. The Cats-kill and Adirondack Mountains are in this State. Penn-sylvania is quite mountainous ; here the Alleghany system has attained its greatest breadth. The greater part of New Jersey, Delaware, and Maryland is a low and gener-ally sandy tract.
What can be said of New York?
In wealth, population, and commercial import-ance, it holds the first rank in the Union.
For what are Rochester, Syracuse, West Point, and Saratoga noted ? Locate them.

Rochester has the most extensive flour-mills in the East, and Syracuse the greatest salt-works in the country. West Point is the seat of the United States Military Acad-emy, and Saratoga is well-known as a fashionable watering place.
What is noticeable of Pennsylvania?
It is one of the wealthiest States in the Union. Much of its wealth lies in mining and manufacturing ; but it is also rich in grain and live stock.
What is said of its minerals ?
The mines of iron and coal far surpass those of any other State. Petroleum in large quantities is found in the northwestern part.
For what are Pittsburg and Philadelphia noted?
Pittsburg is the greatest iron manufacturing city, and Philadelphia is the second greatest manufacturing city in the Union.
Where is Fairmount Park? What great event was celebrated there ?
It is in Philadelphia, and the great celebration of 1876, called the Centennial, was held there.
What is the estimated population of the City of New York during the day ? What is the population within a radius of thirty miles ?
It is estimated at 1,500,000. In a radius of thirty miles it is more than 2,500,000.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Describe the Brooklyn Bridge.
See answer No. 651 on United States History.
What great gift of the French people is located in the New York harbor ? Describe it.
The Statue of Liberty Enlightening the World.
What are the pursuits of the people of New Jersey ?
Chiefly agricultural. They supply New York and Philadelphia with vegetables.
What fashionable resorts for sea-bathing in this State? Locate them.
Long Branch, Cape May and Atlantic City.
How large is Delaware ? What are the chief pursuits of the people ?
With the exception of Rhode Island, it is the smallest State in the Union. It contains 2,120 square miles.
Describe the District of Columbia.
It is situated on the Potomac River, and has an area of seventy square miles. It contains Washington and several small towns. It is under the government of Congress.
How great a fall has the cataract of Niagara ?
One hundred and sixty-five feet.
How are the West India Islands divided?
Into three divisions: the Greater Antilles, the Lesser Antilles, and the Bahamas.
What islands belong to the Greater Antilles ?
Cuba, Hayti, Jamaica, and Porto Rico.
What do the Lesser Antilles include ?
The chain of islands which extends from Porto Rico to South America.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What and where are the Bahama Islands ?
They are low islands of coral formation, and lie north of the Greater Antilles.
To what country do these islands belong ?

Cuba and Porto Rico belong to Spain; Jamaica, the Bahamas, and most of the Lesser Antilles belong to Great Britain; Hayti is independent; and the remaining islands are owned by France, Denmark, Holland and Venezuela.
What are the inhabitants of these islands ?
They consist principally of whites, free negroes, and slaves. About one-sixth are white.
What is the surface of the Antilles ?
They are mountainous, and are supposed to be the remains of a mountain chain which at some remote period connected North and South America.
What are the productions ?
Tropical fruits, sugar, coffee, tobacco, rum, mahogany, and spices.
What is rum ?
Spirits distilled from molasses.
How is Hayti divided ?
Into two distinct states: the Republic of Dominica and the Republic of Hayti.
What is the history of this republic?

The island once belonged to France and Spain. In 1791 the slaves in the western, or French division, rose against their masters, and after a bloody and cruel war succeeded in establishing themselves, first as an empire, then as an independent republic. In 1849, the President (Soulouque) proclaimed himself emperor, with the title of Faustin I. After a reign of ten years, Soulouque was deposed and a republican form of government was again adopted. In 1821 the eastern division revolted from Spain and formed a republic.
Where are the Bermuda Islands? To whom do they belong ?
They are east of the United States and belong to Great Britain. There are about 400 of these islands. Most of them are so small and barren that they have neither name nor inhabitants.

How large are Cuba and Hayti ? (a) Name and locate the capital cities of the West Indies. (b) Locate Mantanzas, Kingston, Falmouth, Ponce, Manzanillo, Nuevitas, Samana, Gonaives. (c) Describe the following capes : Maysi, Morant, St. Antonio, Negril, De Cruz, Engano. (d) Describe the Straits Mona Passage, Windward Passage Channel of Yucatan, and Florida Strait. (e) To what divisions do the following islands belong : Barbadoes, Dominica, Guanahani, Antigua, Tobago. Trinidad, Tortuga, St. Thomas, Guadaloupe, Grenada, Isle of Pines, Orchilla ?
Cuba is about the size of Pennsylvania, and Hayti is about half as large as Michigan, containing 28,000 square miles.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are keys?
Small rocky islands of coral formation.
What is a reef?
A chain of rocks lying near the surface of the water.
Describe the southern coast of Florida.
It is fringed with reefs, and as many of the rocks are just below the surface of the water, it becomes dangerous to navigation.
Where is Key West? What are its exports?
It is the most southern town in the United States, and is situated upon an island of coral formation, southwest of the mainland of Florida. Its exports are salt and sponge.
How is salt made in Key West ?
By solar evaporation.
What is sponge ?
A soft porous substance, which is found attached to rocks below the surface of the water, supposed to be the work of marine insects.
What is the general surface of Florida ?
It is generally level, there being no mountains in the State. In the south, dense marshy thickets called everglades cover the surface for a distance of 160 miles with an average breadth of 60 miles.
What are the productions of this State?
Cotton, corn, sugar-cane, rice, and tobacco. Tropical fruits, such as oranges, lemons, figs, pomegranates, pineapples, olives, etc., are abundant.
What is Lake Okeechobee, and where is it ?
It is near the southern part of Florida, and is a great swamp connected with the everglades.
What scheme is proposed by civil engineers in regard to this great central swamp of Florida ?
They propose that capitalists shall drain these lands by a system of canals. As the lands when drained will be unsurpassed in fertility, it is reasonable to suppose that eventually it will be done.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.

Why has this State so few good harbors ? (a) Locate the following cities : Pensacola, Apalachicola, Tallahassee, St. Augustine, St. Marks. (b) How is the Apalachicola River formed ? Describe these rivers. 385. Which State is separated by the Mississippi River?
On account of the numerous reefs, or keys, which skirt the shores, and the lowness of the land as it approaches the sea.
On which side of the river is New Orleans? Describe it.

On the west side. It is the greatest cotton mart in the world, and is about 100 miles from the mouth of the Mississippi. It is built round a bend in the river, and therefore is called the "Crescent City." The foundation is artificial, having been built on a swamp. It lies below the level of the river, but is protected by the Levee, which at this place is a continuous quay, four miles long and 100 feet wide.
What is the soil of Louisiana?

It is various. The richest tract in the State is a narrow belt of land, from one to two miles wide, on both sides of the river, extending from 150 miles above to 100 miles below New Orleans. This tract is annually inundated by the spring floods. Only a small part of the State is under cultivation.
How many territories in the United States? Name them.
There are ten: Washington, Idaho, Montana, Dakota, Wyoming, Utah, Arizona, New Mexico, and Indian Territory. Alaska is not an organized territory.
What does Danish America include?
Greenland and Iceland.
Has Greenland been fully explored?
It has not. The interior and northern parts are unknown, and the supposition by some is, that the island is only a projection of an Arctic continent not yet discovered.
Why was Greenland so named?

It was named by an Icelandic chief, who, for some crime, was obliged to flee from his native land. To induce his countrymen to follow him to Greenland, he falsely represented it to be superior in fertility to Iceland.
Describe Iceland.
It is of volcanic formation. Ranges of high, rugged mountains border the coast, while the interior is a dreary desert of volcanoes, ice-clad mountains, or fields of lava.
What natural curiosities here?
The Geysers, or boiling springs. About fifty of these hot springs occupy an area not exceeding twelve acres. Some are so powerful that they throw up water, and even large stones, to a great height.
What is supposed to be the cause of this phenomenon?
The nearness to Mount Hecla, which is thirty-five miles distant, or the volcanic commotion beneath.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What gave the name of Iceland?
It was so called by a Norwegian pirate, who, on his first visit, saw a bay filled with ice, which had floated there from Greenland.
Which are the principal towns of Danish America?
Lichtenfels and Reikiavik.
How much larger is Asia than Africa?
About 4,000,000 square miles.
What is the greatest length and breadth of Africa?
From north to south it is about 5,000 miles, and its greatest breadth is about 4,800.
Give a short description of Africa.

Large portions of it have not, as yet, been explored. The shores are not indented by deep gulfs or inlets, which is one of the principal reasons why the interior is so little known. The surface is various. There are mountain systems, sandy deserts, and elevated plateaus. The greater part is in the torrid zone, and, much of the country being desert land, it is, as a whole, the hottest and dryest grand division of the globe.
What are the inhabitants?
Except the northern part and some settlements on the coast, the inhabitants are Negro tribes, whose religion is Paganism and their condition barbarous.
What does Barbary include?
Morocco, Algiers, Tunis, Tripoli and Barca.
To what nation do these countries belong?
Morocco is an independent empire. Algiers is a colony of France. Tunis and Tripoli are subjects of Turkey.
How large is the Desert of Sahara? (a) Describe the following mountains: Atlas, Mountains of the Moon, Snow Mountains, Kong, Crystal and Mocambe.
It is from 750 to 1,200 miles wide, and 3,000 in length.
Describe Soudan. What are the exports, and how is commerce carried on?

It occupies a large portion of Central Africa, extending across the continent south of the Great Desert. The soil is fertile, climate tropical and similar to Amazon Valley. It contains a large number of semi-barbarous States with a large population. The religion is largely Mohammedan, although a great many tribes of Negroes are Pagans and combine with their religion a belief in evil spirits, witchcraft and magic charms, and is known as Fetichism. The exports are gold, ivory, ostrich feathers and gum arabic. Commerce is carried on by caravans.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How is Southern Africa divided?
Cape Colony, Natal, Caffraria, the Transvaal Republic, Orange Free States, and Zoolu, Bechuna, Namaqua, and Damara.
How are these countries governed?
Cape Colony and Natal are British colonies. The others are in possession of independent tribes.
Where and what is Liberia?

It is on the western coast of Africa, and is a Negro republic, with a government modeled after that of the United States. Liberia was founded in 1821, by the American Colonization Society, as a place of refuge for free blacks and liberated slaves from the United States.
Where is the colony of Sierra Leone?
It is situated above Liberia, and was founded for the same purpose in 1787, by the British government.

What are the principal exports of Africa? (a) Locate Morocco, Monrovia, Free Town, Cairo, Cape Town, Mozambique, Cobbe, Tripoli, Sego, Condar, Sokoto, Algiers, Antananarivo, Petermaritzburg, Timbuctoo, Wara, Mesna, Coomassie, Kouka, Zanzibar, Graham Town, and Port Natal. (b) What capes project from the four extremities of Africa? (c) Describe the Mozambique Channel, Gulf of Aden, Guinea, Sidra, Niger River, Senegal, Orange, and Congo. (d) Locate the following islands: Azores, Canary, Cape Verd, Ascension, Helena, Madeira, Peak of Teneriffe.

They differ in different parts. The southern portion exports corn, wine, wool, hides, horns, ivory, gold, and diamonds; the western part, ebony, palm oil, gold dust, and ostrich feathers; the northern, fruits, wool, hides, gums, indigo, salt, leather, cotton, rice, opium, coffee, and many other articles.
Describe Madagascar.
It is the largest of the African islands, containing 240,000 square miles. It is a mountainous country, and but little known. Part of the inhabitants belong to the African and part to the Malay race.
What city is the largest in Africa? The second in size?
Cairo is the largest and Alexandria the second.
Where are Port Said and Suez?
At the ends of the Suez Canal.
How long is the Suez Canal? To what nation does it belong?
It is 65 miles long and belongs to Egypt.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Locate the capital cities of the territories of the United States.
Helena, Olympia, Boise City, Cheyenne, Salt Lake City, Prescott, Santa Fe, Tahlequah, Bismarck and Sitka.
For what are Idaho and Montana noted?
For their rich gold and silver mines, and the natural advantages for grazing and stock-raising.
Where is the Yellow Stone Park? What is it?

The Yellowstone Park is in the northwest corner of Wyoming and contains 3,600 square miles. Its deep canyons, lofty falls, and numerous bathing pools, geysers, and lakes, make it in many respects the most wonderful portion of the continent, and even of the world. It has been set aside by Congress as a "perpetual reservation for the benefit and instruction of mankind."
Describe Dakota.
It has less mountains than Idaho or Montana. Much of its surface consists of rolling prairie land, well watered, and especially adapted for farming and cattle-raising.
Describe the Union Pacific Railroad.

This name is given to that part of the great railroad west of the Missouri River. It passes through the southern part of Nebraska, up the valley of the Platte River, touching the northern boundary line of Colorado and the southern part of Wyoming, through Northern Utah and Nevada, and thence through California to the Pacific Ocean.
Locate the principal towns on this railroad.
Omaha, Fremont, Columbus, Kearney, Julesburg, Cheyenne, Laramie, and Ogden.
How is Salt Lake City situated?
It is on the Jordan River, near Great Salt Lake, south of the Union Pacific Railroad, on a branch of that road.
Describe Utah.

It contains the Great Salt Lake and the eastern portion of the Great Basin, which is the most desolate region in the United States. Rain seldom falls in this basin, but some portions near the rivers and lakes have been made fertile by irrigation. Gold, silver, iron, salt, coal and other minerals are plentiful.
What people settled Utah?
The Mormons, or Latter-day Saints.
For what are the southern territories and Texas especially adapted?
For grazing.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How is the western part of Texas occupied?
By tribes of Indians, and large herds of wild horses.
What are the productions of Texas?
Cotton, corn, sugar, tobacco, rice, and tropical fruits. Stock-raising is the principal business of the inhabitants.

What is the climate? (a) Locate Austin, Houston, Matagorda, Brownsville, Corpus Christi, Colorado City, Greeley, Fillmore City, Portland, Ft. Benton, Ft. Union, Sioux City, Ft. Laramie, Silver City, Dallas, Preston, Pembina, Vermillion. (b) Describe the following rivers: Columbia, Snake, Humboldt, Colorado, Gila, Rio Grande, Brazos, Trinity, Arkansas, Smoky Hill Fork, Platte. (c) Locate Pike's Peak, Mt. Hood, Mt. Baker, Mt. Shasta, Diamond Peak, Long Peak, Spanish Peaks, Fremont's Peak, Mt. Fairweather, Mt. St. Elias.

A great portion of the State is much elevated, and therefore free from extremes of heat and cold. Snow is seldom seen, but from October to March, the " Northers " (violent winds) sweep over the prairies of Texas and plains of Mexico.
Which of the States and Territories border on British America?
Maine, New Hampshire, Vermont, New York, Michigan, Minnesota, Dakota, Montana, Idaho, Washington.
Which of the States are separated by the Mississippi River?
Wisconsin, Illinois, Kentucky, Tennessee, Mississippi, on the east, and Minnesota, Iowa, Missouri, Arkansas, and most of Louisiana, on the west.
Which are separated by the Ohio River?
Ohio, Indiana, and Illinois, from Kentucky and West Virginia.
What is the general outline of Europe, and what advantages does it offer?
It is more irregular than that of any other division of the earth. Large seas and gulfs penetrate far into the interior, affording unequaled advantages for commercial intercourse.
What is the surface?

It is divided into extensive plains and mountain systems. Southern Europe is crossed from west to east by a great mountain system, which is continued, in Asia, to the Pacific Ocean. Northward from this system extends a great plain, which stretches from the Atlantic to the Pacific.
What are the principal European parts of this great mountain system? Describe each part.
Caucasus, Balkan, Alps, Cevennes, Pyrenees, and Cantabrian.
What part of Europe is included in the Great Plain?
Russia, Northern Germany, Denmark, Holland, Belgium, and a part of France.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the climate?

It varies in the same latitude. In Eastern Europe the winters are excessively cold, while the summers are very hot. On the shores of the Atlantic the warm ocean current renders the climate mild and moist.
Name the political divisions. How are they classified?
Russia, Austria, Germany and Turkey are empires; Great Britain, Norway and Sweden, Denmark, Holland, Belgium, Spain, Portugal, Italy and Greece are kingdoms; France and Switzerland are republics.
Which divisions are called the "Five Great Powers"?
Great Britain, Russia, Germany, France and Austria.
Name and locate the peninsulas of Europe.
Scandinavian, Jutland, Iberian, Italian, Morea and Crimea.
Describe the Scandinavian peninsula.

It comprises Sweden and Norway, two distinct States with separate governments, but united under one king. Nearly the whole of Norway and the eastern part of Sweden are mountainous. The southeastern part of Sweden is a level plain.
What are the exports of this peninsula?
Iron, copper, lumber, and fish.
How much farther north can grain be raised in Norway than on the Atlantic shore of America?
1,400 miles. These grains are rye, oats and barley.
What point on the American continent is in the same latitude as Christiania?
The southern point of Greenland.
Where is Lapland?
It is in that part of Europe north of the Arctic Circle, and between the White Sea and Atlantic Ocean. It belongs to Russia, Sweden and Norway.
Is this country inhabited?
It is, by a tribe of the Mongolian race called Laplanders, who own numerous herds of reindeer, which supply them with food, clothing and means of traveling.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the size of the Russian Empire?
It is next to the largest, in extent, in the world, comprising fully one-half of Europe, and more than one-third of Asia. The entire area is about 8,000,000 square miles.
What is the surface of this empire?
For the most part it is very level. The only mountainous section in European Russia, is between the Azov and Caspian Seas.
How are the inhabitants divided?
Into four classes: 1, the nobles; 2, the clergy; 3, the merchants; 4, the lately emancipated serfs.
What is the government? Religion?
It is an absolute monarchy. The greater part of the inhabitants are adherents of the Greek Church.
What are the inhabitants called? Their monarch?
The inhabitants are called Sclaves, and their monarch the Czar.
What subject is strongly agitating the minds of the Russian people?
The question of a constitutional government. The Czar has recently signified his willingness to grant one, which if done will place the government among the list of limited monarchies.
Who are the Nihilists? What do they seek to accomplish?
They are a people who believe in self-government and the overthrow of the empire. They resort to deeds of secret personal violence to accomplish their cause.
What political relations, of a strained character, exist between Russia and England?
England is fearful of the Russian advances in Asia and jealously watches every movement. It is believed that the situation is critical and may terminate in a declara-tion of war.
In what does the principal wealth of Russia consist?
In its forests (two-fifths of the country is covered with forests), and the products of agriculture and grazing.
Where are the great wheat-growing regions of Europe?
In Central and Southern Russia.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the nature of the country around the Caspian Sea?
It is mostly a sterile region, and has the appearance of having been subjected to volcanic fires. Salt lakes and fire-hills are numerous.
Describe the Caspian Sea.
It is an inland sea, containing 140,000 square miles, and below the level of the Mediterranean Sea. Several large rivers flow into it, but there is no visible outlet.
What are steppes, and where found?
Large, barren plains in southeastern Russia.
Where is Poland? Circassia?
Poland is that part of Russia lying north of Aus-tria. Circassia is the region of the Caucasus Mountains.
Describe Moscow. When was it burned?

Near Central Russia, on a branch of the Oka River. It is a railroad center, and one of the principal cities for trade. It was burned by the Russians during Napoleon's invasion of 1812, to defeat him, by depriving his troops of their expected winter quarters. This great sacrifice of the Russians saved their empire and compelled Napoleon to withdraw from the country.
For what is Sebastopol famous?
For the siege which it sustained for one year, against the English, French, Turkish and Sardinian armies.
What was the cause of this siege?
It was one of the strongest Russian fortifications, and was a perpetual menace to Turkey. Its destruction ended the Crimean war.
Who inhabit the steppes, or barren plains of Russia?
The Cossacks are the inhabitants, and are of great service to the Russian army as light cavalry.
Where is Niznei Novgorod, and what takes place there every year?
A great fair is held there every year, which is attended by thousands of people who come from different parts of Europe and Asia to buy and sell goods.
Which are the principal ports for foreign commerce? Locate them.
St. Petersburg, Cronstadt, Riga, Archangel, and Odessa.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What seas and gulfs wash the shores of Russia? Describe each.
Caspian, Azov, Black, Baltic, White and Kara; Riga, Finland, Bothnia, Onega and Petchora.
What do the British Islands comprise?
Great Britain, Ireland, and many small adjacent islands.
What does Great Britain include?
England, Scotland and Wales.
What is the surface of the British Islands?
England and Ireland are generally level or undulating; Scotland and Wales are rugged and mountainous.
How is Scotland divided?
It is divided by the Grampian Hills into the Highlands and Lowlands. The Highlands lie north, and the Lowlands south of the mountains.
What is the nature of the sea coast?
It is very irregular, and abounds in fine harbors and roadsteads.
What is the climate?
It is moist and mild. The winters, even of Scotland, are rarely severe. The moisture of the climate and low temperature in summer sometimes prevent crops from maturing. Corn will not ripen.
In what does the chief wealth of Great Britain consist?
In her commerce, manufactures and mining.
How does she rank with other nations in the amount of her manufactures and commerce?
She surpasses every other country in the world. The principal manufactures are those of cotton, wool, and iron.
What is the extent of the merchant vessels of Great Britain? Her seamen? How is the English navy regarded, and how does it compare with other countries?

They have 25,000 merchant vessels and more than 200,000 seamen. The navy is regarded as the strongest in the world, although that of Russia is hardly inferior. Germany, France and Italy have powerful navies. The United States have practically none.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How do the armies of Europe compare with that of the United States? What is the natural result of such immense defenses?

Great Britain .................150,000 men. German Empire................475,000 " France.......................500,000 " Italy........................734,000 " Russia.......................975,000 " United States................25,000 " Besides these immense armies they hold in reserve millions subject to call. The result of such enormous defence is a distressing taxation and must eventually breed discontent and civil revolution. No country can stand for a long period the terrible drain on the resources of the people.
What is the government of Great Britain? What is Parliament? Who is the Prime Minister?

It is a constitutional monarchy. The Legislature, or Parliament, has two branches—the House of Lords and the House of Commons. The members of the House of Commons are elected by the people. The executive power and the entire responsibility of the government are in the hands of the Ministry, who are appointed by the sovereign through the influence of the Commons. The Prime Minister is practically the real ruler of the empire ; if opposed by a majority of the House of Commons, he resigns, and the sovereign appoints his successor.
What is the population of the several divisions?
Where is tin obtained?
Principally from Cornwall (England) and the Island of Banca. The mines of Cornwall have been famous from remote antiquity.
What is the chief article of fuel in Ireland?
Peat, or bog turf, of which there are over 3,000,000 acres.
What is the extent of the British Empire?
It has colonies in every quarter of the globe, and unites under one sovereign a greater number of people than are ruled by any other government.
Which are the principal possessions of Great Britain in Europe?
Gibraltar in Spain, and the Islands of Malta and Gozo.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Which in Asia?
British India, including Ceylon, Hindoostan, a part of Farther India, and Singapore ; the Island of Hong Kong, and the City of Aden.
In Africa?
Sierra Leone and other settlements on the western coast ; Cape Colony and Natal ; the Islands of Seychelle, Mauritius, Ascension and St. Helena.
In Oceanica?
Australia, Tasmania, New Zealand, Norfolk, Labuan, and a part of Borneo.

In America? (a) Locate Malta, Gozo, Ceylon, Singapore, Hong Kong, Aden, Mauritius and Seychelle Islands. (b) Where are the Hebrides, Orkney, Shetland, Anglesea, Man, Isle of Wight, Skye, Mull, Lewis, Uist, Islay, Arran? (c) Describe St. George's Channel, North Channel, English Channel, Bristol Channel, Dover Strait, Menai, The Minch, Little Minch, Jura Sound. (d) Describe the following capes: Land's End, Lizard Pt., Dunnet Head, Kinnaird Head, Malin Head, Mizzen Head, Flamborough Head, Butt of Lewis, and Cape Wrath.
British America, Bermuda Islands, Balize, Jamaica, Bahama Islands, and many of the Lesser Antilles, British Guiana, and the Falkland Islands.
For what are Liverpool, Leeds and Bradford, Birmingham, Limerick, and Merthyr Tydvil noted? Locate them.

Liverpool, for its great commerce; its fine dock covers more than 400 acres. Leeds and Bradford, for their manufactures of wool. Birmingham, for hardware. Limerick, for the manufacture of gloves, laces, and fish-hooks. Merthyr Tydvil, for its iron-works.
How large an area is covered by the City of London?
It covers 122 square miles.
What questions agitate the people of Ireland, and what measures have been proposed in Parliament to satisfy these demands? 488. Describe the Spanish Peninsula.

The question of Home Rule. In order to bring content, the great political leader of the English people, Mr. Gladstone, proposed a bill to grant them a Home Government subject to the Crown. The bill was defeated, but still continues to agitate the people of England. Mr. Gladstone also proposed to have the government buy the great estates of Ireland and sell them in small farms, thus giving the Irish people a chance to own their own lands.
What are the productions of Spain?
The vine, olive, mulberry, and orange. Large flocks of sheep are raised on the tablelands of the interior.
What parallel crosses the central portion of Spain, and through which of the United States does the same parallel pass?

The 40th parallel. It passes through Central United States, or New Jersey, Southern Pennsylvania, Ohio, Indiana, Illinois, Northern Missouri, Central Nebraska and Kansas, Colorado, Utah, Nevada, and Northern California.
What other countries in Europe does the same parallel cross?
Southern Italy and Turkey.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Were we to follow this parallel, what countries in Asia would we pass through?
Turkey, Georgia, Turkistan, Pekin, and the Empire of Japan.
Where is the Republic of Andorra?
In the northeastern part of the Spanish Peninsula.
What is noticeable of this republic?
The population is only 5,000 or 6,000, but the country has been independent for more than a thousand years.
What are the foreign possessions of Spain? Of Portugal?
Cuba, Porto Rico, a part of the Philippines, Ladrone, Caroline, and the Canary Islands. Those of Portugal are the Azores, the Cape Verd, and the Madeira Islands, and Lower Guinea in Africa.
How wide is the Strait of Gibraltar?
Twelve miles in its narrowest place.
What is noticeable of the Fortress of Gibraltar?
It is situated on a mountainous promontory, and is the strongest fortification in the world.
How large is France?
It contains 204,000 square miles.
How does she rank with other countries?
As one of the richest and most powerful. The position of the country and the fertility of the soil give it great commercial and agricultural advantages.
What is the surface?
The eastern portion is mountainous, while the rest belongs to the Great Plain, which extends across the continent.
What and where are the landes?
They are plains of shifting sands in the south-western part of France.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are the principal exports?
France exports more wine, brandy, and silk goods than any other country.
What influence has Paris throughout the civilized world?
The influence of fashions.

Name and locate the principal possessions of France. (a) Where is Pondicherry? (b) Locate Minorca, Majorca, Sardinia, and Ivica. (c) Describe Bay of Biscay, Gulf of Lyons, Gulf of Genoa, and Strait of Bonifacio.
Algeria, Senegal, Pondicherry, Isle of Bourbon, New Caledonia, Marquesas Islands, French Guiana, St. Pierre and Miquelon, Martinique, Guadaloupe, and Corsica.
What are the pursuits of the people of Holland and Belgium? What is Holland now called?
In Holland, stock-raising and dairying are the chief pursuits. Belgium is an agricultural and manufacturing country; it also has extensive mines of coal and iron. Holland is now called Netherlands.
What is the condition of much of the coast of this country?
It is so low that in many places dykes, or embankments, are necessary to prevent inundations.
How is internal communication promoted?
By numerous canals. In Holland especially, canals run through the principal streets of the towns, and form a complete network over the country.
What is the Zuyder Zee?
An arm of the sea, or gulf, which indents the northern part of Holland.
What people are called Dutch?
The people of Holland.
What are the colonial possessions of Netherlands? How valuable are they regarded?

Next to Great Britain they are the most extensive and valuable in the world. They include the most important portions of the East Indian Archipelago, Dutch Guiana, and several islands in the West Indies.
What are the occupations of the people? The commerce? Exports? Wealth?

Agriculture is the principal occupation in the Netherlands and manufacturing and mining in Belgium. Their foreign commerce is one of the largest in the world and their exports are fine manufactured goods, including those of silk, cotton, wool, linen, leather and iron. Lace forms an important industry. Many of the people control great amounts of wealth.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the surface of Denmark?
It is nearly flat; some portions are below the surface of the sea, from which it is defended by dykes.
How is Copenhagen situated?
On the eastern coast of the Island of Zealand, and partly on the adjacent Island of Amak. Many of the buildings are built above the water.
What are the colonial possessions of Denmark?
Greenland, Iceland, Faroe Islands, and several small islands in the West Indies.
What does the German Empire comprise?
