The kingdoms of Prussia, Bavaria, Würtemburg, and Saxony, six grand duchies, five duchies, seven principalities, three free cities, and the new State of Alsace.
Which are the free cities?
Hamburg, Bremen and Lübeck.
Where is the State of Alsace?
It was formerly a part of France, but since the late Franco-Prussian war it was ceded to Germany.
What are the capitals of the four divisions?
Prussia is Berlin, Saxony is Dresden, Bavaria is Munich, Wurtemberg is Stuttgart.
Describe the government of Germany.

It is a limited monarchy, and all the States are the same, with the King of Prussia as the German Emperor. The Legislature of the empire consists of two bodies. The Federal Council somewhat resembles the English House of Lords and consists of 59 members; and the Reichstag, whose members, 397 in number, are elected by universal suffrage.
What can be said of its military power?
It is the strongest in the world, and every move of the government is to strengthen its position, its armor and its power.
What of its school system? Its manufactures?

Prussia has the most complete and comprehensive system of public schools of any nation, and the general education of the whole empire is remarkably good. The manufactures are varied and extensive, and they export wheat, wine, zinc, and almost every description of manufactured goods.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Which are the chief ports of Germany?
Hamburg and Bremen.
What are the productions?
Nearly the whole of Germany is under cultivation, and large crops of wheat, rye, barley, oats, tobacco, beet-root, and linseed are produced.
What are heaths?
Sandy tracts in Northern Germany and Denmark.
What are the inhabitants of Austria?
They are of different races. The Germans, Hungarians, Italians and Poles are most numerous.
What is the proper title of Austria?
The Austro-Hungarian Empire.
How is this empire divided? What is the government?

It is divided into two distinct and nearly independent groups of provinces. Each group has a government of its own, but the whole is under one general government. The Kingdom of Hungary and its dependencies constitute nearly one-half of the empire. The Emperor of Austria is King of Hungary.
What are the exports of Austria?
The chief exports are wheat, wine, salt, wool and the manufactures of linen, cotton, iron and glass.
What can be said of the Danube River?
It forms an important system of waterways, extending entirely across the empire.
How is Vienna situated?
On eighty-two small islands, separated by 150 canals crossed by 360 elevated bridges.
What was Poland formerly?
An important kingdom of Central Europe, but, after suffering many reverses, it was divided between Russia, Germany and Austria.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Which is the most mountainous country of Europe?
Switzerland.
Of how many States is Switzerland composed? How large is it?
It is composed of twenty-two States, or cantons. It is about one-third as large as the State of New York.
For what are the Swiss noted? What language do they speak?
For their love of liberty, and attachment to their native country. Most of the population speak the German language. Italian and French are spoken in the cantons adjoining Italy and France.
For what is Geneva noted?
For the manufacture of watches and jewelry.
How is Italy governed?

It was formerly divided into a number of States, but after a protracted struggle, the efforts of the people to obtain national unity were successful, and the whole country is now united under a liberal and enlightened government.
In ancient times, for what was this country celebrated?
It was the central part of the Roman Empire, which ruled all the known world.
For what are the towns and cities noted?
Almost every place is connected with some remarkable event, which is renowned in history or art.
Where is San Marino?
It is a small republic, with an independent government, on the Adriatic Sea.
To what country do the Islands of Sardinia and Sicily belong?
Italy.
What is the extent of the Ottoman Empire?

It comprises Turkey in Europe, Asiatic Turkey, Isle of Candia, and Egypt. Tripoli, Barca, and Tunis are nominally under the control of the government. Also a portion of Arabia, Crete and other small islands.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Describe the Turk.
They are a grave and solemn people,—ignorant, bigoted, and indolent. Their religion is Mohammedanism.
How does Turkey compare with other European countries in regard to civilization, agriculture, and the arts and sciences?
She is inferior in every particular. The people are not energetic and their civilization is far below the standard.
What are Roumania, Servia and Montenegro?
They were formerly parts of the Turkish Empire, but are now independent Christian States.
How is Bulgaria governed?
Bulgaria is a self-governing but tributary principality to Turkey.
What are the exports of Turkey?
Tobacco, wine, olive-oil, cotton, fruits, wool, carpets, and morocco leather.
What was Greece in ancient times?
This country was in advance of all other nations in civilization and learning. Athens was the birthplace of many renowned philosophers and orators.
What is the shape of Greece?

It is very irregular, projecting southward, and having many indentations from the sea, one of which, the Gulf of Lepanto, nearly separates it, forming the peninsula of Morea, which is connected with the mainland by the Isthmus of Corinth.
Name and locate the major capital cities of Europe.
St. Petersburg, Stockholm, Copenhagen, Berlin, Hague, Brussels, Paris, Madrid, Lisbon, Rome, Vienna, Athens, Constantinople, Berne, London, Edinburgh.
With what country is England most extensively engaged in trade?
The United States.
Which is the most northern town in Europe? Why is this port never frozen?
Hammerfest. It is due to the warm waters of the Gulf stream which flow by the coast of Norway, and the warm southwest return tradewinds.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Name the four most important rivers of Europe.
The Po, the Rhone, the Rhine and the Danube.
What countries are termed Spanish America?
Mexico, Central America, West Indies, and the greater part of South America.
What part of Spanish America is still in possession of Spain?
Cuba and Porto Rico.
What is the surface of Mexico?

The interior consists of tablelands from 4,000 to 9,000 feet high. The Pacific coast is abrupt and abounds in fine harbors, but upon the Gulf of Mexico it is flat and sandy, and deficient in good harbors.
Are the tablelands accessible from the Gulf?
Only by two carriage roads ; one by Jalapa, from Vera Cruz ; the other by Saltillo, from Matamoras and Monterey.
What is the climate?

As Mexico contains high and lowlands, it exhibits a great variety of climate. In ascending from the coast to the surface of the plateau, there are three regions : the hot, swampy and pestilential lowlands ; the temperate regions ; and the cold, desolate plains above.
What are the inhabitants?
Whites, Indians, mixed races and Spanish Creoles. The Indians and mixed races form the greater part of the population.
How are the seasons divided?
The wet and dry. The rainy season continues from May to October, and the dry from October to May.
What are the productions? Its commerce?

They vary with climate. Wheat and barley are produced in the cooler regions, and cotton, tobacco, sugar-cane, tropical fruits and spices in the plains and valleys below. Commerce is limited and is mostly with England and the United States.
What are the principal minerals?
Gold and silver, iron and copper.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How is the City of Mexico situated?

It is situated in a plain 7,000 feet above the level of the sea, enclosed by lofty mountains. The city is about two miles from Lake Tezcuco, is in the form of a square, and noted for its numerous churches, convents, and squares.
What two volcanoes are visible from the city? What do their names signify?
Popocatapetl and Iztaccihuatl. The former is 17,717 feet high, and its name signifies Smoking Mountain. The latter is 15,600 feet high, and is White Lady.
What is cochineal? Pulque?

The cochineal is an insect which feeds on a species of cactus. When killed and dried in the sun it yields a brilliant crimson dye. Pulque is a favorite Mexican beverage, manufactured from the Maguey plant. It is intoxicating.
What is the history of Mexico?

In 1521 Fernando Cortez, a Spanish adventurer, conquered the country. He found an extensive kingdom, with regular laws, and with cities, temples, public roads, and many of the arts of civilization. After the conquest the cruelty of the Spaniards drove the Indians back into a state of barbarism. In 1821 Mexico became independent of Spain, and established first an empire, then a republican form of government. The people are restless, and revolutions have been common. By the war with the United States she lost valuable territory.
What peninsulas belong to this republic?
Yucatan and Old California. These parts are but little known. Yucatan is noted for its ruins of ancient cities and temples.
What is the government of Mexico?
It is a federal republic of twenty-seven States, one territory, and a small federal district which contains the capital. It was for three centuries the most important colony of Spain.
What political differences occurred between Mexico and the United States in 1886?

The question of difference is called the Cutting Case, the Mexican government claiming the right to arrest an American citizen for an act committed on American soil. In this case Cutting published an article which was considered libelous by Mexican authority and on his crossing the line was arrested and thrown into prison. This act was condemned by the United States government and Cutting was released, but the question of the right to arrest has not been settled.
What does Central America comprise?
Five independent republics: Guatemala, Honduras, San Salvador, Nicaragua, Costa Rica and the British colony of Belize. This country bears a general resemblance to Mexico.
Locate the capital cities.
Comayagua, San Salvador, Managua, Nicaragua and San Jose.
Where is Belize? To what country does it belong?
It is situated south of the eastern part of Yucatan. It belongs to the British.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are the exports of Belize? (a) Locate the following cities: Vera Cruz, Matamoros, Monterey, Merida, Belize, San Luis Potosi, Puebla, Acapulco, Guatemala.
Mahogany and other hard cabinet wood, cochineal, tortoise shells, sarsaparilla and cocoa-nuts.
What three routes were projected from the Atlantic to the Pacific?
Across the Isthmus of Panama; by way of the San Juan River and Lake Nicaragua; and by the Isthmus of Tehuantepec.
How does Mexico compare in size to the United States?
About one-fourth as large.
In what zones is Mexico?
North temperate and torrid; the Tropic of Cancer in the central part.
Where are the greatest copper regions in the United States?
In Northern Michigan and Wisconsin.
Where is lead found most abundantly? (a) How many Western States? Name them. (b) How many Southern States? Name them.
In Southwestern Wisconsin and parts of Illinois and Iowa which adjoin; also in Nevada and Colorado.
What are the great staple productions of the Southern States?
Cotton, tobacco, rice, sugar, corn and sweet potatoes.
Which State produces the most cotton? Rice? Sugar? Tobacco? Potatoes? Corn? Wheat?
Which State has the greatest number of swine? Cattle? Horses? Mules? Sheep?
Which State produces the greatest quantity of gold? Silver? Iron? Copper? Lead? Coal? Pine lumber? Turpentine, resin and tar?
Gold, California ; silver, Colorado ; iron, Pennsylvania ; copper, Michigan ; lead, Illinois ; coal, Pennsylvania ; lumber, Michigan ; turpentine, etc., North Carolina.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How is turpentine, resin and tar manufactured?

Turpentine and resin are made of the sap of the pitch pine, by boiling; turpentine is the vapor, while resin, like sugar, is what remains. Tar is also made from the pitch, but by burning; the timber having been placed in nearly a perpendicular position, and covered to prevent a flame, the heat causes the pitch to ooze out and run below where it can be saved.
From what does North Carolina derive her chief wealth?
From her pine forests.
What is South Carolina often called?
On account of the abundance of palmetto trees which grow there, it is often called the " Palmetto State."
What article can be raised extensively in all the States and Territories?
Corn.
Into what sections is the United States divided?
The Atlantic Slope, the Pacific Slope, and the Central Plain.
What does the Atlantic Slope embrace?
The country which extends from the Alleghany Mountains to the Atlantic Ocean and Gulf of Mexico.
What does the Pacific Slope embrace?
The country between the Rocky Mountains and the Pacific Ocean.
What division is included in the Central Plain?
The Mississippi Valley, the Texas Slope, the basin of the St. Lawrence, and the small valley of the Red River of the North.
Into what divisions may the United States be divided with reference to its drainage? 591. What is the surface of Asia ?
The St. Lawrence Basin, Atlantic Slope, Mississippi Valley, Texas Slope, Pacific Slope, inland Basin of Utah, and Red River.
What divisions can we apply to this country ?
It may be divided into four divisions: Arctic Asia, Desert Asia, Peninsular Asia and Insular Asia.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What portion is included in Arctic Asia? In Desert Asia? In Peninsular Asia? In Insular Asia?

Arctic Asia represents the Great Siberian Plain. Desert Asia is that belt of high and dry plateaus, extending through the central sections. Peninsular Asia is the most important division of the continent, and includes Hindoostan, Indo-China, Corea, with the projecting portion of China. Insular Asia includes the richest and most important archipelagoes in the world.
What portion of the population of the human race are in Peninsular Asia?
One-half of the human race.
What can be said of the productions of Asia ?
As it embraces all varieties of climates, so, too, are the productions of all descriptions, from those of the arctic regions to those of the tropical climate.
Where is the longest inland commercial route on the globe ?
The caravan trade from Pekin through Irkoutsk, Tobolsk, to Moscow and St. Petersburg.
How are the tablelands divided? What is said of them ?
They are divided into two classes—those of Central and those of Southern Asia. They are the loftiest and most extensive in the world.
What countries constitute the Great Northern Plain of Asia?
Turkistan and Siberia. This is a continuation of the great plain of Europe.
Describe the mountain system of Asia.

It commences with the Taurus Mountains, and is continued by the Elburz and Hindoo Koosh to the Balor Mountains. From this point it branches off into four great chains—the Himalaya, Kuen Lun, Thian Shan, and Alti—which are the grandest and most stupendous mountains upon the globe.
Where is the Great Desert of Gobi ?
It is near the central part of Asia, south of the Thian Shan Mountains. This desert is only a part of an immense belt of desert land which extends almost across the continent from west to east.
How may the climate be described ?
Into three divisions, that of Northern, Central, and Southern Asia.
What is the climate of Northern Asia ?
It is characterized by intense cold, with short intervals of great heat during summer.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Describe Siberia.

It is a great plain, comprising more than one-third of Asia. Near the Arctic Ocean it is a barren and desolate region, and the cold is so intense that the spongy soil is frozen to the depth of several hundred feet. Near the Irtysh River the soil is rich and pasturage good, but there are few inhabitants.
What is the climate of Central and Southern Asia?

Central Asia has the climate of the temperate zone. It is subject to great extremes of heat and cold, and, except near the shores of the Pacific, is remarkably dry—for the mountain system intercepts the warm and moist winds of the Indian Ocean. Southern Asia has the climate of the torrid zone.
To what three races do the inhabitants of Asia belong ?
Mongolian, Caucasian, and the Malay.
What people belong to the Mongolian race?
Those nations living north of the Himalaya Mountains, those of Farther India, the Japanese, and Turks.
What nations, or tribes, living in Asia, belong to the Caucasian race ?
The Georgians, Armenians, Arabs, Persians, Afghans, and Hindoos.
What countries are occupied by the Malay race ?
The Peninsula of Malacca, and many of the islands of Oceanica.
What is the estimated number of inhabitants of the several races ?
Caucasian race, 554,500,000; Mongolian, 554,500,000; Ethiopian, 200,000,000; Malay, 56,000,000; American, or Indian race, 13,000,000. Total, 1,380,000,000.
For what has Siberia long been used ?
As a place of banishment for exiles and criminals. A large portion of the population consists of them and their descendants.
To what country does Georgia belong? What is noticeable of the inhabitants ?
It is a Russian province. The people are a vigorous and handsome race.
How is Turkestan divided? What is the government of each?

Into Russian and Independent Turkistan. The Russian division contains a number of districts called Khanates, which are ruled by native rulers, although only as vassals of Russia. Independent Turkistan includes those Tartar khanates that are not yet annexed by Russia, representing only a small area of what was formerly Turkistan.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the Khan ? (a) Locate the following cities : Tobolsk, Irkoutsk, Tashkend, Bokhara, Yarkand, Khokan, Khiva, Tiflis, Smyrna, Jerusalem, Damascus, Medina, Mecca, Mocha, Aden, Muscat.
The name given to the rulers of the several independent States of Turkistan.
What does the Chinese Empire include?
It includes China, the dependent provinces of Mantchooria, Mongolia, Kashgaria and Thibet, and the tributary kingdom of Corea.
What is said of the population of China ?

So dense is the population in some districts that great numbers of the inhabitants dwell constantly upon boats in the rivers, or on rafts in the lakes, which they convert into gardens by covering them with earth. They are a very industrious people, but deceitful and very immoral, and jealous of Europeans.
What are the productions of China?
The most important are rice, tea and silk. Rice is the chief article of food for the entire population.
What is the government ?
A despotic monarchy. The laws are severe; for trifling offenses punishment with the bamboo is inflicted, while serious crimes almost always meet with death.
What about the education of the Chinese ?
Education is compulsory. All adult males are able to read and write and possess a knowledge of the elements of arithmetic.
How are all government officers below the emperor selected ?
They are selected by means of a public competitive examination, and the humblest subject may be chosen to the highest position, except the Emperor.
What can be said of these people in regard to their trades, arts and sciences ?

The domestic commerce is immense, but the foreign commerce is in the hands of foreigners. Their skill in their various occupations cannot be excelled, and many of our first inventions were known to them hundreds of years before being discovered by the Europeans, but these inventions were not put to a practical use.
Describe the great wall of China. For what purpose was it built ?

The wall is 1,200 miles long, from thirty to forty feet high, and twenty feet thick with numerous towers and equipments for defense. The wall was built about 211 B. C. to keep the Mongol Tartars out of China.
Which are the most important seaports for foreign trade ? Locate them.
Canton, Shanghai, Ningpo, Fuchow, Amoy.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Name the principal islands of Japan.
Nipon, Sikoke, Kiusui, and Jesso.
What are the chief productions of these islands ?
Rice, wheat, barley, tea, tobacco, and camphor.
What is the government of Japan ?
It is a constitutional monarchy.
What is the emperor called ?
He is called the Mikado.
How do the Japanese compare in enlightenment with the Chinese ?

They surpass the Chinese in intelligence and equal them in mechanical skill. Although of the same race, and having similar beliefs, yet they differ greatly in their language and political institutions. They are quick to adopt the great inventions of the Europeans, such as their military systems, lighthouses, steam power and electricity.
What two peninsulas comprise India ?
Hindoostan and Indo-China.
What does British India include ?
Nearly all of Hindoostan and the western coast of Indo-China. It is the richest and most important dependency ever possessed by any nation.
How is Indo-China divided ?
It includes Burmah, Siam, Anam and many smaller independent States. All have despotic governments.
What peculiarity is found in the literature of the Hindoos ?
It is written in a peculiar poetical and religious language which has been the custom long before the Christian era. Besides being poetical, they are almost universally musical.
What is the government of British India ?
The country is under the control of a Governor-General and a Supreme Council appointed by the British government. The Queen of England is "Empress of India."

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Where is Ceylon, and what is it ?
It is a rich and populous island south of Hindoostan and a separate colony of Great Britain.
Where is the country of Cochin-China ?
The southeastern coast of Indo-China is called Cochin-China.
Where do the French hold possessions ?
They possess an important province in the southwestern part of Indo-China, and Saigon is its capital.
Name and locate the capitals of India.
Calcutta, Colombo, Mandalay, Bangkok and Hue.
What are the productions of India? (a) Locate Singapore, Rangoon, Monchobo, Bombay, Madras, Benares, Mandalay, Cabul, Candahar, Herat, Kelat, and Lassa.
Cotton, rice, wheat, opium, sugar, indigo, jute, fine silk fabrics and costly cashmere shawls. Ceylon is noted for its coffee and cinnamon.
Describe Afghanistan.

Persia, Afghanistan and Beloochistan occupy the desert plateau of Iran, between the valleys of the Tigris and the Indus. The people of Afghanistan are hardy, warlike, and divided into numerous states or tribes. As this country is the chief route from India to Western Asia, it is of great commercial and military importance.
What can be said of Beloochistan?
This country is mostly a barren wilderness, and consists of a number of small States under the headship of the Khan of Kelat.
What is the government of Persia ?
A despotic monarchy. The sovereign is called a Shah.
What is the surface of Arabia ?
The greater part is a desert plateau. The principal fertile tracts are Oman and Yemen, and the mountain valleys.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Who are the inhabitants ?

The greater portion of them are Bedouins, who are a wandering people and inhabit the deserts, devoting themselves to the care of their camels, horses, goats and sheep. They are generous and hospitable, but quarrelsome, revengeful, and addicted to plunder.
For what is Mecca noted ? Medina ?
Mecca is regarded by the Mohammedans as a holy city, the birthplace of Mohammed, and is annually visited by vast numbers of pilgrims. Medina is the burial place of Mohammed.
What is the general appearance of the Persians ?
They are very polite in their manners, and having a comparatively high civilization, are often called the French of Asia.
What is the ruler of Persia called ?
He is called the Shah.
What can be said of the past history of Persia ?
It was once one of the grandest and most formidable governments of the world.
Describe Arabia. Its government.

Arabia is about one-third as large as the United States. It consists of three regions: A great plateau, having many large and fertile valleys, occupies the interior; surrounding this is a broad belt of deserts, covering more than one-third of the peninsula; along the coasts is a low and narrow sandy plain, containing a few fertile districts. There are several independent States governed by despots called Sultans.
Name and locate the peninsulas of Asia.
Arabia, Hindoostan, Farther India, Corea, and Kamtschatka.
Where is the Isthmus of Kraw?
It connects Malacca with the mainland.

Which is the most elevated lake in the world? (a) Locate Formosa, Hainan, Socotra, Nova Zembla, Ceylon, Laccadive, Maldive, Hong Kong, Lew Chew, Saghalien, Kurile, Aleutian, New Siberia. (b) Describe the following straits and capes: Babel-Mandeb, Ormus, Tartary, Corea, Formosa, Manaar, Malacca, Comorin, Ras-al-Gat, Cambodia, Lopatka, and Vostochnoi.
Sirikol, the source of the Amoo River, on the table-land of Pamer, is 15,600 feet above the level of the sea.
Where is Palestine? Jerusalem?
Palestine is in the northeastern part of Arabia, on the Mediterranean Sea. Jerusalem is a city of Palestine.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How wide is Bering Strait? Gibraltar? Dover?
Behring 40 miles, Dover 30, Gibraltar 12.
How wide is the Isthmus of Panama? Suez? Tehuantepec?
Panama 27 miles, Tehuantepec 130, Suez 65.
Which are the largest five islands in the world?
Australia, Greenland, Borneo, New Guinea, and Madagascar.
What is the size of the oceans?

The Pacific Ocean contains about 82,000,000 square miles; greatest width, 10,000 miles. The Atlantic, 30,000,000; width, 5,000. The Indian, 22,000,000; width 6,000. Antarctic, 12,000,000; and the Arctic, 4,000,000.
What is the highest point reached by Arctic explorers? By the Antarctic?
By Capt. Perry, in 1827, 502 miles from the Pole. By Capt. Ross, in 1842, 803 miles from the Pole.
Name four of the largest cities in the world, in order of population.
Name the next four in order.
New York 1,400,000, Berlin 1,350,000, Vienna 1,300,000, St. Petersburg 1,000,000.
Name the five largest cities of the United States.
New York, Philadelphia 974,000, Chicago 724,000, Brooklyn 625,000, St. Louis 420,000.
In the production of what minerals does Great Britain exceed every other country?
Coal, iron, salt, lead, and tin.
Name the largest seven rivers in the United States. Describe them.
Mississippi, Missouri, Arkansas, Rio Grande, Columbia, Nebraska, and Red.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the population of the Chinese Empire? Japan? Hindustan? United States?
Of the Chinese Empire 400,000,000, Japan 37,000,000, Hindoostan 225,000,000, United States 60,000,000.
How does China compare, in size and population, to the United States?
It is a little more than one-fourth as large as the United States, and contains about 420,000,000 people.
What are the principal uses of rivers?
For drainage, commerce and milling.
What important places of the globe are situated on or near the 40th parallel north latitude?
Philadelphia, Columbus, Indianapolis, Spring-field, Denver, Pekin, Constantinople, and Madrid.
What part of the United States is in the same latitude north of the equator, that the southern part of Africa is south of it?
The boundary line between the States of Mississippi, Alabama and Georgia, from Tennessee.
What is the latitude of Cape Horn? Cape Good Hope?
Cape Horn is 53° south, and Good Hope about 32°.
What is the difference between the location of commercial and manufacturing cities?
Commercial cities require good harbors and water communication to facilitate commerce, while manufacturing cities depend very much upon water power to run their machinery.
Why is the water of Great Salt Lake not fresh?
Lakes of this class lose water by evaporation only, and retain the salt and other materials carried into them by their affluent rivers.
Name five of the principal articles exported by the people of the United States.
Cotton, wheat, pork, cheese, machinery.
Name five of the principal articles imported.
Wool, tea, coffee and spices, dry goods, sugar and liquors.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are the great waterways for the internal commerce of the United States?
It may be divided as follows: The Atlantic system, the Mississippi system, the Great Lakes with the St. Lawrence River, and the canals.
What obstructions are usually found in the Atlantic system to prevent large boats from traversing them?

The rivers in the Atlantic system are usually rapid and form great manufacturing points, which prevent a free passage of large vessels. Besides these obstructions to navigation, large sand-bars are formed at the mouths of many of these streams, which prevent navigation only at high tide.
Describe the Mississippi system.

This system contains fifty-six great tributaries, containing 17,000 miles navigable to steamboats and more than 20,000 by barge. It carries an immense commerce, but in some localities the annual floods and droughts prevent a continuous shipment. Below the mouth of the Ohio the Mississippi sometimes rises more than fifty feet.
What does the St. Lawrence comprise?

It contains the Great Lakes, Superior, Michigan, Huron, Erie, Ontario and the navigable streams which flow into and through the St. Lawrence River. This system is closed for several months in the year on account of ice, but floods or droughts never affect it.
Where are the chief canals located?

The principal canals are those that lie at the entrance of Lake Superior, the Niagara Falls, the Rapids of the St. Lawrence and the Erie Canal. Besides these great commercial canals there is in contemplation the Hennepin to connect the Mississippi River system with Lake Michigan, the Fox River, the canals of North Carolina and Florida.
What can be said of the railroads of the United States?
They form one vast network of connections, to which the great prosperity of the country is due. It opens up inaccessible sections, calls for emigration and virtually annihilates distance.
What are called Trunk Lines?
Like rivers, they form numerous systems, and the main lines of these systems are called Trunk Lines.
Into what systems may these roads be divided?
The Eastern Trunk Lines, the Central, the Southern and the Western.
Name the principal roads of the Eastern Trunk Lines.
The Grand Trunk, New York Central, Erie, Pennsylvania, and Baltimore & Ohio.
Which of these transacts the most business? Describe it.

The New York Central, which runs from Buffalo to Albany, and there is divided into two roads: the Hudson River Railroad to New York and the Boston & Albany to Boston. From Buffalo to Albany the road maintains four tracks. This road is known as the Trunk Line of the Vanderbilt System, and has heavy branches as feeders, the Lake Shore & Michigan Southern from Chicago to Buffalo being the most important. The traffic on this system is immense.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Name the Central Trunk Lines.

They are the Michigan Central; Lake Shore & Michigan Southern; Toledo, Wabash & Western; Atlantic & Great Western; Nickle Plate; Pittsburg, Fort Wayne & Chicago; Pittsburg, Cincinnati & St. Louis; Marietta & Cincinnati; Ohio & Mississippi; Illinois Central; Chicago & Alton.
How many compose the Southern System?

These lines may be divided into three divisions: those of the Atlantic coast, connecting the Atlantic cities with those of the Eastern States; those running from the Atlantic west, crossing the Mississippi River at New Orleans, Vicksburg and Memphis; and those running north and south connecting the cities of the South with the cities of the North.
Mention the most important of the Western System.

The Northern Pacific; the Central Pacific; Union Pacific; Kansas Pacific; Southern Pacific; Atchison, Topeka & Santa Fe; Texas Pacific; New Orleans Pacific; Galveston, Harrisburg & San Antonio; Chicago, Burlington & Quincy; Chicago, Rock Island & Pacific; Chicago & Northwestern; Chicago & Alton; Wabash, St. Louis & Pacific; Chicago, Milwaukee & St. Paul.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Reading. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is reading?
The enunciation of written language.
What is elocution?
The delivery of composition.
What does elocution embrace?
Orthoepy and expression.
What is expression?
It is the manner of delivery.
What mental qualifications must a good reader possess and employ?
A good reader or speaker must have : A clear conception. A vivid imagination. Real sympathy. Good imitation. Vocal power. Artistic skill. Good judgment.
What are the physical requisites for good reading?

The physical requisites are : Distinct articulation. Full and free respiration. Perfect control of a clear, full, round, musical tone of voice. Graceful and expressive action. Cultivated taste and judgment.
What, then, does good reading demand?
Articulation, Accent, Emphasis, Inflection, Modula-tion, and Pauses.
What objects are to be aimed at in the study and teaching of reading?

The objects to be aimed at are : The acquisition of great knowledge. The acquisition of a love for reading. The improvement of the memory, judgment and taste. Improvement of the social faculties. Improvement of the health. A graceful carriage and address. A preparation for public life. The prevention and correction of improprieties.
What does orthoepy embrace?
Articulation, Syllabication, and Accent.
Define articulation.
It is the distinct utterance of the oral elements.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Reading. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is correct articulation?
Accurate and distinct utterance of the elementary sounds.
How can a good articulation be acquired?
By continued practice of the utterance of the elementary sounds, and an observance of the phonetic analysis of syllables and words.
What is Phonetic Analysis?
It is the separation of syllables and words into the elementary sounds of which they are composed.
What is an elementary sound?
It is a simple distinct sound made by the organs of speech.
What are oral elements? How produced?
They are the elementary sounds that form syllables and words, and are produced by the positions of the organs of speech in connection with the breath.
Into what classes are the elementary sounds of the English language divided?
They are divided into vocals, subvocals, and aspirates.
Define vocals. Subvocals. Aspirates.

Vocals are pure tones, and are the prominent elements of all words. Subvocals are those consonants which produce an undertone of voice when their sounds are uttered. The aspirates are mere whispers made by the organs of speech and breath.
Give a list of the most common faults in articulation.

The faults most common are: Suppression of vocal sounds in unaccented syllables. Suppression of subvocal and aspirate sounds. Incorrect articulation of vocal sounds Omission and addition of syllables. Blending the end of one word with the beginning of the next.
What is accentuation?
The act of applying accents in reading and speaking.
What is accent?
The force given to one or more syllables of a word.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Reading. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How are accented syllables designated?
They are designated by a mark thus, ('); as command'-ment.
Name and define the kinds of accent.
They are primary and secondary. The primary the more forcible stress of voice, and the secondary the less forcible.
When two syllables of a word are accented, which has the greater force?
The primary.
What are the expressions of speech? 25. What is emphasis?
Emphasis, Slur, Inflection, Modulation, Monotone, Personation, and Pauses.
What is the object of emphasis?
The object is to attract particular attention to the word or phrase upon which it is placed.
How is it accomplished?
It is usually done by an increased stress or empha-sis, but sometimes by an unusual lowering of the voice, even to a whisper.
Define slur.
It is that smooth, gliding, subdued movement of the voice used in parenthetic clauses, words contrasted or repeated, and in explanation.
How are emphatic words distinguished?
By italics, SMALL CAPITALS, and CAPITALS.
When should emphatic words be used?
When words and phrases are important in mean-ing, or when they point out a difference; when emphatic words are repeated, or when a succession of important words or phrases occurs.
How many kinds of emphasis are there?
There are two kinds: absolute and antithetic.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Reading. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is absolute emphasis?
It is used to designate the important words of a sentence, without any direct reference to other words.
Define antithetic emphasis.
It is founded on the contrast of one word or clause with another.
What are inflections?
The upward or downward slides of the voice.
How many inflections are there?
Three: the rising inflection, falling inflection, and circumflex.
What is the circumflex?
A union of the rising and falling inflections, begin-ning with the one and ending with the other.
What is a series?
A series is a number of words or phrases following one another in the same sentence.
What is a commencing series, and what a concluding series?
Where a succession of particulars occurs at the be-ginning or middle of a sentence it is called a commanding series. Where it concludes a sentence it is a concluding series.
What is the Monotone?
A sameness of tone.
What is Transition?
A change in the manner of expression.
Define Monotony.
Monotony is a frequent occurrence of the same tone, without reference to the sense.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Reading. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
When is the rising inflection used?

It is generally used when the sense is incomplete; in questions which may be answered by yes or no; when a word or sentence is repeated as a kind of interrogatory exclamation; usually in negative sentences; and in the last but one of a passage.
When is the falling inflection used?
When the sense is incomplete; when language de-mands strong emphasis; in exclamations; and in questions which cannot be answered by yes or no.
In what is the circumflex mainly used?
In the language of irony, sarcasm, and contrast.
What is modulation and its divisions?
Modulation is the variation of the voice made in reading and speaking, and is divided into pitch, force, quality, and rate.
What is pitch? How divided?

Pitch is the degree of elevation of the voice, and is divided into high, moderate, and low. High pitch is that which rises above the usual speaking key, and is used in expressing joyous and elevated feelings. Moderate pitch is that which is heard in common conversation, and is used in expressing ordinary thought and moderate emotion. Low pitch is that which falls below the usual speaking key, and is employed in expressing emotions of sublimity, awe, and reverence.
Define force. What are its divisions?

Force is the volume, or loudness of voice, and is divided into loud, moderate and gentle. Loud force is used in expressing violent passions and vehement emotions. Moderate force is a medium loudness of voice, and is employed in narrative, description, and ordinary assertion. Gentle or subdued force is employed to express fear, caution, secrecy, solemnity, and tender emotions.
Define rate. How classified?

Rate is the speed, and is divided into quick, moderate, and slow. Quick rate is used to express joy, mirth, violent anger, and sudden fear. Moderate rate is similar to moderate force. Slow rate is used to express grandeur, vastness, pathos, horror, and consternation.
What is quality, and how divided?
Quality has reference to the kinds of sound uttered, and is divided into the pure tone, orotund, aspirate, guttural, and trembling.
What is the difference between quantity and pitch?
Quantity has reference to loudness or volume of sound; pitch to the elevation or depression of a tone.
What is the difference between force and emphasis?
Force is the energy with which the whole is uttered; emphasis is the stress on a particular portion.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Reading. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is cadence?
It is the dropping of the voice at the end of the sentence, which indicates that the sense is complete.
What is stress?
Stress is the manner in which force is applied.
What is climax?
Climax is an utterance gradually increasing in intensity, and changing in pitch and movement.
Define grouping.
Grouping is that nice modulation and adaptation of the voice to the sentiment expressed which renders the utterance not only more impressive, but more pleasing to the ear.
What is pure tone?
A clear, smooth, flowing sound, with moderate pitch.
What is the orotund?
It is the pure tone deepened, enlarged, and intensified, and is adapted to the expression of the sublime and pathetic emotions.
Describe the aspirated tone.
It is an expulsion of the breath, the words being spoken in a whisper.
What is the guttural quality?
The guttural is a deep undertone, expressing hatred, contempt, loathing.
When is the trembling tone used?
The trembling is a constant waver of the voice, used to express an intense degree of suppressed excitement, or to represent the tones of enfeebled old age.
What is personation?
Changes of the voice necessary to represent two or more persons speaking.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Reading. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are pauses?
Suspensions of the voice in reading or speaking.
What are grammatical pauses?
Grammatical pauses are indicated by the punctua- tion marks.
Define rhetorical pauses.
They are suspensions of the voice which the sense requires when a grammatical pause is not admissible.
What are etymological points?
They are the points used to indicate something in regard to the formation, use, or omission of words or parts of words.
Explain the Apostrophe, Caret, Diaeresis, Marks of Quantity, Marks of Accent, Hyphen, Period.

The apostrophe (') is used to show the omission of a letter or letters; as, Sec'y. The caret (v) is used to show some omissions in writ- ing; as, come "The king is v to marshal us." The diæresis marks the separation of contiguous vowels; as, preëngage. Marks of quantity are used to show that the vowel is long or short, and is placed over the letter, ( ¯ ˘ ). The marks of accent express the tones of the voice and are the grave (`), the acute (´), and the circumflex (^). The hyphen (-), is used to separate syllables, or to unite the parts of a compound word. The period (.), is used to show the abbreviation of a word; as, lat. for latitude.
What are the points of reference?

The points of reference are used to refer the reader to some other place on the page or the book, and are as fol- lows : The Asterisk (*). The Obelisk or Dagger (†). The Double Dagger (‡). The Section (§). Parallels (||). The Paragraph (¶). If necessary, these points may be doubled.
What is suspensive quantity ?
Prolongation of the voice at the end of a word with- out making an actual pause.
What does quantity embrace ?
Force and rate.
What is the difference between enunciation and pronunciation ?
Enunciation is the utterance of words; pronuncia- tion, the mode of utterance.
What quality of voice is mostly used in reading and speaking?
The pure tone.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Reading. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What determines the proper accent of words?
General usage.
How is inflection sometimes affected by emphasis ?
It sometimes changes the falling to the rising inflection.
What is the difference between the construction of prose and poetry?
Prose pays no attention to the melodious arrangement of its words, while poetry is written with regard to the rhyme and feet of each line.
What is a parenthetic clause, and how should it be read ?
Something abruptly introduced into a sentence for the purpose of modifying, explaining, or adding to the leading proposition, and should be read in a lower voice.
When melody comes in conflict with accent, which must yield ?
Accent.
Give a principle of reading that will admit of general application.
Be sure you understand what you read, and endeavor to express the sentiments of the author as you would express the same if they were your own, and you were talking.
What are some of the essential qualities of good reading ?
To read slowly, mind the pauses, give the proper inflections, speak plainly, and read as if talking.
Give rules for the use of capitals.

Capital letters are used: To begin the first word of a sentence. To begin all proper names. To begin all titles of honor. To begin the first word of every line of poetry To begin the names of objects personified. In writing the pronoun I, and interjection O. To begin appellations of the Deity. To begin the names of the days of the week and of the month. To begin direct quotations. To begin words derived from proper names. To begin the chief words in the titles of books, headings of divisions of books, chapters, discourses, etc. To begin words of special importance

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is Physical Geography?
That science which treats of the natural divisions of water, the atmosphere, and all organic life.
Into how many classes may the objects of which Physical Geography treats, be divided?
Into Land, Water, Air, Plants and Animals.
In what condition was the earth supposed to be in a remote period of its existence?
In a state of igneous fluidity. All the elements of the earth were one molten mass, surrounded by a thick atmosphere, charged with vapors and gases
What are the proofs of this molten condition of the earth?

A direct proof is the ball-like form of the earth, which, but for its plastic condition, would not have been. Astronomy furnishes us with examples of like condition. Our sun is even now in a state of intense ignition, and the stars of the firmament are burning suns. The moon was once a fiery ball, but is now cooled, and covered with extinct craters.
How did the earth become solid?

In the course of countless ages the earth lost heat and cooled sufficiently to form a solid crust, while the interior, even now, is liquid lava, gradually losing its temperature, which in time will cause a solid earth.
What is the thickness of the earth's crust?

It is variously estimated at from fifty to one hundred miles. At the depth of 60 feet, water obtains its greatest degree of cold; at 2,200 feet the temperature rises to 90° Fahrenheit; at 8,000 feet water would boil, and as the temperature increases regularly from the surface downward, at a depth of twenty-eight miles iron would melt.
What is a simple substance?
An element that cannot be separated into other component parts.
How many elements have been discovered?
Sixty-three, and of these only fourteen have been found in a pure state, as gold, silver, copper, platinum.
How many elements mainly compose the crust of the earth?
Twelve : oxygen, silicon, aluminum, magnesium, calcium, potassium, sodium, iron, carbon, sulphur, hydro-gen, chlorine.
What is Oxygen? Silicon? Carbon? Hydrogen? Sodium? Chlorine? Sulphur? Potassium? Calcium? Magnesium? Aluminum?

Oxygen is a gas, and a constituent of all rocks, and composes about one-half by weight of the earth's crust. Silicon is the element next in abundance; combined with oxygen it forms silica or quartz, sandstone, and ordi-nary sand. Carbon is an element which, combined with oxygen, enters largely into the composition of various rocks. Char-coal and diamonds are pure carbon. Hydrogen is a gas, and combined with oxygen, consti-tutes water. Sodium is a metal, and with oxygen forms soda. It is the basis of salt. Chlorine with sodium forms common salt. Sulphur is a simple mineral substance, and united with oxygen and lime makes gypsum. Potassium is a metal which, combined with oxygen, forms potash. Calcium is a metal which, with oxygen, forms common quicklime, and with carbon limestone. Magnesium is also a metal which, combined, with oxy-gen, forms magnesia. Aluminum is a white metal, and with oxygen forms alumina, the basis of clay.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How are rocks classified?
As stratified and unstratified, the unstratified being divided into igneous and metamorphic.
What are stratified rocks?
Those which are made up of a series of layers, as sandstone, slate-rock, limestone.
How are these rocks formed into layers?
By the waters of the globe removing materials from one place and depositing them in another, as stratified sediment.
How did water originate on the earth's surface?

When the surface of the earth had become cooled, or fallen below the boiling point of water (212° Fahrenheit), the vapors of the atmosphere were condensed and gathered as water in the depressions of the earth.
What are igneous rocks?
Those which, instead of being a sedimentary de-posit, were forced in a melted condition from the burning depths below.
What are metamorphic rocks?
These rocks were at first stratified, but by the igneous lava breaking forth and flowing near them, a sub-terranean heat was produced, thus destroying the sedimen-tary or stratified condition.
What are fossils?
Petrified remains of animals or plants that lie imbedded in the rock formation of the earth's crust.
What are fossiliferous rocks? Non-fossiliferous?
Stratified rocks that contain fossils. Non-fossiliferous rocks are those without fossils, or igneous rocks.
Into how many ages does geology divide the globe?
Into seven: The Azoic Age, the Silurian Age, Devonian Age, Carboniferous Age, Reptilian Age, Mammalian Age, and the Age of Man.
What is the Azoic Age? The Silurian Age? Devonian Age? Carboniferous Age? Reptilian Age? Mammalian Age? Age of Man?

The Azoic Age is the earliest, and includes the time from the first forming of a solid crust to the appearance of animal life. Rocks of this age contain no animal fossils. The Silurian Age succeeded the Azoic, and the rocks abound in fossils of the simplest animals and plants. Star-shaped animals and animals that consist of joints or segments, as worms, make their first appearance on the earth; but the mollusks, animals like our oyster, predominate. The Devonian Age, or Age of Fishes, succeeds the Silurian. Rocks of this age contain fossils of higher and more developed animals and plants than those of the preceding age. Of fishes, the shark and gar-fish predominate. The Carboniferous Age was the Age of Vegetation. Great forests and jungles covered the land from the poles to the equator. It was a period of unceasing change. Destructive floods undermined the dense vegetation, which fell, layer upon layer, gradually changing into vast coal-fields under the pressure of the waters. The Reptilian Age is remarkable as the era of the culmination of reptiles and mollusks. Among the former were the gigantic ichthyosaurus, a marine animal with the teeth and head of a crocodile, and from ten to thirty feet long; the plesiosaurus, a huge reptile with the head and neck of a snake, and the pterodactyl, a flying reptile. In the Mammalian Age many of the lower animals and vegetable forms become extinct, and more perfect ones appear. The gar-fishes give place to the salmon, perch, and herring; the cycads among plants, to the oaks, willows, and palms. Animals of great size tread the earth, which are now known only by their remains. The Age of Man is the present era. The animal element is no longer dormant. The majority of the large beasts, that during the preceding era were the chief denizens of the world, have become extinct, and made room for smaller but higher creatures. Man, as the most highly organized being, appears latest upon the earth, which has, by means of successive changes in the past, arrived at that stage of development which renders it a fit dwelling-place for him. The earth, in this last stage, has reached a state of comparative stability. Changes take place, but they are less sweeping and less violent than those of the preceding ages.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Where are minerals found ?
The greater part lie deep in the earth, and require much labor to obtain them. Some are found in the soil and sandy beds of rivers.
Name the most important metals.
Gold, silver, platinum, mercury, iron, lead, copper, tin, nickel, zinc, and antimony.
How does the United States compare with other countries in mineral resources ?
It surpasses all others.
In precious stones ?

There are no well-defined localities where precious stones are found. Some have been found in Alaska, California, Arizona, and occasionally one picked up, apparently out of place, or lost. The largest diamond found in the United States was picked up by a workman on the banks of the James River, opposite Richmond. It weighed 24 carats.
Where is zinc found in the United States ?
It is found in the Appalachian region, particularly Pennsylvania and New Jersey; also in Wisconsin, Illinois, Missouri and Tennessee.
How much zinc is annually produced ?
The annual product is about 6,000 tons.
Where is salt found most abundantly?

It is produced most abundantly in New York and Michigan, but in a natural state Nevada has inexhaustible quantities of rock salt. In this state it can be obtained in blocks so perfectly pure and clear that a newspaper can be read through the thickness of a foot. A railroad cutting one of these immense mines graded its bed for a distance of three miles.
When was the first petroleum well sunk ?
It was sunk in 1859, in Pennsylvania, and yielded 400 gallons a day.
By what agencies are the changes of the earth's crust produced at the present time ?
By the atmosphere, the water, and by the action of the heated interior.
How does the atmosphere produce a change ?
The moisture in the atmosphere crumbles the exposed rock-surfaces, and the dusty fragments are drifted by the wind, and in the course of time deep deposits of it are formed.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How does the water produce a change ?
Rivers and creeks are everywhere at work gradually moving the whole surface of continents towards the oceans. Rivers also produce important changes by erosion.
What is erosion ?
Rivers wearing out deep channels in their courses.
Where is the greatest example of erosion?
The Canon of the Colorado River, which is a narrow chasm three hundred miles long, and with perpendicular walls of rock, from three thousand to six thousand feet in height, worn by the flowing river.
How much earthy matter is annually discharged by the Mississippi River ?

It is estimated that over 400,000,000 tons are carried into the Gulf of Mexico. This yearly discharge, if placed in one accumulation, would cover an area of ten square miles to the depth of twenty-five feet.
What are the changes which are produced by the action of the heated interior ?
Changes of level, which are slowly progressing by the century, or suddenly by the action of volcanoes and earthquakes.
Where are there illustrations of the slowly progressing change?

The northern part of the Scandinavian peninsula is slowly rising at the rate of four feet in a century, while the southern part, and Holland and Denmark, are gradually subsiding. It is by this subsidence that the greater part of Holland and Denmark is below the level of the sea, and should the dykes which protect these countries become broken, great inundation would follow.
What will eventually be the condition of the earth's surface ?

When the interior of the earth has cooled beyond the power to react and disturb its surface, the leveling of continents by the elements of nature will begin to be apparent. By the washings of rain the surface of the country is slowly moving to the bottom of oceans; by the action of the moist atmosphere rocks crumble to dust and are borne away. Though the process of decomposition is slow, yet the all-devouring element of time will tear down the mountains of earth and fill up the valleys of the seas, until land shall disappear, and the earth shall be one wide expanse of ocean.
What proportion of the whole volume of the globe is water?

About one-five-hundred-and-thirtieth part of the whole volume of the globe, or if the earth were a perfect sphere, without elevations and depressions, the volume of water would be about 10,000 feet in thickness.
What is the position of the continents ?
They lie with their masses about the Arctic and Atlantic Oceans, and narrow as they extend southward.
What proportion of the land surface do the islands form?
About one-seventeenth, or 3,000,000 square miles.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How are islands divided?
Into two classes, continental and oceanic.
What are continental islands ?
Those that lie near continents, and appear, by their position, to be detached portions.
Give illustrations of continental islands.
The West Indies, Bahamas, Aleutian, Japan, East Indies, and many others.
What are oceanic islands ?
Such as lie far removed from any continent. Nearly all of the islands of the Pacific Ocean belong to this class.
What two classes of oceanic islands ?
They are mainly of two kinds, the volcanic and coral.
How do continental islands compare in size with oceanic islands ?
Continental islands, as a rule, are larger than oceanic islands.
What is the difference in the rock material of the two classes of islands ?

The continental islands have the same formation as of the mainland, being composed of sandstone, slate, granite, and the various metamorphic rocks, while the volcanic islands are composed of volcanic substances, or of limestone.
What is coral? Where found ?
It is the limy secretion or deposit of small marine animals that lie together in vast numbers. It is found in tropical seas, or where the temperature of the water is not below 68° (Fahrenheit).
Into how many classes are coral formations divided ?
Four : fringing reefs, barrier reefs, encircling reefs, and atolls, or coral islands.
What are fringing reefs ?
Lines or ribbons of coral near the shores.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are barrier reefs ? Encircling reefs ?

They lie parallel to the shore, but are separated from it by a channel of water of considerable depth and varying greatly in width. The grandest example of coral formation known is the Great Australian barrier reef. Externally it rises, with little inclination, from a fathomless ocean—stretches upwards of 1,000 miles along the north-east shore—varies in breadth from two hundred yards to a mile, and in distance from the shore from twenty to seventy miles. There are many openings through the reef, by which vessels enter the interior ocean, which is everywhere safely navigable. An encircling reef is a barrier reef which encloses one or more islands.
What are atolls, or coral islands ?
They consist of a low, narrow rim of reef surrounding a lagoon.
What is a lagoon ?
The vacant place enclosed by atolls or coral islands.
What is the theory of the circular formation of atolls?

It is based upon a regular and gradual subsidence of the bed of the ocean. A fringing reef is first formed near the shore of an island, which becomes an encircling reef, and as the ocean bed subsides, the coral deposits still continue to grow upward until by the subsidence the interior island disappears, and the atoll alone remains.
Describe the common forms of coral islands.
Though of a great variety of shapes, they agree in one particular, viz.: They consist of a low narrow rim of coral rock, enclosing a body of water called a lagoon.
Upon what are coral islands built?

As the reef-building polyps do not live below the depth of 100 or 120 feet, they select for a foundation the submarine mountains and plateaus, or the slopes of volcanic cones, which come near the surface.
Where are these islands found, and why are they confined to these regions?

They are found in tropical waters where they are protected from cold ocean currents, from the influence of fresh river waters, and are remote from active volcanoes. They are not found where the mean annual temperature of the water falls below 68° Fahr.
Into how many great classes may we divide the surface of the earth?
Into three classes : mountains, tablelands or plateaus, and lowlands.
Where is found the highest elevation of the earth? The lowest depression?
The Himalayas are the highest elevation, rising 29,000 feet above the level of the sea. The lowest depression is the Dead Sea, in Palestine, which lies 1,300 feet below the level of the ocean.
What is trend?
The direction of a mountain chain.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What constitutes a relief of a country?
The shape, its elevation, plains, tablelands, mountains, and valleys.
What two forms of relief exist?
Elevations as a whole and linear elevations.
What influence has the relief of a country upon its climate, life and importance?

As an elevation of 330 feet produces a difference of temperature of 1° Fahr., then the altitude of but a few thousand feet changes the character of a region, like a removal of it from torrid to temperate latitudes, or from temperate to frigid. The relief also controls the drainage of a continent, and influences to a certain extent the direction and character of the winds, and the distribution of rain.
What elevation is given as necessary to become tablelands?
Above an elevation of 1,000 feet. Low plains are below 1,000 feet.
Where are the great plateaus of the world?

Mean elevation in feet. The Plateau of Thibet 14,000 The Bolivian Plateau, in the Andes 13,000 Plateau of Anahuac, in Mexico 8,000 Arabian Plateau 7,000 to 8,000 Colorado Plateau 7,000 The Great Basin 4,000 to 5,000 The Sahara 2,000 to 3,000 Southern Africa 3,000 to 5,000 Central Asiatic Plateau 2,000 to 4,000
Where are the great low plains of the world?

In the Western Continent there is the Mississippi Plain, the Arctic Plain, Atlantic Coast Plain, Llanos of the Orinoco, Selvas of the Amazon, and the Pampas of the La Plata. In Europe there is the Great Central Germanic Plain. In Asia, there is the Siberian Plain, the Plain of Turkistan, the Chinese Plain, and the Mesopotamian Plain. Africa has no great low plains.
Into how many classes are mountains divided?
Into three: those rising to an elevation of 20,000 feet or upwards ; those between 10,000 and 20,000 feet, and those between 2,000 and 10,000 feet
How are mountains supposed to have been formed?
By the pressure of the inner forces of the earth.
In the manner of formation, what two kinds of mountains do we find?
Mountains by folding, which are generally of moderate elevation ; and mountains by fracture, to which belong the highest chains of the globe.
What is a longitudinal valley? A traverse valley?

Longitudinal valleys are those which separate parallel ridges of mountain chains, and extend in the same direction. Transverse valleys cut the ridges at right angles, and extend in an opposite direction.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are continental axes?
The great dividing ridges of a continent. The sub-divisions are called secondary axes.
How many mountain systems in America?
Six: the Rocky Mountains, Californian, Alleghanian, Andean, Parime, and Brazilian.
What is the extent of the Rocky system?
This system, including the Californian system, occupies the whole western part of the continent, extending from the Isthmus of Panama to the Arctic Ocean, a distance of 5,700 miles.
Describe the Alleghany system.

It extends in a southwesterly direction from the shores of the Gulf of St. Lawrence to the northern part of Georgia and Alabama. This system consists of many parallel chains, varying in height from 1,000 to 3,000 feet, enclosing fertile valleys.
Describe the plateau of North America. How may this region be divided?

It extends from the 50th parallel of north latitude, between the Rocky Mountains and the coast range of the Pacific, through the central part of Mexico, to the Isthmus of Panama, and may be divided into the Great Basin of Utah, the Great Mexican Plateau, and the tablelands of Central America.
How may the plateaus of South America be divided?
The Great Plateau of the Andes; the elevated plains of Quito, Bogota, and Popayan; and the tableland of Brazil.
Describe the plateau of the Andes.
It is an extensive tract of lofty tableland, stretching along the top of the Andes between the parallels of 15° and 3° south latitude, with an elevation of nearly 13,000 feet.
Into what divisions are the plateaus of Asia divided?
They are divided into Central, Southern and South-western Asia.
What does the central tableland of Asia embrace?
The great desert of Gobi, and the tableland of Thibet.
What tablelands does Southern Asia embrace?
Those of Hindoostan, Iran, Asia Minor, and Arabia.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are the known tablelands of Africa?
Those of Abyssinia and South Africa.
Describe the principal plateau of Europe.

It is the Spanish peninsula, the whole central part of which consists of a series of lofty plains, divided from each other by parallel mountain chains. This plateau comprises 93,000 square miles, or nearly one-half of the peninsula.
Where is the great plain of North America?
It extends from the Arctic Ocean to the Gulf of Mexico, and from the Rocky Mountains to the Alleghanies, and is divided about midway into a northern and southern slope.

What does the great plain of South America comprise? How is it divided? (a) What are llanos, selvas, pampas, and wastes? (b) What are steppes, heaths, lands, and where found? (c) What is a plain? Plateau, or tableland? (See Questions in Geography Nos. 227, 228, 229, 230, 231.)
The entire peninsula east of the Andes, except the mountain systems and the Brazilian tableland, and is divided vided into the llanos, selvas, pampas, and wastes of Pata-gonia.
What is the boundary of the great plain of the Eastern Continent ?
It extends from the Bay of Biscay and the North Sea, to Behring Strait.
What are some of the chief points in the physical geography of Siberia ?

It contains a great plain, extending from the foot of the Ural Mountains to the Pacific Ocean, and from the Altai Mountains to the Arctic Ocean. This plain, near the Arctic, is a barren and desolate region, and the cold is so intense that the spongy soil is perpetually frozen to the depth of several hundred feet. Near the Irtysh River, the soil is rich and pasturage good, but there are few inhab-itants.
What are the principal known plains of Africa ? (a) Describe the Desert of Sahara.
Sahara, the plains of Egypt, Central Africa, and the region of Zambezi.
Of what is the interior of Australia supposed to consist ?
Of vast, barren plains.
What resemblance between North and South America ?
Each have a predominant mountain system in the west, two secondary systems in the east, and vast, low plains intervening.
What general law of relief has been observed from a study of the continents ?
That all of the long, gentle slopes descend to the Atlantic and Arctic Oceans, while the short and rapid slopes are directed towards the Pacific and Indian Oceans.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Where are the land masses of the globe located ?

They are crowded around the North Pole, their northern limit being about the 70th parallel. As they extend south they gradually diminish in area, until they terminate in points widely separated from each other.
What is the common form of the land masses ?
With the exception of Australia, they are more or less triangular.
What difference between the Eastern and Western Continents in the direction of their length ?
The western continent extends north and south, while the eastern has its greatest length somewhat opposite.
What difference in other respects result from this ?
The American continent extends about 9,000 miles and traverses all the climatic zones. Asia-Europe also extends about the same length, and has a general similarity of climate, vegetation and animals.
In outline, what is the difference?
They exhibit striking differences. Some are deeply indented with gulfs and inland seas, while others are almost void of these indentations.
What influence has the articulation of the coast upon a country and its people?

The greater the indentations of a country the more moderate are the extremes of temperature, and the more convenient for the development of the industries of man-kind. The climate is subdued, and the enlightenment of man becomes but the natural tendency of nature.
What fact is true of well articulated countries ?
They become the abode of the most highly civilized nations.
How do the continents compare in this respect ?

Europe, Asia, and North America are most indented and its people are the most industrious and enlightened. Africa and South America have few indentations and the people are less ambitious and enterprising.
From what do volcanic phenomena result? How are these phenomena divided ?
From the reaction of the heated interior upon the solid crust. These phenomena are divided into three classes: volcanoes, earthquakes, and a rising or subsidence of portions of the earth's surface.
What is a volcano?
A mountain which emits fire, smoke, and lava.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How are volcanoes classified ?
As active, intermittent, and extinct.
What are intermittent volcanoes ?
Those having periods of rest.
What is the crater of a volcano ? Lava?
It is the opening, or several openings, of a volcano. Lava is the matter thrown from a volcano.
What is the width of craters ?
They differ greatly in size, the larger varying from 2,000 to 18,000 feet.
Where may the crater be located ?
They are sometimes on the sides.
Where is the largest known crater in the world ?

It is that of Mauna Loa, on the Island of Hawaii, 4,270 feet high. This crater is over three miles long and one mile wide, and from 600 to 1,000 feet deep. Large pools of boiling lava may be seen from the brink.
Into what classes are volcanoes divided?
Two: the central and lineal systems.
What is the central ? The lineal ?

The central consists of several vents grouped together, one of which usually serves as a common point of eruption. The lineal consists of several vents extending in one direction, at no great distance from each other, forming, as it were, chimneys along an extended fissure.
Where are volcanoes most numerous ?
On the islands and shores of the Pacific Ocean.
How many volcanoes have been enumerated?
Humboldt has enumerated 225 still in action, and the number of extinct volcanoes is much greater.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the peculiarity in the distribution of volcanoes ?
Nearly all of the volcanoes are situated along the mountain ranges which are near the shores of the continents, while the interior is almost destitute of them.
Explain this fact.
Volcanoes appear where the earth's crust is weakest, and these mountain chains are a line of fracture formed by the gradual separation of the ocean's bed from the coasts of the continent.
Locate the two volcanic zones which encircle the earth ?

One is the vast array of mountain chains, or continents and islands, which encircle the Pacific Ocean; the other, though less continuous, is a belt of broken lands and inland seas, which, extending around the globe, separates the northern from the southern continents.
What theory is advanced as the cause of volcanoes ?
That the cooling of the earth's surface produces an eruption or vent that is seen in the freezing of a barrel of water. Volcanoes are the outlet of this expansion.
What are some of the volcanic phenomena ?
Geysers, mud volcanoes, and fields of fire.
What are mud volcanoes? Where found ?
Miniature volcanoes which emit steam and mud, but never molten rocks. They are found in large numbers in Sicily, and on the shores of the Caspian Sea, near the base of the Caucasus Mountains.
What are fields of fire ? Where found ?

Regions where columns of fire break forth from the ground. These fires are classed with volcanic phenomena, although they may have their origin from the connection of the internal heat with large quantities of gases which accumulate in the crevices of rocks and burst forth in flames. They are found to the greatest extent west of the Caspian Sea.
What is a mountain pass ?
A depression or valley crossing a mountain chain, through which a road is formed for traveling.
What are earthquakes, and where confined?
Convulsions of the earth, and are most common in volcanic districts.
What are the movements of earthquakes ?
Horizontal, vertical, and rotary.
