The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the theory of earthquakes ?

That the convulsions are the results of the heated interior seeking vent, and may be caused by the collection of steam and gases, or like congealed water, the crust of the earth may contain the property of expanding during its process of cooling, thus forming a pressure which is resisted by violent agitations to obtain relief. The movements of earthquakes appear to be on the principle that waves recede when any substance is thrown into the water; thus, when convulsions occur great waves of the fluid interior recede, by which the crust is uplifted and more or less violently shaken.
What is the dynamic force of earthquakes ?

They vary according to their power. The terrible earthquake of Lisbon, November 1st, 1755, in which 60,000 persons lost their lives, disturbed an area of 7,500,000 square miles, or, as the earth's crust is about twenty-five miles in thickness, 187,500,000 cubic miles of matter were moved.
What is the duration?
Some of the most destructive lasted but a few seconds. That of Lisbon produced its terrible effects in less than five minutes.
What connection exists between volcanoes and earthquakes ?
Volcanoes are supposed to be outlets of the pressure of the subterranean forces, while earthquakes are the forces seeking vent.
What relation exists between earthquakes and atmospheric conditions ?

In the tropics, especially, earthquakes are most frequent in that part of the year in which the greatest atmospheric disturbances take place. They are most dreaded at the beginning of the rainy season, when the monsoons are changing directions.
What noted volcanic action occurred A. D. 79 ?
A terrific eruption of Mt. Vesuvius destroyed the cities of Pompeii, Herculaneum, and Stabiae, and covered them with ashes and cinders to the depth of fifteen feet.
How many eruptions of Mt. Ætna are recorded ?

About sixty of its eruptions are recorded. In 1669, a stream of lava from this mountain overflowed the ramparts of Catana, sixty feet in height, and destroyed a portion of the city. In 1832, several craters opened in the sides, and a stream of lava eighteen miles long, one mile broad, and thirty feet deep, poured over the adjacent fields.
Describe the eruption of Casequina in 1835.

In 1835, a terrible eruption occurred. It lasted three days, during which the light of the sun was obscured over half of Central America; more than 40,000 square miles are said to have been covered with dust, ashes, and lava.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How many lives are estimated as having been lost by earthquakes ?
It is estimated that 13,000,000 human beings have lost their lives by these convulsions of nature, but the estimate has no solid foundation for statistics.
By what signs is an earthquake usually preceded ?

At times the electrical condition of the air seems to be changed and felt by man and beast—the former experiencing oppression and dizziness; the latter uttering cries of distress and running wildly about. At these times the atmosphere is unusually still and hazy, and the sun seen through it looks like a ball of fire.
For what was the year 1868 remarkable ?

One of the most terrible earthquakes on record occurred in South America, in August, 1868. Its center of activity seems to have been in Arica, a seaport of Peru, which was completely destroyed. The shock extended through the whole eastern shore of South America, and numerous towns and cities were laid in ruins. Probably 50,000 people lost their lives.
Give an account of the earthquake at New Madrid.

The most remarkable earthquake that has occurred in the United States is that of New Madrid, on the Mississippi River, in 1811-12. For several months there was an incessant quaking of the ground, which for a distance of 300 miles rose and sank in undulations. The most of the town was submerged.
Of what does hydrography treat ?
The waters upon the earth.
What are springs, and how may they be classified ?
Fountains of waters which flow from reservoirs underneath the ground, and are classified as perennial or constant, intermitting, and periodical.
What are perennial springs ? Intermittent ? Periodical ?
Perennial springs are those which continue to flow at all seasons. Intermittent are those that alternately flow and stop. Periodical depend upon the prevailing character of the seasons.
How are springs produced ?

They depend upon the peculiar arrangement of the pervious and impervious stratification. The water penetrates the pervious stratum and sinks to the impervious, where, if the stratification is tilted, the water will follow this layer until an outlet is found. Should the pervious stratum lie between two convex layers of impervious matter, springs could be obtained at any place where the height is less than the highest points of the pervious stratum, by breaking through, whereby the water would rise to the surface and flow.
Where are springs most numerous ?
In and around mountainous regions.
Upon what does the temperature of springs depend ?

It depends much upon the strata through which the water passes. Some are icy cold, particularly in mountain regions. Others coming from great depths are warm, hot, and even boiling. This heat, in some places, is due to volcanic action, and in some instances to chemical action.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are artesian wells ?
Artificial springs formed by boring to the pervious stratum, as explained above.
What is bifurcation ?

When a river is so situated that it may divide and flow to two distinct river basins. The best known example is the Cassiquiare River, in Venezuela, which connects through its bifurcation the river systems of the Amazon and Orinoco rivers.
What is the composition of water ?
It is composed of two gases, oxygen and hydrogen, chemically combined in the ratio, by weight of eight to one.
What office do the waters of the earth perform ?
It sustains vegetable and animal life, and in a large measure was the principal agent in shaping what is now the solid land.
What important peculiarity does water possess ?
Water contracts in volume with a falling of temperature, until reduced to 39.2° Fahr., where its density is greatest. Below this temperature it expands.
What is the great reservoir of water ?
The great reservoir is the sea.
How are the waters dispersed over the earth ?
They are raised by evaporation and spread over the earth in the form of rain.
What are the sources of springs ?

A spring is the outlet of an underground current of water which has for its source some reservoir or supply higher than where the spring comes from the ground. It is this pressure which produces the flow.
Into what classes are rivers divided? Describe them.
Into oceanic and continental. The oceanic are those that flow into the ocean, and are divided into five classes, one for each ocean. The continental are those which never reach the ocean.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are canyons ? (a) What is the basin of a river ? A watershed ?
A deep gorge, ravine or gulch between high and steep banks, worn by water-courses.
On what does the amount of water in a basin depend ?
Upon the extent of the territory which it drains; the rains; the physical features of the country—a well-wooded country impeding evaporation; and the climate.
What is an avalanche ?
A large mass of snow, ice and earth, sliding or rolling down a mountain.
What is a confluence ?
The junction of two or more rivers.
Into what divisions may the United States be divided with reference to its drainage ?
The St. Lawrence basin, Atlantic slope, Mississippi valley, Texas slope, Pacific slope, inland basin of Utah, and the Red River basin.
What are the divisions of Europe?
There are three classes : the Atlantic basin, the Arctic slope, and the inland basin of the Caspian Sea.
What is a singular fact in regard to the watershed of Europe which separates the three systems of drainage ?
They are entirely within the low plains of Russia.
Where do the largest streams of Africa flow ? Of Asia?
Into the Atlantic Ocean.
Why do larger streams flow into the Atlantic than into the Pacific?

The position of the several grand divisions partially enclose, and slope to the Atlantic, while mountain chains which form the divides between the river basins of these oceans are near the shores of the Pacific.
How are lakes divided? Describe each class.

Into four classes : first, those which receive no streams and have no outlets; second, those which receive no streams, but have an outlet; third, those which receive streams, but have no outlet ; fourth, those which both receive and discharge streams.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Why are most inland waters fresh ?

As rivers and lakes are formed by rain or snow they are fresh because in the evaporation of water the impurities are left behind. Salt lakes have no outlet which retains all of the salt which flows into them.
What name is given to lakes without an outlet?
Steppe lakes.
Where are the principal steppe lakes ?
Great Salt Lake of the United States, Lake Titicaca of South America, the Caspian Sea, Lake Tchad and Nagami of Africa, and numerous smaller ones throughout Central Asia.
Describe the Caspian Sea.
It is over 176,000 square miles in extent, and 2,957 feet deep; its surface lies eighty-four feet below the level of the ocean, and is slowly diminishing in size. Several large rivers flow into it.
Describe the most extensive lake region of the globe.

This region is in North America, and sweeps in the shape of a broad belt around Hudson Bay, from Labrador to the shores of the Arctic Ocean. The lakes of Canada, those in the New England States, New York, Minnesota, the great northern lakes, and the many lakes in the great northern plain east of the Rocky Mountains, belong to this region.
What is said of the Great Lakes ? How large are they?

These lakes have been estimated to contain one-half of all the fresh water of the globe. Lake Superior contains about 32,000 square miles ; Lake Michigan about 22,400; Lake Huron, 21,000; Lake Erie, 10,815; Lake Ontario, 6,300.
Describe the lake regions of Europe.

In Europe there are two lake regions. The one surrounds the Baltic Sea and its branches. To it belong the countless lakes of Finland, the great Ladogo and Onega lakes of Russia, the numerous small lakes in Northern Germany, and those of Denmark and Sweden. The other lake region is that of the Alps, containing 426 lakes, the largest of which lie along the base of this mountain system.
What are the Tundras ?

They are the most extensive swamps on the globe, and belong to Northern Russia and Siberia. These swamps are frozen to a great depth, and thawing during the summer months at the surface only, are covered with a dense covering of mosses.
What place have lakes in the economy of nature ?

They form reservoirs, which, receiving the surplus waters in time of freshets, equalizes the flow of rivers and prevent destructive inundations. In their basins the wild mountain torrents find rest and the muddy waters deposit their sediment, and flow out pure and transparent with a gentle current.
What is the cause of the saltness of some lakes ?
Because they have no outlets to carry off the saltness which is continually washed from the earth by rains or contact with beds of salt.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the color of the ocean ? Depth? Transparency ?

The color is generally a deep, bluish green, which becomes clearer near the coast. The depth depends upon the formation of the bottom, which, like the land, is diversified by mountains and valleys, high and low plains. The greatest depth in the region of the Atlantic Telegraph Cable is about 12,700 feet; in the South Atlantic, 25,000 feet; in the Pacific, over 43,000 feet. The clearness and transparency vary in different localities. In the Arctic Ocean shells and animals are distinguishable at a depth of 500 feet; around the Lesser Antilles objects are distinctly seen 150 feet below the surface. Sea-water, in general, is more transparent than fresh water.
Which is heavier, fresh or sea-water? At what temperature will sea-water freeze ?
By the presence of mineral substances, chiefly salt, sea-water becomes heavier. Fresh water congeals at 32° Fahrenheit, seawater at 28½°.
What causes the phosphorescence of the sea ?
It is due to myriads of minute animalcules which inhabit the water.
What is constantly going on in the ocean?
The great body of the ocean is never at rest; not only is the surface agitated by the winds, but even at great depths the water moves from one region to another.
How many, and where are the great mediterranean seas? (a) What is a mediterranean sea ?
There are four: The Gulf of Mexico, Hudson Bay, Mediterranean Sea, and the Baltic Sea, with the Gulfs of Bothnia and Finland.
Name the principal border seas. What separates these seas from the ocean ?
Caribbean Sea, Gulf of St. Lawrence, North Sea, Behring Sea, Sea of Okhotsk, and the Japan, Yellow, and China seas.
What is the width and velocity of the Gulf stream ?
It is from 25 to 150 miles wide, and moves with a velocity of from 1½ to 5 miles an hour.
What is the color of the Gulf stream ?
A deep indigo blue, strikingly different from the green of the surrounding ocean.
What influence has this stream on the climate of Western Europe ?
A branch of this stream flows by the shores of Western Europe, and being warm, tempers the winds which blow over it, making the climate mild and genial.
Describe the equatorial current of the Atlantic? Of the Pacific?

The Equatorial current crosses the Atlantic from the coast of Africa to Cape St. Roque, where it divides into two branches, a northwesterly and a southwesterly, each of which follow the South American coast—the former making its way into the Caribbean Sea and the Gulf of Mexico, where it takes the name of Gulf Stream. In the Pacific two Equatorial currents are found—one north and the other south of the equator. The former, turning to the northeast near the Loo Choo Islands, continues under the name of the Japan current. The south Equatorial current of the Pacific consists of two branches, one of which, striking the Australian coast, divides into a northwesterly and a southwesterly branch, as does the Atlantic current on the coast of Brazil; the other, farther north, turns near the Caroline Islands, and reverses its course, crossing the Pacific again in a direction nearly due east to South America, under the name of the Equatorial counter-current.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Describe the Japan current.

The Japan current is the north branch of the Equatorial current, and bears a remarkable resemblance to the Gulf Stream. Its course is through the China Sea along the Asiatic Island chains, in a northeasterly direction toward the Aleutian Islands and Alaska, the climate of which it improves, as the Gulf Stream does that of Northwestern Europe. Between the Japan current and the main land a cold current sets in the opposite direction, as in the case of the Gulf Stream.
Describe the Arctic currents.

From the Arctic Ocean two cold currents flow to the south, one on each side of Greenland. Uniting at the mouth of Davis Strait, they continue their southerly course as far as Newfoundland, where part of this broad Arctic River, as an under-current, flows beneath the Gulf Stream; and the rest, as a surface-current, keeps inside of this stream, close to the American shore as far south as Florida. It is this Arctic current which chills Labrador and makes it a desolate region.
Mention the Antarctic currents.

There are three currents—one which flows into the Atlantic in a northwesterly direction along the coast of Africa, joining the Equatorial current near the Gulf of Guinea; one flows into the Pacific along the South American shore under the name of the Peruvian or Humboldt current, and with the Equatorial west of the Gulf of Guayaquil; one flows into the Indian Ocean along the western shore of Australia and unites with the Equatorial just north of the Tropic of Capricorn.

What are whirlpools ? (a) What are three distinct movements of the ocean ? (b) What are tides? Neap tides? Spring tides? Flood tides? Ebb tides? (c) What are the chief causes of the tides ? (d) What are waves? Breakers? How high do waves roll?
Circular currents, which occur in certain localities and are caused by the action of the tides flowing against a peculiarly shaped shore or island.
What causes the advance of the waves ?

It is a movement communicated from one portion of the sea to another following in a line of succession. In shallow water, or the shore, these movements are broken and the last motion throws the water as if it came in the form of a current.
To what depth is the sea agitated by waves ?
Experiments show an agitation from 450 to 620 feet.
What is the height of tides ?

The great tidal-wave rises less than three feet in mid-ocean, but when it strikes against the shores of continents its altitude is much greater, varying according to the formation of the inlets, sometimes rising as high as seventy feet.
Describe the great tidal wave.

This vast tidal swell or wave, probably originates southeast of Australia, and, like other waves, it is not an onward flow of water, except over shoals and near the land. The reason assigned for giving this point as the place of commencement, is, the position of the continents. Beyond this commencement extends the great ocean, which consists of the Antarctic, Pacific and Indian Oceans. Over this vast water-area the flood-tide journeys westward around the earth, sweeping up against the African and Asiatic coasts into the Atlantic Ocean, and around Cape Horn into the Pacific Ocean.
What are ocean currents ? How are they divided?

They are like vast rivers, transporting its waters from one part to another, and are caused by the heat of the sun, rotation of the earth, the saltness of the sea, by winds, tides, and melting ice. They are divided into constant, periodical and temporary.
What is the cause of currents ?

The winds, the rotation of the earth, the excessive evaporation of the ocean in the tropics, and the Polar currents which flow to replace that evaporation. The waters of the Polar under-currents are heavier, because of their temperature, and in flowing into the tropics force the warmer waters to flow away as surface-currents.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are temporary currents ?
Those which continue for a limited time, and are caused by tides, melting ice, or gales of considerable duration.
What are periodical currents ?
Such as flow at regular intervals, and are occasioned by tides, and by periodical winds called monsoons.
What are constant currents ?

Those which flow throughout the entire year in the same direction, and have their origin in permanent causes. There are two kinds: Polar currents, flowing from the Poles to the equator, and Equatorial currents, flowing from east to west near the equator. The origin of constant currents may depend upon the rotation of the earth and enormous evaporation in the equatorial regions. By the evaporation currents are formed from beyond the equator to supply the loss, and by the rotation of the earth from west to east, the waters at the equator, where the velocity of the earth is greatest, are inclined to flow or slide to the west, which produce currents in an opposite direction from the motion of the earth.
What is a counter current? Where are they found ?
A stream which runs by the side of, or beneath another current, and in an opposite direction.
To what three classes of currents does difference of temperature give rise ?
Polar, Equatorial, and Return Currents.
What modifies the direction of the polar and return currents ?

If acted upon by no external forces, they would move in a direct line between the Poles and the Equator, but the rotation of the earth causes the Polar currents to tend towards the west and the Return currents towards the east. The prevailing winds also modify these directions, and the basins of the several oceans somewhat shape their course.
How are ocean basins divided ?

The Atlantic and Pacific Oceans are subdivided, each having a northern and a southern basin; the Indian Ocean has only a southern basin; the Arctic is properly a continuation of the Atlantic; the Antarctic, also, is not properly a separate ocean but is the common center from which the three great basins radiate.
Describe the Pacific basin.
The Pacific is oval in outline and broadly open at the south, but is nearly closed at the north.
Describe the Atlantic basin.

The Atlantic basin has been likened by Humboldt to a long valley, with approximately parallel sides. This is the only basin widely open at the north and, stretching from pole to pole, it forms the only complete channel for the interchange of polar and equatorial waters.
Wherein do the ocean basins present great differences ?
In regard to the position and character of the branches, by which the coasts of the continents are indented.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How are coast waters classified ?
According to their forms, they are classified as inland seas, border seas, and gulf or bays.
What is known of the ocean bed ?

Numerous soundings have given an approximate idea of the ocean beds, especially of the Atlantic Ocean, the Mediterranean Sea, the Indian Ocean, and the Red Sea. In the Pacific these soundings are somewhat limited.
How are the depths estimated in the absence of soundings ?
From the velocity of the tide-wave and earthquake waves. These waves depend upon the depth of the basin in which they move.
Of what does meteorology treat ?
Of the atmosphere, temperature, winds, and moisture (dews, fogs, rain, snow, and hail).
What is the composition of air ?
It is composed of three gases, in the following proportion: Nitrogen, 76.84; oxygen, 23.1; carbonic acid, 0.06.
What is the depth of the atmosphere ?
Estimates based upon the pressure of the atmosphere in high and low localities, place the depth at 45 to 50 miles.
How does air compare with water in weight ?
Experiments prove that air is 815 times lighter than water.
To what is air essential ?
To the transmission of sound, to combustion, and to life.
What is temperature ?
The quantity of sensible heat which a body possesses, as indicated by a thermometer.
On what does the temperature of a place depend ?
The direction of the sun's rays, its vicinity to the sea, the prevailing winds to which it is exposed, and its elevation above the sea.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the depth of the limit of invariable temperature in the several zones ?
In the tropical zone the limit is but one foot from the surface; in the temperate zone it is from sixty to seventy feet; and in the frigid zone it is about fifty.
What is the depth of the limit of invariable temperature in the ocean ?
In the tropical zone, it is probably about 7,200 feet; 60° north and 56° south latitude, it reaches the surface, and in 70° about 4,500 feet.
What is the mean temperature of a place ?
The average amount of heat a place receives during the year.
Does the mean temperature of a place vary one year with another?
The variation is but little. The variable produce of our harvests is owing more to the change in distribution of heat through the different months, than to any difference in the annual supply.
What are isothermal lines ?
Lines which are drawn through all places, which have nearly the same mean annual temperature.
Into how many zones of climate may the surface of the earth be divided by the isothermal lines ?
Six: the torrid, hot, warm, temperate, cold, and frigid.
What is wind ? How produced ?
Air in motion. It is produced by the different amount of heat which the atmosphere receives in different localities.
To what region must we look for the cause of the circulation of the winds of the globe?
To the equatorial regions.
Explain the circulation of the atmosphere. 217. What are trade-winds ?

Within the tropical regions the air is heated. It expands, becomes lighter, and rises. From both sides the colder air rushes in to restore the disturbed equilibrium, thus producing two lateral currents, one from the north, and the other from the south. But the air drawn by these currents must be restored, else there would be an accumulation of atmosphere in these regions. This is effected by the ascending current, which, on reaching a certain elevation, is pressed outward, forming two return currents, one flowing to the north, the other to the south. It is by these currents, one ascending current, two lateral Polar currents, and two returning Equatorial currents, that the general circulation is effected.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Why do these currents become distinct winds at 30° of latitude?

The Equatorial currents, which in the tropics flow high above the Polar currents, become chilled in their transit through the upper regions of the atmosphere, and have a tendency to descend and intermingle with the Polar currents, which replace each other successively, thus preventing constant currents above that latitude.
In what direction do the trade-winds blow ? Why ?

They are northeast winds in the northern hemisphere, and southeast winds in the southern hemisphere. The direction is due to the rotation of the earth, the velocity of the surface of the earth being greater as the Polar currents approach the equator, which cannot acquire, suddenly, the same velocity, and the earth moves under it, and the trades, in the northern hemisphere, by this reason blow from the northeast, instead of coming directly from the north. By a similar reason the Equatorial currents flow in an opposite direction. As these currents commence to flow from the equator, they acquire the equatorial velocity, which cannot, suddenly, conform with the decreased velocity of the earth's surface as they approach the Poles, but unlike the trades, which lag behind, they have acquired a velocity and flow beyond the meridian from which they started.
Name some periodical and variable winds.
The marked periodical winds are Monsoons, and Land and Sea Breezes. The variables are Whirlwinds and Hurricanes, Typhoons or Cyclones.
How many wind-zones are there ?
Seven: the zone of calms, the two zones of the trade-winds, the two sub-tropical zones, and the two zones of the variable winds.
Where is the zone of calms ?

It extends from 1½° to 11° north latitude. This place has the greatest mean annual temperature, consequently it is where the trade-winds cease, or rather, where they rise and flow back again as Equatorial currents, thus producing a series of calms.
Where are the sub-tropical zones ?
Those belts which lie between the summer and winter limits of the trade-zones. The trades prevail here during the summer, but in winter they recede, and the variable winds prevail.
Where are the zones of variable winds ?
They extend from the limits of the sub-tropical zones to the Poles.
What are monsoons ?

Trade winds whose direction has been modified by the unequal heating of the land and sea. The name was originally restricted to periodical winds which blow in the Indian Ocean, blowing part of the year in one direction, and part in the opposite.
Where are the principal monsoon regions ?
In the Indian Ocean, along the coast of Guinea, and in the Caribbean Sea and Gulf of Mexico.
Describe the monsoons of the Indian Ocean.

During the cooler months of the year, the monsoons are the regular northeast trade-winds, but as the sun crosses the Equator, and the continent of Asia becomes intensely heated, an ascending current results, and the trades are deflected and rush to the continent to restore the equilibrium, thus turning the trades in almost an opposite direction. A similar deviation is also produced south of the Equator when Australia becomes heated.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the cause of the monsoons of the Caribbean Sea ?
The northeastern trades are deflected by the overheated Mississippi valley, and like the trades of the Indian Ocean, they are bent to supply the demand on the continent.
What are the "Northers" of Texas and Mexico ?
Violent winds which sweep over the prairies of Texas and plains of Mexico. They prevail from October to March, and are considered as winter monsoons.
What are land and sea breezes ?

Those winds which blow in summer from the sea to the land during the greater part of the day, and from the land to the sea at night. They are caused by the different temperature of the land and water, the land being warmer than the water in the day, and colder at night.
Where are the Etesian winds ? To what do they owe their origin ?
They are periodicals which blow on the Mediterranean Sea in summer, and owe their origin to the Sahara desert.
What are the most noted hot winds, and where do they prevail ?

The simoon, khamsin, harmattan, sirocco, and the solano. The simoon prevails on the deserts of Arabia, Nubia, Persia, and Syria; the khamsin blows in Egypt, but is not so oppressive as the simoon; the sirocco is a well-known hot wind of Greece and Italy, and the solano of Spain, which owe their origin to the vicinity of the Sahara. 233. The pamperos and bora. The pamperos are cold southwest winds which originate among the snows of the Andes, and sweep with great violence over the level pampas of South America. The bora is a northeast wind, common on the eastern shores of the Gulf of Venice.
What are whirlwinds? Waterspouts? Sand-pillars?

A whirlwind is the meeting of two winds at an angle. Waterspouts are whirlings occurring on the sea or on lakes. They first appear in the form of an inverted cone attached to a dark cloud. The cone swings backward and forward, and gradually approaches the water, which becomes violently agitated. The whirling eddy draws up masses of spray, which unite with the descending cone. When fully formed, they appear as tall pillars of cloud of sombre grey, stretching from the sea to the sky, whirling around an axis. They continue but for a short time, when the column breaks, and rain often descends from the cloud above. The drops of water forming this rain are never salt, as would be the case were they carried up from the ocean. They must, therefore, have been derived from the clouds, as in ordinary rain. Sand-pillars are produced by whirlwinds on a desert where columns of sand are drawn up in a body.
How are the tropical storms variously named ?
As hurricanes, typhoons, cyclones, and tornadoes.
Where are the three principal hurricane regions ?
In the West Indies, Indian Ocean, and the Chinese Sea. The wind revolves.
What is the nature of tornadoes ?
They differ from the other tropical storms merely in their extent and duration. Their extent is quite limited, and their duration at any one place is seldom more than a few seconds.
What is dew, hoar-frost, fogs, clouds, rain, and snow ?

Dew is moisture collected on substances during the night. Hoar-frost is frozen dew. Fogs are masses of vapor resting on or near the surface of the earth. Clouds are masses of visible vapor, differing in no respect from fogs, except in position. Rain is water which, originally taken up in the atmosphere in the form of vapor, is returned to the earth in drops. Snow is the frozen vapor of the atmosphere.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How is hail formed ?

As they are composed of alternate layers of snow and ice, it is supposed that the wind revolves around a horizontal axis, by means of which the moisture of the air is successively carried into warm and cold clouds. In the cold clouds the particles of snow are collected, and by the whirl are carried into rain clouds, where they receive a coating of water, which when returned to the cold cloud becomes ice. In this manner the hailstones grow rapidly in size, and are finally hurled to the ground.
Upon what does the distribution of rain depend ?

The quantity of moisture depends upon its tem-perature, and its vicinity to the sea. The rain-fall decreases as we pass from the Equator to the Poles, and from the coasts of the continents towards the interior.
How are clouds formed ?
Clouds or fogs result whenever two bodies of air of different temperatures are mingled, especially if, as is generally the case, the warmer of the two is the moister.
Under what circumstances is dew formed ?
The cooling of a still moist atmosphere causes a portion of this vapor to fall or collect in the form of small drops of water.
What is hoar-frost, and how is it formed ?
It is the freezing of the falling dew.
What are vapors ?
The particles of water so fine and light that they float in the air unseen, except where they condense and become clouds.
Mention some of the uses of snow.

Being a non-conductor of heat, it protects the roots of vegetation from the severe frosts of winter. It also collects in great masses on lofty mountains and grad-ually thawing, it feeds streams, springs, and numerous underground currents, which are of great benefit in the economy of nature.
Into what classes are clouds divided? Describe each class.

The cirrus, cumulus, stratus, and nimbus. The cirrus occupy the highest position in the atmosphere, and resemble a lock of hair, being composed of parallel streaks. The cumulus resemble a mountain of snow, and are most common in summer. The stratus consist of horizontal bands near the surface of the earth, and belong to the night. The nimbus is the rain-cloud, and much more dense than the others, although the others may be changed to the nimbus.
How may the surface of the earth be divided in reference to the quantity of rain that falls ?
Into rainless regions, regions of periodical rains, and regions of frequent rains.
Where are the regions of periodical rains ?
In the zone of calms, the zone of trade-winds, and the sub-tropical zones.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Describe the zone of calms.

In this zone it rains daily. The ascending current, hot and highly saturated, is cooled when it reaches greater elevations. In the afternoon, usually, a ring of clouds appears, and, accompanied by thunder and lightning, tor-rents of rain descend.
Describe the zones of the trade-winds.

These zones are characterized by almost constant serenity, and a deep-azure sky. But as the sun stands over the zenith during a part of the summer in these zones, it carries with it the calms and rains of the first zone. Hence the serenity of the trade-zones is interrupted by a brief rainy season during summer, while the winters are dry.
What are the characteristics of the sub-tropical zones ?

They are characterized by dry summers and rainy winters. This is due to the trades which prevail there during the summer, and absorb rather than bring moisture. During the winter the variable winds prevail, and bring the irregular rains of the temperate regions. A large portion of the United States lies within this zone, and would have dry summers if it were not for the monsoons occasionally blowing from the Mexican Gulf far inland, and bringing thunder storms.
Where are the regions of frequent rains ?
A part of the temperate zone and the frigid.
Where are the rainless regions ?
They lie almost entirely within the zones of the trade-winds.
Why are the Pacific shores of Peru and Bolivia rainless ?

The trade-winds coming across the entire continent, are intercepted by the Andes Mountains, and are forced to ascend their slopes, where they meet the cold air, which condenses their vapor, and the rain flows down the eastern slopes. The wind passes over the crest and reaches the western slopes dry and vaporless.
What would be the condition of South America, providing the Andes were on the Atlantic coast ?
The greater part would be a barren desert.
Why is the Pacific shore of Mexico rainless ?
The trade-wind is here deprived of its vapor by the Plateau of Anahuac.
Where is the largest rainless district of the globe ? Why are these regions rainless ?
The Great Desert, Arabia and Persia. The trades prevail here, but have lost their moisture on the passage across the mountains and plateaus of High Asia.
What is the cause of barrenness of the desert of Gobi ?
It is found to be in the Himalayas, which intercept the prevailing southwest winds, and deprive them of almost all their moisture.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Where does the greatest amount of rain fall ?

In the tropics of the New World. This amount is about 115 inches, while in the tropics of the Old World the fall is about 77 inches. In the temperate parts of the Old World, 34 inches fall; in the New World, 35 inches.
What particular place has the greatest annual rain-fall on the globe ?
On the Khasia Hills in Farther India, which face the Bay of Bengal and receive winds heavily laden with vapor on their passage over the Indian Ocean. The annual fall is about 50 feet.
What is climate ?
The state of the atmosphere in regard to tempera-ture, winds, moisture and salubrity.
What circumstances affect the climate of a place ?

The latitude of a place; its height above the level of the sea; the position and direction of the mountain chains; its distance from the sea; the slope of the coun-try; the character of the soil; the degree of cultivation and density of population; and the quantity of rain that falls.
What are the two chief classes of climate ? Describe them.

Oceanic and continental. Those regions which are open to the influence of the ocean are moist and mild, while those which are removed from the influence are commonly dry and suffer from great excesses of heat and cold.
Define Astronomical climate.
It is the climate based upon the latitude of a place and not the general conditions of that country.
Define Physical climate.
It is the modifications of the general law of climate by the action of winds, ocean currents, mountain ranges, deserts and the bodies of water.
What is the general law of astronomical climate ?
That the heat gradually decreases from the Equator to the Poles.
Why is temperature greater at the Equator, and why does it diminish towards the Poles ?

It is due to the direction of the sun's rays. As the sun is perpendicular to the Equator the rays fall direct and the heat is retained, while the direction, as we approach the Poles, becomes more oblique, on the same principle that heat decreases from midday to evening.
What modifications of climate do the earth's motions produce ?
Because of the inclination of the earth's axis the position of the earth to the sun is continually changed in its revolution, which produces a constant change of climate.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Where are the general deviations from the astronomical climate most marked ?
Chiefly in the middle latitudes.
Where are the extreme deviations from astronomical climate found ?

The extreme deviations occur on the coasts of the North Atlantic, those of Western Europe being warm and mild while the shores of Northeastern America are cold and desolate. These differences of temperature are due to the physical conditions, the Gulf Stream and the Arctic currents.
What difference between oceanic and continental climate ?

Oceanic climate is characterized by uniformity of temperature, while continental climate is subjected to sudden changes and great extremes of heat and cold, especially in the middle and higher localities.
What effect has the soil on the climate of a country ?

A country covered with a barren, sandy soil is subject to great and rapid changes in its temperature, owing to the readiness with which it receives and parts with heat, while marshy lands, or lands covered with forests or vegetation, are more salubrious.
Why does California have but little rain between May and November ?

It is owing to the eastern or trade-winds which prevail, they having been deprived of moisture by crossing the continent and meeting the Rocky and Sierra Nevada Mountains. The westerly winds prevail from December to May, which produces the rainy season.
Where are storms most violent ?

The most remarkable for violence, and for the regularity of their course, are the hurricanes of the West Indies and of Mauritius in the Indian Ocean; the typhoons of the South China Sea; and the cyclones of the Gulf of Bengal.
What are glaciers ?

Immense masses of ice and snow, which move with extreme slowness down the higher mountain valleys or slopes. The upper parts are formed of soft snow, but the great weight and pressure condense the lower portions into clear hard ice.
What is the origin of glaciers ?
It is the weight of the great fields of snow pressing downward and forcing the whole mass as explained above.
Where is the most remarkable glacier region ?
The glaciers of the Alps.
What evidence exists of former systems of glaciers ?

By the condition of the country; masses of unstratified rocks left behind; deep grooves, or scratches, cut in the bottom or sides of the valleys by imbedded rocks. These cuts are parallel, and show the direction of the motion.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the snow line ?

It is the lower limit of perpetual snow. Within the tropics it is about three miles above the sea level, in the temperate latitudes about two miles, and at the northern limit of the continents about half a mile.
What is electricity ?

One of the great forces of nature, with the origin and nature of which we are unacquainted. Like the power of gravitation it is a part of nature itself, indispensable in her organization, mild and terrific, and at last becoming the greatest agency in the advancement of civilization.
How many kinds of electricity are there ?
There are two kinds—a positive and a negative. A substance charged with one kind will attract a substance charged with the other, but repel a body charged with the same.
What are conductors and non-conductors of electricity ?
Conductors are those substances which transmit electricity freely; non-conductors do not.
Mention some of each.
Water, metals, living plants and animals, smoke, steam and moist air are conductors. Silk, glass, India rubber, guttapercha, hair, feathers and dry air are non-conductors.
What is lightning ?
Lightning is a discharge of atmospheric electricity, accompanied by a flash of light.
What is heat-lightning ?
It is the weak discharge of electricity from the clouds, producing a slight flash but not of sufficient strength to cause an explosion.
How is thunder produced ?
Thunder is the sudden rushing of the atmosphere to fill the vacuum produced by the lightning.
Why do we see the lightning before we hear the thunder ?
As light travels faster than sound, we see the flash before we hear the report.
Where are thunder-storms most frequent ?
Within the tropics during the rainy season.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the aurora borealis ?
It is a beautiful exhibition of nature, at night, in the Polar skies. In the temperate regions it is known as northern lights.
How is it supposed to be produced ?
It is supposed to be electric currents passing through a strata of highly rarefied air.
What is mirage ?

A reflection of some distant object plainly visible on the clouds. It is due to certain peculiarities of the atmosphere and conditions of the country. Vessels have been seen and recognized in this mysterious way. Sailors often regard this phenomena of nature as an evil omen.
Name some electrical phenomena.
St. Elmo's fire and Aurora Borealis and Aurora Australis. The latter is the electric display in the Southern Hemisphere.
What is organic life ?
It is that department of Physical Geography which treats of all vegetable and animal life.
Under what three divisions is this department considered ?
Botanical Geography, Zoological Geography, and Ethnography.
What is botanical geography ?
Botanical Geography treats of the different divisions of the vegetable kingdom and their geographical distribution.
What constitutes the flora of a country ?
The plants of any section of country taken together are called its flora.
What gives varieties to the plant life of the globe ?
They differ widely, by reason of the differences in heat, moisture, light, slope and soil—more particularly by the first two.
What is indispensable to the existence of vegetable life ?
Moisture, in the form of rain or dew.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How many species of plants are supposed to exist ?
More than 250,000 different species of plants are supposed to exist on the earth. Over 120,000 have been named by botanists.
What two forms of distribution of vegetation do we find ?

A horizontal and a vertical. The horizontal is the distribution arising from the influence of the heat and cold as we go from the Equator to the Poles. The vertical is the same influence from the base of a tropical mountain to the summit.
In what region is found greatest luxuriance, and why ?
In the equatorial regions, where heat and moisture are most abundant.
What similarity exists between the vertical and horizontal distribution ?
Plants of the same species are found as we go from one elevation to another up the tropical heights, to correspond with the plant creation as we journey north or south from the Equator.
What four regions are destitute of vegetation in consequence of the want of moisture ?
The deserts of Gobi and Arabian in Asia, the Sahara in Africa, and the Atacama on the western slope of the Andes.
Into what two great classes are vegetable forms divided ?
Into the cryptogamous (flowerless) and the phenogamous (flowering). 305. The mosses, lichens, fungi, ferns and seaweeds.
How are flowering plants divided ?
Into two classes: the endogenous and the exogenous.
Describe the endogenous. The exogenous.
The endogenous are those plants which increase from within, as grasses, sugar-cane, corn, etc. The exogenous are those which increase by coatings from without, as the trees of the forest, etc.
About how many distinct plant species are there ?
It is estimated that there are about 250,000 distinct plant species upon the earth. Less than half of these have been described by botanists.
Which are the simplest forms of the plant species?
The lichens, algæ and mosses.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Where are the lichens, algæ, and mosses found ?

Lichens cover the rocks in tropical deserts and in the regions of ice or snow. They are the most widely distributed class of plants. Algæ are sea and fresh water weeds, of the most varying forms and colors. Some attain an enormous size. One species, growing in the Straits of Magellan and near the Falkland Islands, is often 400 feet long. Mosses are also found in all zones, wherever moisture is abundant. In our forests they are quite common.
Which are the most stately of all vegetable forms ?

The palms. The tall and slender shaft rears on high its crown of shining, fan-like leaves. Some are nearly 200 feet high. They require a mean annual temperature of 78° to 82° Fahrenheit, and occur, therefore, chiefly in the hottest parts of the tropical zone.
What are the principal food-plants of the torrid and hot zones ?
Rice, bananas, bread-fruit, dates, cocoanuts, yams, cassava and sago.
What are bananas, bread-fruit, dates, cocoanuts, yams, cassava, and sago ?

Bananas are the fruits of tropical plants. This plant rises 15 or 20 feet high, with leaves six feet long and a foot broad. The fruit is four or five inches long, and an inch or more in diameter. They grow in large bunches, weighing fifty pounds or more. Bread-fruit is produced by the bread-fruit tree, which grows on the isles of the Pacific Ocean, of the size of the common apple tree. The fruit is of round or oval shape, as large as a small loaf of bread, which is eaten as food. Dates are a delicious fruit, produced by the date-palm of Asia and Africa. Cocoanuts are the fruits of the cocoa tree. These nuts hang in clusters of a dozen each on the top of the tree, and are bound together by tough, stringy filaments. Yams are roots resembling the potato, and are cultivated in a similar manner. Cassava is a kind of bread made from the roots of the cassava plant. Sago is obtained from the pith of several species of palm trees, which form entire forests in many of the Spice Islands. The pith is prepared into sagomeal by mixing it with water and straining. A tree commonly yields from 300 to 500 pounds.
What are the principal food-plants of the warm and temperate zones ?
Wheat, potatoes, corn, rye, oats, and barley.
What plants are the most valuable for furnishing clothing ?
Cotton, hemp, and flax.
What spices are in common use in the various countries of the globe?
Pepper, cinnamon, cloves, nutmegs, and vanilla.
What are the principal narcotics used in the different parts of the globe?
Tobacco, opium, betel, and haschish.
What is opium, betel, haschish?

Opium is prepared from a species of poppy, and is very extensively used in China and Turkey. The betel plant is a climbing shrub, which grows in Hindostan and the islands of the Indian Ocean. The leaves are used by the natives as tobacco. Haschish is a powerful narcotic, obtained from a species of hemp-wort, and is used in India to produce intoxication.
What is Zoological Geography?
That department of physical geography which treats of the divisions of the animal kingdom and its geographical distribution.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What constitutes the fauna of a country?
The animals found in any region of country are called its fauna.
What law of distribution prevails?

As a rule, the luxuriance and diversity of animal life decreases as we pass from the Equator to the Poles. A similar decrease is noticed in passing from the coasts of the continents towards the interior.
What exception to this rule in the case of marine animals?

In marine animal life it is the reverse ; both numbers and size of the species increase from the Equator to the Poles. This is probably due to the more equable temperature of the ocean in high latitudes.
What marks the range of animals?
Large bodies of water, deserts, or mountain ranges mark the boundaries of animals, as their physical condition in a great measure, draw the isothermal lines.
What exception to this rule?
Man, and his faithful friend, the dog.
What appears to be the basis of distribution of animals?
The distribution of heat, moisture and vegetation.
What modifies the fauna of each continent?
The physical condition of that country—its heat, moisture and vegetation, which are governed by its elevation, mountains, deserts, winds, and ocean currents.
Where does the musk-deer live? The cashmere goat? The elephant?

The musk-deer and cashmere goat live in Thibit and other parts of High Asia. (The costly musk is formed in a gland about the size of a pigeon's egg.) The elephant lives in India, Ceylon, and the Eastern Archipelago.
How have the animals upon the globe been classified?
Into four classes : vertebrates, mollusks, articulates, and radiates.
What are vertebrates? How have they been divided?
This division includes all animals which have an internal skeleton joined to the backbone. It comprises four classes : 1. Mammalia ; 2. Birds ; 3. Reptiles ; 4. Fishes.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are mollusks? Articulates? Radiates?

Mollusks are animals of soft texture, and have no skeleton; as the oyster, snail, and mussel. Articulates are animals consisting of a number of joints or rings, soft or hard, supplying the place of a skeleton; as the lobster, worms, spiders, and insects. Radiates are so called because in many cases their organs are arranged like rays proceeding from a center. The coral and microscopic animals belong to this division.
What is the mammalia?
They are the most perfect of the animal creation. They differ greatly in appearance and habits, but correspond in one particular of suckling their young. They number about 3,000 species.
How have the mammalia been classified?

Into eight classes : Quadrumana (four-handed), monkey, ape ; carnivora (flesh-eaters), bear, cat, dog ; Marsupialia (pouched), oppossum, kangaroo ; rodentia (gnawers), beaver, squirrel, rat ; edentata (toothless), sloth, armadillo ; pachydermata (thick-skinned), elephant, horse, hog ; ruminantia (chewing the cud), camel, ox, sheep ; marine mammalia, whale, dolphin, seal.
Of what does Ethnology treat?
It treats of the varieties of the human race, their physical and intellectual characteristics, and their geographical distribution on the earth.
What evidences exist as to the unity of the human race?

By a comparison of the different tribes and races of men we find the same general modification of types. The highest race will produce the same degraded forms of humanity as the lowest, there being no distinct species except that variety of modification produced by the enlightenment of man.
What three strongly marked types exist?
The Caucasian, Mongolian, and the Negro.
What three secondary races are found?
The Malay, or Brown Race ; the Australian, and the Indian, or Copper-colored.
What is supposed to have produced the diversity of races?
The ever varying external conditions.
What is the law of perfection of type in man?
It is an exception to that observed in the lower orders of creation. The human family appears in its highest physical perfection, not within the Tropics, but in the Temperate Zones.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Which is the typical or normal race?
The White race seems to be the normal race from which the others have gradually deviated.
From what race of people did the people of Europe descend?
From the Aryans, a primitive people supposed to have lived in prehistoric times, in Central Asia, east of the Caspian Sea and north of the Hindu Koosh Moun-tains.
Mention the chief European divisions of this race.
The Celtic, Teutonic, Slavonic, Gaulic, Pelasgians, and the Northmen or Normans.
What nations are descendants of the Celtic race?

The Celts are an ancient race of people who formerly inhabited a great part of Central and Western Europe, and whose descendants at the present day occupy Ireland, Wales, the Highlands of Scotland, and the north-ern shores of France.
The Teutonic?
The Teutons were a people of ancient Germany and are now represented by the Germans, Dutch, and Saxons.
The Slavonic?
The Slaves were a people who anciently inhabited the country between the Rivers Save and Drave, and their descendants are people of Russia, Poland, Bulgaria, and Bohemia.
The Gaulic? What country was ancient Gaul?
The people of France. This country was anciently known as Gaul.
The Pelasgians?
The Pelasgians were the ancient people of Greece, but were largely supplanted by the conquests of the Hel-lenes, who now form the greater portion of the Greek ancestry.
The Northmen, or Normans? 348. Who were the Saxons? Who the Anglo-Saxons?

The people of Norway and Sweden are descend-ants of that ancient race of Norsemen, who formerly occu-pied the northern portion of Europe. These people were a bold, hardy race who conquered large portions of Europe, establishing a powerful monarchy in England in the eleventh century, which, in a large measure, is the foun-dation of the present kingdom. At this time they were known as Normans.
From whom have the Italians derived their origin?
From the Romans, Germans, and Normans.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physical Geography. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
The Spanish and Portuguese?
A mixture of Celts, Romans, Germans and Arabs.
Write a brief description of these races, giving their order of descent.

Aryan. Celtics.        { Irish. { Welsh. { Highlanders of Scotland. Teutonic.       { Germans. { Saxons { Dutch. { The English or { Anglo-Saxons. Slavonic.       { Russians. { Polanders. { Bulgarians. { Bohemians. Gaulic.         { The people of France. Pelasgians      { The people of Greece. and Hellenes. Northmen        { Inhabitants of Northern Europe. or Normans.        { The Ancient Scandinavians. The Huns were a wild and barbarous race of Tartars who overrun Europe in its early history, but were finally defeated and driven out, except in the province of Hungary. These people and the Turks do not trace to Aryan ancestry.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in English Grammar. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is English Grammar?
The science of speaking and writing the English language correctly.
What is the object of studying grammar?
To be able to understand, speak, and write a language correctly.
What is language, strictly speaking?
Any method of communicating thought or feeling.
What can you say of the composition, of language?
It is of two kinds, prose and verse.
Into what parts is grammar divided?
Pronunciation, orthography, etymology, syntax and prosody.
Of what does pronunciation treat?
Of the sounds of letters and syllables.
Define etymology.
It treats of the different parts of speech, their derivation and modification.
Of what does syntax treat?
Of the arrangement and relation of words in sentences.
Of what does prosody treat?
Of versification, punctuation, figures and utterance.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in English Grammar. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the basis of grammar?
The usage of the best authors.
Of what does language consist?
Of sounds which, combined, form words which represent ideas.
How are words classified?

They are classified : As names of beings, places, or things. As substitutes for names or facts. As qualifiers or limiters of names. To assert action, being, or condition. To modify an assertion or a quality. To express relations of things or of thought. To introduce or connect words and sentences. To express a sudden or intense emotion. For rhetorical effect.
How are words distinguished?
They are distinguished as nouns, pronouns, adjectives, verbs, adverbs, prepositions, conjunctions, exclamations, words of euphony.
By what general name are the different classes of words called?
They are known by the general name of Parts of Speech.
What is a part of speech? How many?
Classes of words, differing according to their usage and meaning.
How are nouns divided?
Into proper and common; and the common are divided into collective, abstract and verbal.
What is a collective noun?
One that denotes a collection.
What is an abstract noun?
The name of some quality of a substance.
What is a participial noun?
A participle or infinitive used as a noun.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in English Grammar. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
When does a proper noun become a common noun?
When it is used to denote a whole class; as, "The Washingtons of the world are few."
When does a common noun become proper?
When personified, or used as a proper noun.
What is the office of a pronoun?
To avoid the repetition of nouns.
What is the antecedent of a pronoun?
The word, phrase or clause which the pronoun represents.
What is the subsequent of a pronoun?

Interrogatives or relatives of the interrogative kind, when they are used in asking questions, have no antecedents, but relate to some word or phrase contained in the answer, which is called the subsequent.
What is the difference between the antecedent and the subsequent?
The antecedent and subsequent are opposed to each other in meaning; the former signifying going before, the latter following after.
Into what classes are pronouns divided?
Personal, relative, interrogative and adjective.
What is a personal pronoun? Name them.
One which distinguishes the persons. *I*, *thou* or *you*, *he*, *she* and *it*, with their compounds and declined forms, are the personal pronouns.
Where are thou, thy, thine, thee and ye used?
In the Bible and addresses to the Deity, and frequently in poetry.
In parsing, how are the pronouns, ours, yours, hers, theirs and mine disposed of?
As these words are equivalent to a noun and pronoun, the parsing should correspond with the words which they represent.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in English Grammar. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What peculiarity about the pronoun it?

The antecedent of *it* is sometimes considered lost, when the pronoun denotes merely the state or condition of things, or a point of time, or when it introduces a sentence and is explained; as, It rains. It is twelve o'clock. It was moonlight. It is mean to take advantage of another's distress. Some authors consider *it* in the above examples as having no antecedent, yet, if we observe closely there may be one somewhat remote; as, in the first example, we might use *clouds* for the antecedent; in the second example, shorn of his beams." "Lo, steel-clad War his gorgeous standard rears.'
When is the feminine gender preferred?

When the character of the object is noted for beauty, amiability, productiveness, or submission, as "Soon Peace shall come with all her smiling train." The earth, moon, and ship, when personified, are given the feminine gender.
What are the methods of distinguishing the two sexes?
By different words, different endings, and by a distinguishing word; as, father, mother; emperor, empress; man-servant, maid-servant.
When and how are proper nouns made plural?
When they refer to a class of the same character, they are made plural by adding s when it does not coalesce in sound, otherwise es; as, the Cherokees; the Napoleons; the twelve Cæsars.
What class of nouns is generally used in the singular number? How may they be used in the plural?

The names of substances, actions, states, qualities, arts, sciences, and diseases, when they refer to the kind of thing; but when different kinds are meant they are plural; as, wine, wines, tea, teas; fever, fevers; religion, religions.
When is a collective noun singular? When plural?

When the whole collection is regarded as one thing, it is singular, but plural when different collections are meant, or when it refers to the individuals composing the collection; as, army, armies; congregation, congregations; most people are eager to succeed.
How are most nouns made plural?
By adding s to the singular; but those ending in i, o, u, y, and preceded by a consonant, and s, x, z, sh, and soft ch, add es.
What is the origin of the use of the possessive sign 's?
It is the abbreviation of the old English ending es or is; the sign (') being called an apostrophe, because it indicates the omission of the e or i.
How are most compound words made plural?
By making plural that part described by the rest; as, mouse-traps, cupfuls.
How are the titles Mr., Mrs., Miss and Dr. made plural?

Mr., Dr. and Miss are made plural by annexations; as, Messrs., Drs., Misses. When the title is Mrs., or when a numeral stands before the title, the title is made plural; as, the Mesdames Brown, the two Misses Smith.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in English Grammar. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How is the plural of foreign nouns formed?
Most of them retain their foreign plural when used in the English language.

How are foreign words ending in a, us, um or on, is, x or ex changed to form the plural? Form the plural of the following words: Formula, larva, nebula, vertebra, focus, radius, sarcophagus, stimulus, phenomenon, datum, analysis, synthesis, axis, basis, crisis, emphasis, oasis, parenthesis, thesis.
When the ending is a, it is changed to æ or ata, us is changed to i, um or on to a, is to es or ides, x, or ex to ces or cies.
How are letters and figures made plural?
By annexing 's.
How is the editorial we used?
To represent one person.
What is enallage as used in grammar?
A change of words, or a substitution of one gender, number, case, person, tense, mode, or voice of the same word, for another.
What is case? Name them.
The property of nouns and pronouns shows their relation to other words. They are nominative, possessive, and objective.
When is a noun or pronoun used in the different cases?
In the nominative when it is the subject of a verb; in the objective when it is the object of a verb or preposition; and in the possessive when it denotes possession.
When is a noun or pronoun used independently?
By direct address. "John, your father is here." By exclamation, "Alas, poor Yorick!" By pleonasm or specification, "He that hath ears, let him hear."
How is the possessive case denoted?
By adding the apostrophe and s, except when the word ends in s, when the apostrophe only is added.
How can the possessive case be expressed without the apostrophe?
By using of, or by making the possessive word an adjective. "The death of Socrates." "Soldiers' Home."

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in English Grammar. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
When are two terms in apposition? When is one predicated by the other? In what must these terms agree?

When a verb joins the terms, one is predicated of the other; as, "He is president;" but when no verb joins them, the latter term is in apposition with the former; as, "Webster, the orator." These terms must agree in case.
How is a compound term expressed in the possessive case?
It takes the possessive sign but once; generally at the end, or next to the name of what is owned. "The court-martial's decision." "Daniel Webster's oration."
When is a noun or pronoun in the same case as another?
When it denotes the same person or thing.
In how many cases are compound personal pronouns used?
Only in the nominative and objective cases, and then they retain the same form.
Decline who, which, what, and that.
Nominative who, possessive whose, objective whom; which and what have the same form in the nominative and objective, but borrow whose for their possessive. That is not declined.
When may that have two cases?
When it is a relative, and equivalent to that which.
What is declension?
A regular arrangement of the grammatical properties of a noun or pronoun.
How are the articles the, a, and an used?
The is used to point out a particular one, object or class; while a or an is used to show that no particular one of a class is meant.
Why are a and an both called the indefinite articles?
They have the same meaning, therefore, used in the same class. One is a later and the other an earlier form of the same word.
When should a be used?
Whenever the next word begins with a consonant sound.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in English Grammar. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
When should an be used?
Whenever the next word begins with a vowel sound.
What is an adjective?
A word used to qualify or limit the meaning of a noun or pronoun.
How are the adjectives divided? Define each class.

Into two chief classes: descriptive and definitive. The descriptive describes or qualifies; the definitive specifies or limits. 81. Into common, proper, numeral, pronominal, participial, and compound.
Define a common adjective.
The common adjective expresses quality.
Define a proper adjective.
A proper adjective is derived from a proper name.
What is a participial adjective?
A participle used as an adjective.
What is a compound adjective?
A compound word used as an adjective.
What is a numeral adjective?
One that expresses number.
How are the numeral adjectives divided? Define them.
Into cardinal, ordinal, multiplicative, and indefinite. The cardinals are one, two, etc.; the ordinals, first, second, etc.; the multiplicative, single, double; the indefinite, few, many.
What is a pronominal adjective?
One sometimes used as a pronoun.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in English Grammar. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Into what classes are the pronominals divided?
The distributive, demonstrative, and indefinite.
What modifications have adjectives?
Number and comparison.
What is comparison? How many degrees?
A variation in the form of adjectives and adverbs to denote the different degrees of meaning. There are three degrees, positive, comparative and superlative.
Why is this called comparison?
To give a variation of the degree of expressions.
Define the positive degree. Comparative. Superlative.
The positive expresses the quality of the adjective; the comparative expresses the quality in a higher or lower degree; the superlative expresses the quality in the highest or lowest degree.
What parts of speech have comparison?
Adjectives and adverbs.
How are degrees below the positive expressed?
Usually by adding less and least.
When should more or most in comparison be preferred?
Usually with adjectives of more than two syllables, or those of two syllables that do not end in le or y.
What is the difference between comparison ascending and comparison descending?
One expands in volume of expression, or grows greater in its comparison, while the other decreases with the prefixes less and least.
When are adjectives redundant?
When they have more than one form for its superlative, as, near, nearer, nearest or next.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in English Grammar. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
When adjectives of one syllable and those of several syllables come together, which are placed first and how are they compared?
The monosyllables are placed first, and all are compared by prefixing more and most; as, "The more nice and elegant parts."
Must adjectives always agree with their substantives in number?
Yes, although the substantive is not always expressed.
When does an adjective become a noun?
When it is used abstractly, or in place of a noun of which it expresses quality; as, the briny deep, the good.
What is a verb? A regular verb? An irregular verb?
A word used to state the act or state of the subject. A regular verb is one that forms its past tense by adding ed; an irregular verb does not add ed.
What are the principal parts of a verb?
The present tense, past tense, present participial, and perfect participial.
Why are these called the principal parts?
By the means of these and the auxiliary verbs all the other parts of the verb can be formed.
What is a redundant verb? A defective verb? A finite verb?

A redundant verb is one that has more than one form for some of its principal parts. A defective verb is wanting in some of its principal parts. A finite verb predicates the act or state of its subject.
What parts of a verb are not finite?
The infinitives and participles.
What is a transitive verb? An intransitive verb?
One that has or requires an object. An intransitive does not have or require an object.
When may a transitive verb become intransitive?
When it is the chief design to set forth the act, and leave the object unknown. "She reads well."

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in English Grammar. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
When may an intransitive verb become transitive?
In poetical expressions, in a causative sense, and in idiomatic expressions; as, to march armies, I laughed myself hoarse.
What is a neuter verb?
One that does not express action or state.
What is a principal verb? An auxiliary verb?
A principal verb expresses the chief act or state. An auxiliary verb helps other verbs to express their grammatical properties.
Define a redundant verb.
They are called redundant because they have more than one form for some of the modes and tenses.
Give examples of redundant verbs.
Am or be, awake, bereave, bet, dig, dwell, lean, keep, hang, gild, kneel, hew, knit.
What properties have verbs?
Voice, mode, tense, person and number.
Define voice, mode, tense.
Voice shows whether the subject does or receives the act. Mode is manner of assertion. Tense expresses time.
What are the person and number of a verb?
The form of the verb to agree with the subject.
When is a verb in the active or passive voice?
In the active, when the subject acts, in the passive when it receives the act.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in English Grammar. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How is a verb in the active voice changed into the passive?
By using the object of a verb for the subject.
To what verbs does voice belong?
To transitive verbs only.
How many modes are there?
Four: indicative, subjunctive, potential and imperative. Some grammarians add the infinitive mode.
What does a verb in the indicative mode express? In the subjunctive? In the potential? In the imperative?

The indicative indicates or declares a fact. The potential expresses power, possibility, liberty, inclination, duty and necessity. The subjunctive expresses a wish, an uncertainty, or future contingency. The imperative commands or entreats.
What is the difference between the indicative and subjunctive modes?
The only difference is in the third person singular of two of the tenses, the present and the present-perfect.
How many tenses in each of the modes?
Six in the indicative; three in the subjunctive—the present, past, and past-perfect; the potential, four—the present, present-perfect, past, and past-perfect; the imperative, one—the present tense.
What are the signs of the tenses?

In the indicative mode have is the sign of the present-perfect tense; had the sign of the past-perfect; shall or will, of the future; shall have or will have, of the future per- fect; the present or past tense is a verb in its simplest form, expressing present or past time.
