The signs of the present potential are may, can, must; of the present-perfect, may have, can have, must have; of the past, might, could, would, should; of the past-perfect, might have, could have, would have, should have. The three tenses of the subjunctive mode are the same in form as the indicative, except in the singular number of the present and past tense, which takes the plural form without variation. The imperative has the same form as the present indicative.
What are the forms of a tense?
The different ways in which it can be expressed; as the common form, the emphatic, the progressive, the passive and solemn.
How is the emphatic form of a verb expressed?
It denotes emphasis, and is expressed by do or did, as a part of the verb.
How is the passive form expressed? The progressive?

By combining the verb be, or some of its variations, with the perfect participle. The progressive form represents the continuation of the act or state, and is formed by combining the verb be, or some of its variations, with the present participle.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in English Grammar. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the ancient form?
The old common form that is still used in the solemn style. It uses thou or ye, and has the ending t, st or est for the second person singular; th or eth for the third person singular.
How is a proposition made interrogative? How made negative?
By placing the verb, or part of it, before the nominative. It is made negative by placing not after the verb, or after the first auxiliary.
When the subject consists of words differing in person, how is the form of the verb or pronoun determined?

The first person is preferred to the second, and the second to the third. You, John and I are attached to our country. If the nominatives are taken separately, or connected by or or nor, the verb prefers the nominative next to it. He or I am to blame.
What is an impersonal verb?
A verb having person and number without a subject; as, methinks, meseems.
What properties have auxiliary verbs?
Voice, mode, tense, person and number.
Which are auxiliary verbs?
Be and its variations—do, did; can, could; have had; may, might, must; shall, should; will and would.
Which are sometimes principal verbs?
Be, or am, was, do and have.
When is it proper to use shall and should?
When required to express a duty, command, determination, resolve; and in future propositions when the subject is of the first person and no reference is made to the will of the subject.
When is it proper to use will and would?
When the expression is of willingness, inclination or in future propositions, when the subject is of the second or third person, and no compulsion required.
When are do, be and have principal verbs?
When not combined with any other verb expressed or understood.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in English Grammar. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What stands as an answer to what? or when? of a transitive verb?
The object or complement of the transitive verb stands as an answer to the question what? or whom? with the verb.
How will you determine whether a verb is transitive or intransitive?
By asking the question what? or whom? and if it will carry an answer relating to some particular thing it is transitive, but if it does not carry the subject to some termination it is intransitive.
How many terms implied in a transitive verb?
It implies three terms—a subject, a predicate and an object.
How many terms implied in an intransitive verb?
It requires but two terms, a subject and a predicate.
What is a copulative verb?
One used to assert a condition of a subject; as, sugar is sweet. It is an incomplete verb using an adjective, noun or pronoun relating to the subject, and is often called a neuter verb.
What is the principal copulative verb?
It is to be and those belonging to this class, as, become, seem, appear, grow, feel, look, smell, taste, etc.
What is the peculiarity of these copulative verbs?
They imply in their meaning the verb to be; thus, become is really to come to be; appear is to be in appearance.
What is an infinitive? How many?
The form of the verb generally preceded by to, expressing an act or state without predicating it. There are two infinitives—the present and perfect.
How is the present infinitive formed, and what does it denote?
By combining to with the simplest form of the verb, or to be with a simple participle.
How is the perfect infinitive formed, and what does it denote?
By combining to have, or to have been, with a simple participle. It represents the completion of the act or state at the time referred to.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in English Grammar. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
When is to, the sign of the infinitive, omitted?
When the infinitive is combined with an auxiliary or after the active verbs bid, dare, let, make, need, see, hear, feel, and sometimes after have, help, please and find.
What is a participle? How many?
A word derived from a verb, participating in the properties of a verb and adjective, and is generally formed by adding ing, d or ed to the verb. There are two participles—the present and perfect.
How is the present participle formed? What does it represent?
By annexing ing to the simplest form of the verb, and represents the act or state as present and continuing at the time referred to.
How is the perfect participle formed? What does it represent?
By annexing ed to the simplest form of the verb, and it represents the act or state as completed at the time referred to.
What voice have participles?

The simple present participle of a transitive verb is nearly always in the active voice. The simple perfect participle of a transitive verb is either active or passive; it is in the active voice when have or any of its forms is combined with it; in the passive voice when it stands by itself, or when be in any of its forms is combined with it.
What is a compound participle?
Being, having, or having been, combined with some other participle.
What do being, having and having been express in relation to participles?
Being expresses the present passive participle; having expresses the perfect active participle; having been the perfect passive participle.
What are some of the chief particulars in which participles and infinitives agree? Some in which they differ?

They have voice and tense, are annexed to auxiliary verbs, they partake of the nature of nouns, adjectives and adverbs, and when nouns they assume case. A participle may become an adjective, it may govern the possessive case, it may become concrete and assume number, it may be used after prepositions; but an infinitive has none of these attributes.
What is the conjugation of a verb?
The regular arrangement of the modes, tenses, persons and numbers, and participles of verbs.
What is the inflection of a verb?
It is called its conjugation.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in English Grammar. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is meant by the weak and the strong conjugation of verbs?
The weak conjugation is the regular formation of the verb, as, move, moved. The strong conjugation is its irregular formation, as, grow, grew, with the past participle ending in n or en.
What is the theme of a verb?
The theme of the verb is that form which admits of the preposition to before it. It is the infinitive form of the verb.
What is a paradigm?
A word given as a model by which to inflect other words.
What is it to make a verb?
To make a verb in grammar is to place a word in the position of a verb where it will carry the properties of an action.
What is the synopsis of a verb?
An outline of its parts through the modes and tenses, in a single person and number.

What forms of a verb are called composite? (a) Conjugate lie, to repose; lie, to speak falsely; dare, to venture; bear, to carry. (b) Give the principal parts of the following verbs: drink, lay, rose, build, eat, sow, dive, gone, set, sit, get, bid, dig, slay, thrust, was. (c) Give the synopsis of the verb eat, in the third person, singular number, indicative mode, passive voice. (d) Give a synopsis of the verb run, in the first person, singular number, interrogative form, in the indicative and potential modes.
Those which consist of auxiliaries combined with participles or infinitives.
What is an adverb? How are most of them formed?
A word used to modify the meaning of a verb, adjective, or other adverb. The most of them are formed from adjectives by adding ly.
What is a conjunctive adverb?
A word used to perform the office of a conjunction and adverb at the same time.
Into what classes are adverbs divided?
Into adverbs of time, place, degree and manner.

What is the difference in the use of an adjective and an adverb? (a) Classify the following adverbs: Now, much, more, so, well, here, whither, everywhere, to-morrow, forever, daily, enough, nearly, yesterday, aloud, hither, whence, forth, away, often, little, yet, too, nevertheless, together, to-day, yonder, out, somewhat.
An adverb expresses manner, or describes the act; an adjective describes the object.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in English Grammar. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How can we ascertain to what class adverbs belong?

Adverbs of time answer to the questions: when? How long? How often? Adverbs of place answer to the questions: Where? Whence? Adverbs of degree answer to the questions: How much? To what extent? In what degree? Adverbs of manner answer to the question: How?
What is a preposition? An adjunct? To what does an adjunct relate?

A word used to show the relation between a fol- lowing noun or pronoun and some other word. An ad- junct is a preposition with its objects and modifiers, and may relate to a substantive, verb, adjective or adverb.
What may a substantive, governed by a preposition, be?
A noun, pronoun, infinitive, participial noun, and a clause.
When does a preposition become an adverb?
When there is no word to govern; as, The eagle flew up, then around, then down again.
What is a conjunction? Correlative connective?
A word used to connect words, phrases or propo- sitions. A correlative connective is one of a separated pair that connect the same parts. Neither this nor that.
What is the difference between analysis and synthesis?
Analysis is the resolving of the whole into its parts. Synthesis is the combining of the parts to form the whole.
What is parsing?
The resolving of a sentence into its parts, giving their properties and syntax.
What is relation? Agreement? Government?
The relation of words is their relation or connec- tion with one another; agreement is their similarity in per- son, number, gender, case, etc.; government is the power one word has over another.
What is a sentence? Clause? Proposition? Phrase?

A sentence is an assemblage of words making com- plete sense; a clause is a proposition that makes but part of a sentence; a proposition is a subject combined with its predicate; a phrase is two or more words, but not an entire proposition.
What is a declaratory sentence? Interrogatory? Imperative? Exclamatory?
The declaratory expresses a declaration; the in- terrogatory asks a question; the imperative expresses a command or entreaty; the exclamatory expresses an excla- mation.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in English Grammar. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is a simple sentence? Compound? Complex?
A simple sentence has but one proposition; a compound has two or more propositions; a complex has one principal clause, with one or more dependent clauses.
What is the subject and predicate of a sentence?
The subject is that of which something is said; the predicate is that which denotes what is said of the subject.
What is the logical and grammatical subject and predicate of a sentence?
The grammatical subject and predicate are the subject and predicate words; the logical subject and predi- cate are these words with all their modifiers.
What is discourse? Of what may it treat?
A train of thought expressed in language, and may be description, narration, science or philosophy.
What is a paragraph?
A sentence or combination of sentences, completing a train of thought, and distinguished by a new begin-ning.
What is analysis based upon?
Three relations : the predicate relation, the adjective relation, and the adverbial relation.
What are elements of sentences?
Words, phrases and clauses.
What must every proposition have?
A subject and predicate.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in English Grammar. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is a modifier? How many kinds?
A dependent word, phrase or clause, used to limit or vary the meaning of some other word or expression, and may be an adjective or adverbial modifier.
By what may a noun and pronoun be modified?
By an article, adjective, a possessive, an appositive, a participle, an infinitive, phrases and clauses.
By what may a verb be modified?
By an object, a predicate adjective, an adverb, a participle, an infinitive, phrases and clauses.
When an infinitive or participle is used as a noun, what modifiers do they take?
The infinitive takes only the modifiers of a verb ; the participle either those of a verb or noun.
What parts of speech are never modified?
Articles, prepositions, conjunctions, and interjections.
What kinds of words are connectives?
Conjunctions, prepositions, relative pronouns, responsive pronouns, and the conjunctive adverbs.
In the analysis of phrases, what forms are given?
Simple, complex, compound, mixed, and prepositional.
What part of a sentence is considered the most important?
The beginning ; the next most important is the end.
When may a subordinate element occupy the chief place?
When it is uppermost in the speaker's mind, or denotes what is most striking. " Out flew millions of flaming words." "By these we acquired our liberty."

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in English Grammar. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is a period in Grammar?
A sentence so constructed that the meaning is suspended till the close.
In what do all the errors of language consist?
In the improper arrangement of words, in the use of improper words, in the omission of words, and the use of unnecessary words.
What is a figure in grammar?
A deviation from the ordinary form ; a construction or application of words, for the sake of brevity, force, or beauty.
Name the figures of grammar.
They are the figures of orthography, of etymology, of syntax, and of rhetoric.
What is a figure of orthography?
It is an intentional deviation from the ordinary or true spelling of a word.
What is a figure of etymology?
It is an intentional deviation from the ordinary formation of a word.
What is a figure of syntax?
It is an intentional deviation from the ordinary construction of words.
What is a figure of rhetoric?
It is an intentional deviation from the ordinary application of words.
How many, and what are the names of the most important figures of etymology?
They are eight : aphæresis, syncope, apocope, prosthesis, paragoge, tmesis, diæresis, synæresis.
What is aphæresis? Syncope? Apocope? Prosthesis? Paragoge? Tmesis? Diæresis? Synæresis?

The shortening of a word by taking a letter or syllable from the beginning; as, 'gainst, there's. Syncope is the shortening of a word by taking a letter or a syllable from the middle; as, red'ning for reddening. Apocope is the shortening of a word by taking a letter or syllable from the end; as, th' for the. Prosthesis is the lengthening of a word by prefixing a syllable. Paragoge is the lengthening of a word by annexing a syllable. Tmesis is the inserting of a word between the parts of a compound; as, on which side soever. Diaeresis is the division of two concurrent vowels into different syllables, usually marked thus, coöperate, aërial. Synæresis is the joining of two syllables into one, as, dost, loved, learned, for do-est, lov-ed, learn-ed.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in English Grammar. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is ellipsis? Aposiopesis? Zeugma? Pleonasm?

Ellipsis is the omission of words, and elision the omission of letters. Aposiopesis is the leaving of something unsaid. Zeugma is the referring of a word to two different ones, when in strict syntax it can agree with only one of them. Pleonasm is the using of more words than the sense of syntax absolutely requires.
What is a simile? Metaphor? Allegory? Synecdoche?

A comparison expressed. A metaphor is a comparison implied. "Life is an isthmus between two eternities." An allegory is a fictitious story about one thing, which is designed to teach some moral or practical wisdom about another. Synecdoche is the name of a part applied to the whole, or that of the whole applied to a part.
What is antithesis? Irony? Paralipsis? Hyperbole?

A contrast: "Virtue ennobles, vice debases." Irony is the sneering use of words with a contrary meaning. Paralipsis is the pretended omission or concealment of what is meant. "I will not call him a villain, for it would be unparliamentary." Hyperbole is exaggeration.
What is euphemism? Interrogation? Exclamation?

A softened mode of speech for what would be disagreeable or offensive if told in the plainest language. An appeal in the form of a question to strengthen a statement. An abrupt mode of speech, designed to express more strongly the emotions of the speaker.
What is versification? Verse?
The art of making verse. Verse is the musical arrangement of words, according to some regular accent.
What is a foot?
A foot is two or more syllables, constituting a portion of a line.
What is rhyme? Blank verse?
The similarity of sounds between the endings of poetic lines. Blank verse is verse without rhyme.
What is a couplet? Stanza?
Two poetic lines that usually rhyme together. A stanza is a regular division of the poem, and consists of three or more poetic lines with complete rhymes.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Orthography, Orthoëpy And Phonology. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is Orthography?
It treats of correct spelling, and the nature and power of letters.
Define Orthoëpy.
The art of a correct pronunciation of words.
What is Phonology?
The science of uttering the elementary sounds.
What is sound? How are sounds classified?
The sensation produced upon the auditory nerve by vibration of the air or other conducting media. They are classified as tones and noises.
What are tones? What are noises?
Tones are harmonious sounds which are pleasing to the ear. Noise is a sound without any regularity of production and is devoid of the harmony of tones.
What is a letter?
A character used to represent an elementary sound.
What are elementary sounds?
Distinct sounds, from which all of the words of our language are formed.
How many elementary sounds in the English language? How divided?
Forty-five*. They are divided into three classes : vowels, subvocals, and aspirates.
Are all of these elementary sounds distinct and true elements of our language?
They are not true and distinct, as many of them are similarly formed, but they are as near the requirements of a pure articulation as Phonetists have been able to devise.
How many true elements in our language?

On this question Phonetists have not united on any definite satisfaction, as this science is capable of being so minutely construed that only experts can detect the variation. Webster gives forty-five.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Orthography, Orthoëpy And Phonology. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How are these sounds produced?
By the organs of speech, with the breath.
What are the organs of speech?
The principal ones are the lips, teeth, tongue, palate and nasal passages. * Some authors give 43, some 41, and divide them into tonics, subtonics, and atonics.
How is voice produced?
By the action of the breath on the larynx.
What are the organs of voice?
They are the lungs, diaphragm, intercostal muscles, bronchi and trachea, larynx and pharynx.
What is the difference between voice and speech?
Voice is tone produced by the action of the breath on the larynx, while speech is the modulation of the voice to express thought, and may be given in a whisper.
Describe the organs of voice separately.
For description of the organs of voice see question on Physiology.
What are the vocal chords?
They are the special vocal apparatus, and consist of two ligaments or bands of fibrous tissue situated within the larynx.
What is the glottis? The epiglottis? What is inspiration, or inhalation? What is expiration?
For definition see Physiology.
What peculiar formation has the tongue?
It consists of a mass of muscular fibers so arranged as to admit of motion in every direction or several directions at once.
Describe the palate. The nasal passages.
The palate is the roof of the mouth continuing backward, where it becomes a yielding, muscular membraneous awning, separating the mouth from the nasal passages and the upper part of the pharynx.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Orthography, Orthoëpy And Phonology. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What connection has the ear with the production of voice?
The ear receives the productions of the voice and by the communication of the auditory nerves with the brain it is understood.
How is speech produced? 23. How are the letters divided ?

By the action of the diaphragm and other muscles the breath is forced from the lungs through the bronchi and trachea into the larynx, where it comes into contact with the vocal cords, which being called into action, produce a vibration and by the resonating of the pharynx and other cavities, is communicated to the external air, and at length falls as a tone upon the listening ear.
What are vowels ? Consonants ?
Vowels are letters that denote pure tones; consonants are letters that represent the subvocal or aspirate sounds.
How are the consonants divided ?
They have three divisions formed from the whole: as single letters and combinations; mutes and semivowels; subvocals and aspirates.
What is a mute? Name them.
A letter which admits of no escape of breath while the organs of speech are in contact. The mutes are b, p, d, t, k, c and g hard.
What is a semivowel ? Name them.

A letter which, in pronouncing, admits of an escape of breath. The semivowels are all the consonants except the mutes.* * The difference between the mutes and semivowels, is that the organs of speech are more closely united in pronouncing the mutes than the semivowels. ā, ă, â, ä, ȧ, ạ, as in ale, add, air, arm, ark, all. ē, ĕ, ẽ, as in eve, end, verge. 20 ī, ĭ, as in ice, ill. vowel ō, ŏ, as in old, odd. sounds ōō, ŏŏ, as in moon, wool. ū, ŭ, û, as in use, up, urge. ou, as in out. oi, as in oil. b, d, g, j, l, as in bet, dot, get, jet, lid. 25 m, n, ñ, r, th, as in mit, not, finger, rat, that, conso- v, w, y, z, zh, as in vat, woe, yet, buzz, azure. nant ASPIRATES : sounds p, t, k, h, as in pet, tin, kit, hat. ch, th, f, wh, as in chin, thin, fat, when. s, sh, as in sin, shot.
According to this classification of elementary sounds, how many letters would be required to form a full alphabet ?
It would require forty-five.
How is this deficiency filled ?

By giving to some letters more than one sound. These sounds are represented by marks or symbols. The Macron - The Breve ˘ Vowel The Circumflex ^ marks. The Tilde, or Wave ~ Two Dots ¨ One Dot • Consonant The Bar - marks. The Dotted Bar .- The Cedilla ,
Of all the elementary sounds, which represent the purest product of the human voice ?
Italian a, as in ah.
What can be said of all other vocals and subvocals ?
All others are considered modifications of this tone, as they require some portion of it.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Orthography, Orthoëpy And Phonology. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is a diphthong ?
A union of two vowel sounds in the same syllable.
How many diphthongs are there ?
Four : ou, ow, oi, oy.
How many sounds are represented by these diphthongs ?

Two: ou and ow represent the same sound, and are called inseparable diphthongs, because they cannot be separated into two distinct vowel sounds; oi and oy represent one sound, and are called separable diphthongs, because their sounds can be separated into broad a and short i. To test the separable diphthongs, spell by sound the words oil and boy, using broad a and short i in each, and pronounce in quick succession.
What is a digraph? Triphthong?
A digraph is the union of two vowels in the same syllable, one of which is silent. A triphthong is the union of three vowels in the same syllable, two of which are silent.
What are the elements of which orthoepy treats?
Articulation, syllabication, and accent.
What is a syllable?
A letter or combination of letters, uttered by one emission of the voice.
What is a word?
A syllable or combination of syllables representing some thought or idea.
What are the words of one, two, three, four or more syllables called?
Monosyllables, dissyllables, trisyllables, and polysyllables.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Orthography, Orthoëpy And Phonology. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is essential in every syllable?
A vowel.
By what is the principal sound in every syllable produced?

By the vowel, except in unaccented syllables with e as the vowel sound. This may be observed in the careless manner in which many pupils spell such words as table, label, center, etc. In these words the vowel is not as distinct as the consonant sounds.
To what does every consonant belong?
Unless it is silent, every consonant in a word belongs to some vowel or diphthong.
When is a consonant antecedent or consequent to a vowel?
When it precedes the vowel in the same syllable, it is antecedent to it; when it follows in the same syllable, it is consequent to it.
What is syllabication?
Dividing words into syllables.
What is the guide for arranging words into sylla-bles?
They must be arranged exactly as they are heard in correct pronunciation.
How may words be divided at the ends of the lines?
Between the syllables.
Why are words divided into syllables?
To assist in the pronunciation of words.
When is the hyphen used?
Between compound words when first formed or when little used; between syllables and the ends of lines; and between syllables for the purpose of instruction.
When two vowels come together, how are they disposed of in syllabication?
Unless it should be a diphthong, two syllables must be formed, or they may be used together with one vowel silent.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Orthography, Orthoëpy And Phonology. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
When is one letter a substitute for another?
When it has the sound that another letter usually represents.
What properties does a substituted letter assume?
Those of the letter which it represents.

Which letters have no substitutes? The following table contains a list of the substitutes of the elementary sounds: A long has two substitutes : ei and ey, as in weight and they. A short has no substitutes. A circumflex has one substitute : e, as in there. A broad has one substitute: o, as in for. E long has one substitute: i, as in machine. E short has two substitutes : a and u, as in says and bury. I long has one substitute : y long, as in rhyme. I short has four substitutes : y, e, u, and o, as in hymn, England, busy, and women. O long has two substitutes : eau and ew, as in beau and sew. O short has one substitute : a, as in what. U long has one substitute : ew, as in new. U short has three substitutes : e, i, and o, as in her, stir, and son. U circumflex has one substitute: o, as in wolf and wool. F has two substitutes : ph and gh, as in philosophy and rough.
By b, d, g, h, l, m, n, p, r, th, and wh.
When is p silent?
When it is initial and before n, s, or t; as in psalm, pneumonia, psalter.
When is t silent?
Before ch in the same syllable; as in notch, latch. It is also silent in Christmas, eclat, mortgage, and a few others.
When is w silent?
Before r in the same syllable; as in wrap, wreck. In whole, sword, whoop, answer and two it is silent.
When is gh silent?
After i in the same syllable; as in fright, neigh; after au and ou; as in aught, ought, bought, slaughter, and through.
In what words is ch silent?
In yacht, schism, and drachm.
In what words is s silent?
In corps, isle, island, puisne, viscount, and belles-lettres.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Orthography, Orthoëpy And Phonology. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Is m ever silent?
In the word mnemonic or mnemonics.
In how many ways are words designated?
In two ways: first, as simple or compound; second, as primitive or derivative.
What is a simple word? Compound?
One that is not composed of two or more whole words; a compound word is composed of two or more sim-ple ones.
What is a primitive word?
One that is derived from no other word.
What is a derivative word?
One formed from a primitive by means of prefixes or suffixes.
What is a prefix? Suffix? Root?
A letter or letters joined to the beginning of a word is called a prefix; when added to the end of a word, a suffix; and the root is the chief word without prefix or suffix.
Under what circumstances is a word primitive which is formed by the addition of prefixes or suffixes?

Whenever the meaning of the word is radically changed by the addition of prefixes or suffixes; as in re-proof, in which the meaning of neither re or proof is retained. In forming derivatives by means of suffixes, something more is required than merely to write the parts together as one word. Thus, if we desire to affix the suffix ed to the word suffer, we have only to unite, without change, the suffix with the radical; as suffered. If we take prefer, a change must be made by doubling the final letter r; as preferred. This and other changes of like nature are made, for the most part, in strict accordance with known rules. These rules should be committed to memory by repeated illustrations in order to fully understand when and how to apply them.
After ascertaining the syllabication of a word what is next to be observed?
To locate the accent.
When two accents occur in the same word which usually precedes the other?
The secondary nearly always precedes the primary.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Orthography, Orthoëpy And Phonology. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Do words ever have more than two accents?
Some very long words have two secondary accents.
What is the rule for accent of a word of two syllables?
Words of two syllables, excepting amen, never have more than one accent.
What applies to compound words?
In compound words each part retains its own accent, the same as two distinct words.
What is the rule where words change their parts of speech from verbs to nouns or adjectives?
When used as verbs the accent is usually on the last syllable, while when used as nouns or adjectives the accent is on the first syllable.
Where is the accent when words end in sion or tion?
The accent is on the syllable next to the last.
Words ending in ical or acal, place the accent on what syllable?
They generally have the accent on the syllable next preceding it.
What is a final letter?
It is the last letter of a word.
What is the power of a letter?
The elementary sound which it represents.
What does the difference of accent sometimes serve to do?
It changes the meaning of the word; as, Au'gust, the eighth month; august', grand, majestic.
Name and define the kinds of spelling.
There are three kinds of spelling, viz., Phonic, oral, and written.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Orthography, Orthoëpy And Phonology. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is phonetic spelling?
It is spelling by sound.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is Mathematics ?
The science of quantity.
What is quantity ?
Anything that can be increased, diminished or measured.
Define arithmetic as a Science and as an Art.
As a science, it logically investigates and philosophically classifies and arranges the principles and rules of the subject; as an art, it applies these principles to the practical affairs of life.
Upon what is arithmetic founded, and how are its operations carried on ?
It is founded on notation, and its operations are carried on by means of addition, subtraction, multiplication and division.
What is arithmetical language ? How many kinds ?
The method of expressing numbers. It is of two kinds, Oral and Written. The former is called numeration and the latter notation.
What is a unit ?
One, or a single thing.
What is a number? A power of a number? Root?
A unit or a collection of units. The power of a number is the product produced by repeating a number any number of times as a factor. A root is a factor repeated to produce power.
What is an integer ?
A whole number.
What is an abstract number ? Concrete number?
An abstract number is one used without reference to any particular thing. A concrete number is one that has reference to a particular thing.
What is a sign? What is the sign of aggregation?

A character indicating an operation to be performed. The sign of aggregation is a parenthesis, or bar, which shows that the numbers enclosed by it are to be considered together subject to the same operation.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is a rule? Problem? Axiom?
A rule is a prescribed method of performing an operation. A problem is something to be done. An axiom is any truth that is self-evident.
What is analysis in arithmetic ?
The process of investigating principles, and solving problems, independently of set rules.
What is notation ? Numeration ?
Notation is a method of writing or expressing numbers by characters. Numeration is a method of reading numbers expressed by characters.
How many systems of notation in general use ?
Two: The Roman and Arabic.
Upon what is the Roman notation founded ?

Upon five principles: first, repeating a letter repeats its value; second, if a letter of any value is placed after one of greater value, it is added to the greater; third, if a letter of any value is placed before a greater, it is to be taken from the greater; fourth, if a letter of any value is placed between letters of higher value, it is to be taken from their united values; fifth, if a bar or dash is placed over a letter, it increases its value a thousandfold.
In how many ways may numbers be written ?
By words, letters, and figures.
In the Arabic system of notation numbers are expressed according to what principle ?
By the position of the nine characters.
Why is the Arabic system of notation also called the Decimal system?
Because of its increase and decrease in a tenfold ratio. Decem is a Latin word, meaning ten.
What is the difference between the simple and local value of a figure ?
The simple value of a figure is its value when taken alone, while the local value depends upon the place it occupies with other figures.
What are the fundamental principles of arithmetic ?
Notation and numeration, addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the effect of placing a bar over a letter?
It increases its value a thousandfold.
What is the fundamental synthetic process of arithmetic ?
Addition, for by it we pass from unity to plurality; from the one to the many. This process, which gives rise to numbers, becomes the primary operation of arithmetic.
Give the principles of addition. 24. How many and what are the cases in addition ?
Only similar numbers can be added. The sum is a number similar to the numbers added. The sum is the same in whatever order the numbers are added.
Why do we write the numbers as suggested by the arithmetics, and why do we begin at the right hand to add ?
Merely for convenience.
What is the fundamental analytical process of arithmetic ?
Subtraction.
Give the principles of subtraction.

Similar numbers, only, can be subtracted. The difference is a number similar to the numbers subtracted. If the two numbers are equally increased or diminished the remainder will be the same. If the first number is equal to the sum of the second and the remainder, then the second must equal the difference between the first and the remainder.
Name and define the cases in subtraction.

Like the cases of addition, one is expressed in the Arabic system, the other by some mental calculation. or square, is the number multiplied by itself. The third power, or cube, of a number, is the product arising from using the number three times as a factor.
What is the reciprocal of a number ?
It is 1 divided by that number.
How find the true remainder by dividing by factors ?

Multiply each partial remainder, except the first, by all the preceding divisors, and add these products to the first remainder. It will be noticed that after the first division, the remainder cannot be of the same denomination as the first number divided; and as each succeeding division increases the value of the remainders, therefore each remainder must be multiplied by all the preceding divisors to reduce it to its former denomination.
What is a prime number ? When are numbers prime to each other ?
One that cannot be resolved into factors. Numbers are prime to each other when they have no common divisor.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is common divisor ? The greatest common divisor ?
One that will divide two or more numbers without a remainder. The greatest common divisor is the greatest number that will divide two or more numbers without a remainder.
What is a multiple ? Common multiple ? Least common multiple ?

A multiple is a number that is exactly divisible by a given number. A common multiple is one that is exactly divisible by two or more numbers. The least common multiple is the least number that is exactly divisible by two or more given numbers.
What are fractions ?
Parts of a unit.
What is the value of a fraction ?
The quotient of the numerator divided by the denominator.
What does the numerator show ? The denominator ?
The denominator shows into how many parts a number is divided; the numerator shows how many parts are taken.
What is a proper fraction ? Improper ? Mixed ? Complex ?

A proper fraction is one whose numerator is smaller than its denominator. An improper fraction is one whose numerator is equal to or greater than its denominator. A mixed number is one expressed by an integer and fraction written together. A complex fraction is one that has a fraction in its numerator, or denominator, or both.
Is a fraction a number ?

The usual expressions are that they are not numbers, but by an analysis of this question we find that a fraction is a number of equal parts of unity, and consequently must represent a subdivision of numbers; thus, five feet is a number, and the fraction five-twelfths of a foot represents equal numbers of a lower order, as five inches.
Is a fraction a denominate number ?
A fraction is not a denominate number.
Why invert the divisor in division of fractions ?
For convenience; if not inverted they must be reduced to a common denominator, and one numerator divided by the other.
How find the greatest common divisor of fractions ?
Find the greatest common divisor of the given numerators, and the least common multiple of the denominators.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How find the least common multiple of fractions ?
Find the least common multiple of the numerators, and the greatest common divisor of the denominators.
What are decimal fractions ?
Fractions whose denominators are increased or decreased in tenfold ratio.
What is the first thing to be considered in the treatment of decimals ?
The method of reading and writing them, or their numeration and notation.
What will the denominator of a decimal always be?
The unit 1 with as many ciphers annexed as are equal to the number of figures in the decimal or numerator.
Upon what does the value of a decimal depend ?
Its place from the decimal point.
How do decimals increase and decrease ?
They increase from right to left, and decrease from left to right, in a tenfold ratio.
What is a pure decimal ?
A pure decimal consists of a decimal only thus, .35, which is read 35 hundredths.
What is a mixed decimal?
One with an integer and a decimal; thus, 15.7.
What is a complex decimal ?
One with a fraction annexed; thus, .005¼.
From what are the names of decimal orders derived ?
From the names of the orders of integers.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How find the product of two decimals by a contracted multiplication?

Write the multiplier with the order of its figures reversed, and with units' place under that figure of the multiplicand which is the lowest decimal to be retained in the product. Find the product of each figure of the multiplier by the one above in the multiplicand and those to the left, increasing this multiplication by as many units as would have been carried had the rejected part of the multiplicand been used. Write these partial products with the lowest figure in the same column and add together, pointing off the required number of decimal figures.
How find the quotient of one decimal divided by another by a contracted division ?

Compare the left hand figure of the divisor with the units of like order in the dividend, and determine how many figures will be required in the quotient. For the first contracted divisor, take as many figures from the left of the divisor as there are places required in the quotient, and in each following division reject one place from the right of the last preceding divisor, using the remainder for a new dividend.
What are circulating decimals ?
A decimal in which a figure or set of figures are continually repeating.
What is a repetend ?
The figure or set of figures continually repeated.
What are continued fractions ?
A fraction whose numerator is 1, and whose denominator is a whole number, plus a fraction whose numerator is also 1, and whose denominator is a similar fraction, etc.
What is the difference between decimal fractions and common fractions ?

The first has a denominator understood, showing that a unit is divided into ten equal parts, or subdivided in a tenfold ratio ; the second has a denominator showing that a unit is divided into any number of equal parts.
What is currency?
The medium of circulation.
From what was the sign $ derived ?
From the initial letters of the United States, U. S. joined together as a monogram.
What is an aliquot part of a number ?
Such a part as will exactly divide that number.
What is a bill?
A written statement of articles bought or sold, together with price of each and the whole cost.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is a compound number ?
A concrete number, whose value is expressed in two or more different denominations.
What is a denominate number?
It is a concrete number in which the unit of meas-ure is established by law or custom.
What is a simple denominate number?
It is a denominate number composed of units of the same denomination; as 3 feet.
What is a compound denominate number ?
When it is composed of two or more denominations which are related to each other; as 3 feet 4 inches.
What is a standard unit ?
A unit of measure from which the other units of the same kind may be derived.
Name the quantities of magnitude which give rise to denominate numbers ?
They are value, weight, length, surface, volume, capacity, angles, time.
What is the standard unit of value?
Money is the standard unit of value, and is of two kinds, coin and paper money. In the United States the standard unit is the dollar. In English money it is the pound.
What is the standard unit of weight ?
It is the Troy pound.
What is the standard unit of length ?
The standard unit of length is the yard.
What is the standard unit of surface ?
It is the square yard for ordinary measurement, and the acre for land.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the standard unit of volume ?
It is the cubic yard for ordinary measurement, and the cord for wood.
What is the standard unit of capacity ?
The gallon for fluids, and the bushel for dry measure.
What is the standard unit of angles ?
It is the right angle, or, in practice, one degree of a circle.
What is the standard unit of time?
The standard unit of time is the day. This is determined by the revolution of the earth.
What is a measure ? How divided ?
That by which extent, dimension, capacity, or amount is ascertained. Measures are of eight kinds: Length, surface or area, solidity, weight, time, angles, capacity and money.
How many dimensions have extension? A line? A surface ? A solid ?
Extension has three: Length, breadth, and thick-ness.
Explain how distance is measured by time?

Every circle is supposed to be divided into 360 equal parts called degrees. As the earth makes its revolution once in 24 hours, the 24 hours must equal 360°, or one hour 15° of distance. Then by subdividing we find 1° would equal 4 minutes, and 1-60 of a degree would equal 4 seconds.
How do you find the difference in time, corresponding to any difference in longitude?
Divide the difference in longitude, expressed in degrees, minutes and seconds, by 15, and the respective quotients will be hours, minutes, and seconds.
How do you find the difference in longitude, corresponding to any difference in time ?
Multiply the difference in time, expressed in hours, minutes and seconds, by 15, and its respective products will be degrees, minutes and seconds of longitude.
How do you find the time at one place, when the time at another place and their difference of time are known ?
When the second place is east of the first, add their difference of time; when it is west of the first, subtract their difference of time.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How do navigators determine their longitude at sea ?

Taking with them a chronometer (an accurate watch) set to mark the time at a given place (as Greenwich or Washington), they ascertain by observation of the sun with the sextant the time at the place they are in, reduce the difference of time to the difference of longitude, and thus they find they are so many degrees east or west of the meridian of the place for which their chronometer is set.
What is a scale?
A series of numbers, descending or ascending, used in operations upon compound numbers.
What is weight ?
A measure of the quantity of matter a body contains, according to some fixed standard.
Describe the Gunter's chain ?
It consists of 100 links, each link containing 7.92 inches, or the whole length 66 feet, or 4 rods.
What is the difference between a pound Troy and a pound Avoirdupois?
Troy has 5,760 grains; avoirdupois has 7,000.
What is linear measure ?
That which is used in measuring lines or distances.
What is a square ? A cube?
A square is a figure having four equal sides and four equal angles; a cube is a figure having six equal sides.
How many inches in a wine gallon ? In a beer gallon ? In a bushel?
In a wine gallon, 231 cubic inches; beer, 282; in a bushel, 2,150.4 cubic inches.
What are duodecimals ?
The division and subdivision of a unit, resulting by continually dividing by 12.
What is percentage? What is its base ?
Such part of a number as is represented by the per cent. Its base is the number on which the percentage is reckoned.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What do the words per cent. mean ?
By the hundred.
Name and define the elements involved in percentage.

The elements are: Base, the number of which the per cent. is taken. Rate, the number of hundredths taken. Percentage, the amount taken. Amount, the sum of the base and percentage. Difference, the base less the percentage.
What is the difference between rate and rate per-cent.
The rate is the number of hundredths, and the rate per cent. is the fraction which denotes how many hundredths are taken. Thus, in 3 % , or 3/100, 3 is the rate and 3/100 the rate per cent.
Give the applications of percentage.
The rules of percentage are applied to the computing of interest, discount, commission, taxes, insurance, duties, and especially profit and loss.
What is commission ? Brokerage ?
The percentage allowed an agent, factor or commission merchant, is commission. Brokerage is the fee paid to a dealer in money, stocks, etc., for the transaction of business.
What is a commission merchant?
An agent who buys and sells goods for another.
What is a consignee ? Consignor?
A person who receives goods to sell for another. A consignor is a person who sends goods to another to be sold.
What is a company ? Corporation ?
Individuals united for the purpose of performing some business or undertaking. A corporation is a company formed and authorized by law to act as one person.
What is a share? Stock? Stockholder?
One of the equal parts into which capital stock is divided. Stock is the amount of capital invested. Stockholders are the owners of stock.
What is the difference between a charter and a firm?
A charter defines the powers of an incorporated body, while firm is the name under which an unincorporated body transacts business.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
When is stock at par ? When below par ?
Stock is at par when it sells for its first cost; below par, when it sells for less than first cost.
What is profit and loss ?
A commercial term, used to express the gain or loss in business transactions.
How find the gain or loss per cent. when the cost and selling price are given ?
Divide the gain or loss by the purchase price.
How find the selling price when the cost and gain or loss per cent. are given ?
Multiply the purchase price by $1, increased or diminished, as the case may be, by the gain or loss per cent.
How find the cost when the selling price and the gain or loss per cent. are given ?
Divide the selling price by $1, increased or diminished, as the case may be, by the gain or loss per cent.
What is an installment? Assessment? Dividend?

A portion of the capital stock required of the stockholders as a payment on their subscription. An assessment is a sum required of the stockholders to meet the losses or the business expenses of the company. A dividend is a sum paid to the stockholders from the profits of the business.
What is insurance ? A policy? A premium?

Security on property guaranteed by one party to another, for a stipulated sum, against the loss of that propproperty by any casualty. A policy is a written contract between the parties. Premium is the sum paid for insurance.
Define underwriter.
The underwriter is the insurer—the person who takes the risk.
Name and define the kinds of insurance.
There are six different kinds of insurance: Fire insurance, Marine insurance, Cyclone insurance, Accident insurance, Health insurance, Life insurance.
What is a tax? Poll tax? Assessor?

A tax is a sum of money assessed on the person or property of an individual for public purposes. Poll tax is a sum required of each male citizen liable to taxation, without regard to his property. An assessor is the person appointed to prepare the assessment roll, and apportion the taxes.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is an inventory ?
A written list of articles of property with their value.
What is general average ? Jettison ?

A method of ascertaining the loss to be sustained by the proprietors of ships, freight, and cargo, where a portion of the property has been sacrificed or damaged for the common safety. Jetsam is the portion of goods thrown overboard.
What are duties or customs ? What is a custom-house ?
Duties are taxes levied on imported goods. A custom-house is an office established by government for the transaction of business relating to duties.
What is smuggling ?
Carrying on foreign commerce secretly, without paying the duties imposed by law.
How many kinds of duties ? Define them ?

There are two kinds: Ad valorem and Specific. Ad valorem duty is the sum computed on the cost of the goods in the country from which they were imported. Specific duty is a sum computed on the weight or measure of goods, without regard to their cost.
What is interest ? Usury?
A sum paid for the use of money. Usury is illegal interest.
What is tariff ?
A list of the rates of duties to be collected.
What is the difference between simple and compound interest ?
Simple interest is the interest on the principal only. Compound interest is the interest on the principal and the previous interest added.
The time, rate per cent. and interest being given, how find the principal?
Divide the given interest by the interest on $1 for the given time at the given rate.
The time, rate per cent. and amount being given, how find the principal ?
Divide the given amount by the amount of $1 for the given time at the given rate.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
The principal, time and interest being given, how find the rate per cent. ?
Divide the given interest by the interest on the principal at 1 per cent. for the time.
The principal, interest, and rate per cent. being given, how find the time ?
Divide the given interest by the interest on the principal for one year at the rate per cent.
What is a partial payment ? Indorsement?
Part payment of a note, bond, or other obligation. An endorsement is an acknowledgment written on the back of a note, or any obligation, stating the time and amount of a partial payment.
What is discount ? Present worth of a debt? How find it?

Discount is an allowance made for the payment of a debt before it is due. The present worth of a debt is such a sum which, being put at legal interest, would amount to the debt when due. It is found by dividing the given debt by the amount of $1 for the given time and rate.
What is a bank? Bank discount ? Days of grace?

A bank is a corporation chartered by law for the purpose of receiving and loaning money, and furnishing a paper circulation. Bank discount is an allowance made to a bank for the payment of a debt before it is due. Days of grace are three days allowed a bank to pay its notes after they become due.
What is a promissory note ? Bank note ? Negotiable note ?

A promissory note is a written agreement to pay a certain sum either on demand or at a specified time. Bank notes are paper circulation issued by banks as money. A negotiable note is one which may be bought and sold, or negotiated, and is made payable to the bearer or to the order of the payee.
Name and define the kinds of notes.
They are classified as time notes, negotiable notes, joint notes, bank notes, drafts, stock, and bonds.
What is a notary-public ? A protest?

An officer authorized by law to attest documents or writings of any kind to make them lawful. A protest is a formal declaration in writing, made by a notary-public, at the request of the holder of a note, notifying the makers and the endorsers of its non-payment.
What is a draft ? A check?

A draft is a note of exchange, issued by a bank payable on some other bank. A check is also a note of exchange, but issued by a person or company and made payable at some bank where the person has deposits or credit.
What is a bond ? What is a coupon ?

Bonds are interest-bearing notes issued by nations, states, cities, railroad companies, and other corporations as a means of borrowing money. The coupons are the due-bills for the interest, which, as they become due, are cut off and presented for payment.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What do the terms five-twenties and ten-forties mean ?

They are applied to government bonds and indicate the time when due. The five-twenties are due twenty years after issue, although the government can pay them any time after five if they choose to do so. The same is applied to the ten-forties.
What is exchange ? How many kinds ?
A method of remitting money from one person to another, or making payments by written orders. There are two kinds, domestic and foreign.
What is domestic exchange ? Foreign exchange?
Domestic or inland exchange is the exchange between different places in the same country. Foreign exchange is the remittance made between different countries.
What is a bill of exchange ?
It is a written request or order upon one person to pay a certain sum to another person, or to his order, at a specified time. A draft or check is one kind of bill of exchange.
How many parties to a transaction in exchange ?
There are always three parties, and usually four, to a transaction in exchange.
What is a set of exchange ? Course of exchange ?

It consists of three copies of the same, made in foreign exchanges, and sent by different conveyances to provide against a loss; when one has been paid the others are void. A course of exchange is the current price paid in one place for bills of exchange on another place.
What is equation of payments ?
The process of finding the mean time of the payment of several sums, due at different times, without interest.
What is equated time?
The date at which several debts may be paid at one time.
What is the focal date?
The date by which all others are compared in averaging an account.
What is partnership ? Partners ?
A relation established by two or more persons in trade. Partners are the individuals thus associated.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the difference between simple and compound partnership ?

A simple partnership is one in which each of the partners has his capital invested for the same length of time. A compound partnership is that in which the capital of the partners is employed for different periods of time.
What is an endorsement ?
The endorsement of a bill is the writing upon its back, by which the payee relinquishes his title, and transfers the payment to another.
What is an acceptance ?
The acceptance of a bill is the promise which the drawee makes to pay it at maturity.
How is this obligation acknowledged?
By writing the word "accepted," with his signature, across the face of the bill.
What is a bankrupt ?
A bankrupt is a person who fails in business and has not property enough to pay his bills.
What is the difference between a bankrupt and an insolvent ?
Bankrupt is strictly applicable only to a trader, while the term, insolvent, applies to any person who is unable to pay his debts.
Define bankruptcy.
It is the condition of the business of a bankrupt.
What is analysis? 163. What is ratio ? Define each kind.
It is the process of arriving at a required result by following a course of reasoning, and not by formal rules.
What are the terms of a ratio ?
The two numbers compared.
What is the antecedent ? Consequent ?
The antecedent is the first term of a ratio; a consequent is the second term.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is direct ratio ? Inverse or reciprocal ?
Direct ratio is dividing the consequent by the antecedent. Inverse ratio is dividing the antecedent by the consequent.
In how many ways may the ratio of two numbers be expressed ?
In two ways: By placing a colon between the terms, and by the form of a fraction, taking the antecedent for the numerator and the consequent for the denominator.
What is the difference between a simple ratio and a compound ratio ?
A simple ratio is the ratio of two numbers. A compound ratio is the product of two or more simple ratios.
What is proportion ?
An equality of the ratio.
Which are the extremes ? The means ?
The extremes are the first and fourth terms. The means are the other two.
What is a continued proportion ?
A proportion with more than two equal ratios; as, 3:5::6:10::9:15.
How many antecedents in a proportion ? How many consequents ?

As each ratio has an antecedent and consequent; then every proportion must have two antecedents and two consequents; the first and third terms being the antecedents, and the second and fourth the consequents.
How are ratio and proportion distinguished ?
Ratio is the relation between two numbers, while proportion is the relation between two ratios.
What is a simple proportion ?
An equality between two simple ratios.
What is a direct proportion ?
One in which each term increases or diminishes, as the one on which it depends increases or diminishes.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is an inverse proportion ?
One in which each term increases as the term upon which it depends diminishes, or diminishes as it increases.
What is a compound proportion ?
A proportion in which either ratio is compound.
Of what does allegation treat ?
Of mixing or compounding two or more ingredients of different values.
How many kinds of allegation ? Define each.

Two: Medial and Alternate. Medial is the process of finding the average price or quality of several ingredients whose prices or qualities are known. Alternate is the process of finding the proportional quantity to be taken of several ingredients whose prices are known.
Give the analysis of allegation.

Allegation is but a short way of performing an analysis. To show the process we will unite 3, 4, 7, 10, to form a value worth 6. It will be noticed that the difference between 10 and 6 is a loss of 4, and to make a loss of 1 will require 1/4, also there will be a gain of 1/3 in the opposite connection. The proportion, then, if the two terms 3 and 10, expressed in fractions, is 1/4 and 1/3, which, reduced to a common denominator, is 3/12 and 4/12, or expressed in whole numbers, 4 and 3, which, in allegation, is understood as an exchange of difference without the use of fractions and their reduction to whole numbers.
What is an annuity ?
A sum of money payable at certain periods of time.
Name and define the kinds of annuity.

There are five kinds : A Certain Annuity, which continues for a fixed period of time. A Perpetual Annuity, continuing forever. A Contingent Annuity, depending upon certain occurrences, as on the death of a person. An Annuity in Reversion, one that begins at a specified future time or event. An Annuity in Arrears, is one the payments of which have been allowed to accumulate instead of being paid.
What is involution ?
Involution is the process of raising a number to a given power.
What is a surd ?
The indicated root of an imperfect power.
What is the power of a number ?
A power of a number is the result obtained by using it a certain number of times as a factor.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is a perfect power ? An imperfect power ?
A perfect power is a number whose root can be found. An imperfect power is one whose root cannot be found exactly.
What is the exponent of a power ?
The exponent of a power is a number placed at the right of the root and just above it, to show the number of times the root is to be used, as 3^4 = 3x3x3x3.
Define evolution.
Evolution is the process of extracting the root of any number considered a power. It is the opposite of involution.
What is the root of a number ?
The root of a number is one of the equal factors of that number.
How are the roots of numbers indicated ?
By the sign called Radical Sign, √^2. The figure on the sign indicates the root.
What is the square root of a number ?
One of two equal factors that produce that number. 192. One of three equal factors that produce that number.
What is arithmetical progression ?
A series of numbers increased or diminished by a common difference.
What is geometrical progression ?
A series of numbers increased or diminished by a constant multiplier.
What five things are to be considered in arithmetical progression ?
The first term, the last term, the number of terms, the common difference and the sum of the series. Three of these being given, the other two can be found.
What things are to be considered in geometrical progression ?
They are the first term, the last term, the number of terms, the constant multiplier, and the sum of the series. Three of these being given, the other two can be found.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is a duodecimal ?
A denominate number in which twelve units of any denomination make a unit of the next higher denomination.
What is mensuration ?
The art of measuring magnitudes.
What is magnitude ?
Any substance which has length, breadth and thickness, or all of them.
What is the measuring unit of surfaces ?
It is customary to use a square as the measuring unit, as a square inch, foot, rod, etc.
How do you find the area of a parallelogram and a square ?
By multiplying the length by the breadth.
How the area of a rhombus? Trapezium? Tri-angle ?
By multiplying the length by the altitude. By multiplying half the sums of the parallel sides by the altitude. By multiplying the base by half the altitude.
How do you find the area of a triangle when the three sides are given ?
From half the sum of the three sides subtract each side respectively ; then multiply together half the sum and the three remainders, and extract the square root of the product.
How find the circumference of a circle when the diameter is given ? How find the diameter ?
To find the circumference multiply the diameter by 3.14159 ; to find the diameter, divide the circumference by the same number.
How find the area of a circle ?
By multiplying half the circumference by half the diameter ; or, by multiplying the circumference by a fourth of the diameter.
How find the side of the greatest square that can be inscribed in a circle of a given diameter ?
Divide the square of the given diameter by 2, and extract the square root of the quotient.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How find a mean proportional between two num-bers?
By multiplying the given numbers together, and extracting the square root of the product.
How find the solidity of bodies whose sides are perpendicular to each other ?
By multiplying the length, breadth and thickness.
How find the solidity of a prism ?
By multiplying the area of the base by the height.
How find the lateral surface of a right prism ?
By multiplying the length by the perimeter of the base.
How find the solidity of a pyramid or cone ?
By multiplying the area of the base by ¼ of the altitude.
How do you find the lateral or convex surface of a regular pyramid or cone ?
By multiplying the perimeter of the base by ½ the slant height.
How find the solidity of a cylinder ?
By multiplying the area of the base by the height or length.
How find the convex surface of a cylinder ?
By multiplying the circumference of the base by the height.
How find the surface of a sphere or globe ?
By multiplying the circumference by the diameter.
How find the solidity of a sphere or globe ?
By multiplying the surface by ⅓ of the diameter.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How find the side of a square equal in area to any given surface ?
By extracting the square root of the given surface.
How do we measure lumber ?
By multiplying the length, breadth and thickness. Boards are considered one inch in thickness, and are so reckoned, even if they are less.
How find the solid contents of a stick of round timber ?
By multiplying the length by ¼ the mean girt or circumference.
What is gauging ?
The process of finding the contents or capacity of casks, barrels, etc.
How find the contents of casks ?
Multiply the square of the mean diameter into the length; then this product by .0034 to obtain the wine gallons. By using the decimal .0028 will give the beer gallons.
Define the metric system of measurement ?
The Metric System is a decimal system of weights and measures, having the meter for the base or unit.
What is the measure of the meter in inches ?
It measures 39.3708 inches.
From what was the meter obtained ?
It is one ten-millionth part of a quadrant of the earth's meridian.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic — Test Examples. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
One gentleman meeting another, and inquiring the time past 12 o'clock, received for an answer, ⅓ of the time from now to midnight ; what o'clock in the afternoon was it?
Where shall a pole 60 feet high be broken, that the top may rest on the ground 20 feet from the stump?
Suppose a man owes $1,000. What sum shall he pay yearly so as to cancel the debt, principal and interest, at the end of three years, reckoning it at 6 per cent. simple interest?
$371¹¹⁄₁₀₀.

A gentleman has a garden 400 feet long and 300 feet wide which he would raise 9 inches higher by means of the earth to be dug out of a ditch that goes around it. To what depth must the ditch be dug, supposing its breadth to be everywhere 6 feet?
Divide $1,000 among A, B and C, so that A may have $156 more than B, and B $62 less than C.
If 21 cows eat up 8 acres of grass in 6 weeks, and 18 cows eat up the same in 9 weeks, how many cows will it maintain for 18 weeks, if the grass grow uniformly during that time?
15 cows.
Require the thickness of the lead of a pipe 1 1/4 inch bore, which weighs 16 lbs. a yard in length, a cubic foot of lead weighing 11,325 oz. avoirdupois.
If a cubic foot of brass were drawn into wire 1/40 of an inch in diameter, required the length of the wire, supposing no loss in the metal.
Divide $25 between 2 boys in such a proportion that, after it is divided, A shall have 1/4 more than B.

A makes B a present of $100, on condition that he shall expend it in cows, sheep, and geese; cows at $10 each, sheep at $1, and geese at 16 2/3 cents, so as to have just 100 in the whole. How many must he purchase of each?
5 cows, 41 sheep, and 54 geese.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic — Test Examples. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
If 1/2 of 6 be 3, what will 1/4 of 20 be?
How long must I keep $300 to balance the use of $500, which I lent a friend 4 months?
6 2/3 months.
If 800 men have provisions for 2 months, how many must leave that the remainder may subsist 5 months on the same?
A hare, running 36 rods a minute, has 57 rods the start of a dog; how far must the dog run to overtake him, running 40 rods per minute?
570 rods.
The hour and minute hands of a watch are together at 12 o'clock; when are they next together?
If a pole be 1/3 in the mud, 3/5 in the water, and 6 feet out of the water, what is its length?
90 feet.
A man being asked how many geese he had, replied: if I had 1/2 as many as I now have, and 2 1/2 geese more, I should have 100; how many had he?
The head of a fish is 4 feet long, the tail as long as the head and 1/2 the length of the body, the body as long as the head and tail; what is the length of the fish?
32 feet.
A and B can build a wall in 4 days, B and C in 6 days, A and C in 5 days; require the time if they work together.

A man left his two sons, one 14, the other 18 years old, $1,000, so divided that their shares, being put at 6 per cent, interest, should be equal when each should be 21 years old; what was the share of each?

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic — Test Examples. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
A gentleman divided his fortune among his sons, giving A $9 as often as B $5, and C $3 as often as B $7: C received $7,442.10 2/3; what was the whole estate?

Three horses, belonging to 3 men, do work to the amount of $26.45; A and B's horses are supposed to do 3/4 of the work, A and C's 7/10, B and C's 1/3, on which supposition the owners are paid proportionately; what does each receive?

A gay fellow spent 2/5 of his fortune, after which he gave $7,260 for a commission, and continued his profusion till he had only $2,178 left, which was 3/8 of what he had after purchasing his commission; what was his fortune?
A general, placing his army in a square, had 231 men left, which number was not enough by 44 to enable him to add another to each side; how many men in the army?

Suppose that a man stands 80 feet from a steeple, that a line to him from the top of the steeple is 100 feet long, and that the spire is three times as high as the steeple; what is the length of a line reaching from the top of the spire to the man?
197 feet, nearly.
How many acres in a square field measuring 70.71 rods between the opposite corners?
15 1/2 acres.
How long does it take sound to travel 120 miles?
9 m. 14 sec. +
A laborer dug a cellar, the length of which was 2 1/2 times the width, and the width 3 times the depth; he removed 144 cubic yards of earth; what was the length?
36 feet.
