A owes B $750, due in 8 months, but receiving $300 ready money, he extends the time of paying the remainder, so that B shall lose nothing; when was it paid?
1 yr. 1 mo. 10 days.
The sum of two numbers is 266 2/3, and the product of the greater multiplied by 3 equals the product of the less multiplied by 5 ; what are the numbers ?

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic — Test Examples. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
A park 10 rods square is surrounded by a walk which occupies 1/10 of the whole park ; what is its width ?

What are the solid contents of a round log of wood 36 feet long, 1.6 feet in diameter at one end, and diminishing gradually to a diameter of .9 of a foot at the other ?

Two men carry a kettle weighing 200 pounds ; the kettle is suspended on a pole, the bale being 2 feet 6 inches from the hands of one, and 3 feet 4 inches from the hands of the other ; how many pounds does each bear ?

If a triangular piece of land 30 rods in length, be 20 rods wide at one end and comes to a point at the other, what number of square rods does it contain ?

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic — Test Examples. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.

A man's desk was robbed three nights in succession: the first night half the number of dollars was taken and half a dollar more; the second, half the remainder was taken and half a dollar more; the third night, half of what was then left and half a dollar more, when it was found he had $50 left; how much had he at first?
A owns ⅖ and B ⅗ of a ship; A's share is worth $10,000 more than B's; what is the value of the ship?

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic — Test Examples. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
A man gave his oldest son ¼ of his property less $50; to the second he gave ⅓; and to the youngest he gave the remainder, which was ⅙ less $10; what was the amount of his property?
What number is that, ⅗ and ¼ of which being multiplied together, will produce the number itself?
The difference of two numbers is 53 and the difference of their squares is 10,759; what are the numbers?
The sum of two numbers is 120, and the difference of their squares is 4,800; what are the numbers?
The diagonal of a square field is 120 rods; what is its area?

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic — Test Examples. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.

A man in Buffalo purchased a draft on St. Paul, Minnesota, for $5,320, drawn at 60 days, paying $5,141.78; what was the course of exchange?

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic — Test Examples. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
The time since noon is ⁷⁄₁₇ of the time to 4 o'clock P. M.; what is the time?

Find the bank discount of a note of $825.50, payable in 3 months, rate 6 per cent.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic — Test Examples. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
If ⅓ of a number be subtracted from ⅔ of the number, the remainder will be 6 less than ¼ of the number; what is the number?
What must be paid for a draft on London for £256 10s., at 10 per cent. premium?
What is the equated time on the following debts: $180 due in 4 months, $540 due in 7 months, and $280 due in 10 months?
7 months and 9 days.
If a certain number be diminished by its ⅓, and ⅔ of the remainder be added to the first number, the sum will be 18.24; require the number?
If 2 ounces of silver, worth $1.10 an ounce, are melted with 2 ounces of gold worth 94 cents a pwt., what is the compound worth per ounce?
Two men are 90 miles apart and travel towards each other, A starting 1 hour before B; A goes 9 miles in 2 hours, B 11 miles in 4 hours; how far will each travel before they meet?
A 57 7/33 miles, B 32 2/33 miles.
What date is 7 months 15 days before July 15th, 1861?
November 30, 1860.
By selling at 33 cents a pound twice as much is gained as by selling at 29 cents a pound; what per cent. is gained by selling at 32 cents a pound?
28 per cent.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic — Test Examples. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What per cent. of ⅓ is ⅔?
In how many years will the error of the Julian Calendar involve the loss of a day?
128¼ years.
At what time between 12 and 1 o'clock do the hour and minute hands of a clock point in directions exactly opposite?
12 o'clock 32 8/11 minutes.

Suppose A, B and C to start from the same point and to travel in the same direction round a circular island 73 miles in distance, A at the rate of 6, B of 10, and C of 16 miles per day; in what time will they be next together?
36½ days.
A blacksmith agreed to shoe a horse for 1 mill for the first nail in his shoe, 2 mills for the second nail, and so on; the shoes contain 32 nails; how much was he to receive?
Sold a horse for $37.05, losing 81 per cent.; what was the loss?
$157.95. 150. The mainmast of a ship is 95 feet long, the diameter of the base is 3½ feet, that of the top 2½ feet; what is its solidity? Ans. 677.73475 feet.
A club spent £2 12s. 1d; on settling, each paid as many pence as there were individuals in the party; how many were there in the party?
25 persons.
A grocer divided a barrel of flour into two parts so that the smaller contained ⅔ as much as the other; how many pounds were there in each?
78⅔ pounds, 117⅔ pounds.
A man having spent ⅓ and ¼ of his money, had £48¾ left; how much had he at first?
If ⅔ of a ton of chalk cost £¾, what will ⅚ of a ton cost?

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Written Arithmetic — Test Examples. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
A man was hired 50 days, receiving 75 cents for every day he worked, and forfeiting 25 cents for every day he was idle; he received $27.50; how many days did he work?
40 days.

A gentleman paid $18.90 among his laborers; to each boy 6 cents, to each woman 8 cents, and to each man 16 cents; there were three women for each boy, and two men for each woman; how many men were there?
A man paid $82.50 for a sheep, a cow, and a yoke of oxen; for the cow 8 times, for the oxen 24 times as much as for the sheep; what did he pay for each?
A, B and C commence trade with $3,053.25, and gain $610.65; A's stock + B's is to B's + C's, as 5 to 7; and C's stock — B's, is to C's + B's, as 1 to 7; what is each one's part of the gain?

There is a windlass, the wheel of which is 60 inches in diameter, and the axis, around which the rope coils, is 6 inches in diameter; how many pounds on the axle will be balanced by 240 pounds at the wheel?
2,400 pounds.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Theory And Practice Of Teaching. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is it to educate a human being aright?
It is to direct the mind, the thoughts, the feelings, the instincts, into the channel of truth, morality, enterprise, determination and economy.
What is economy?

It is the careful utilization of one's forces, and may relate to mind, strength, money, or any of the departments of mind or nature by which the greatest attainments can be acquired with the least expense of those forces.
What is political economy?
It is the utilization of the forces of government, the revenues, and the establishing of those laws of economy by which the greatest good will be distributed among the people.
What are some of the principles of a true political-economy?

To administer the laws without fear or favor, to prevent monopoly, to encourage thrift, to destroy the weeds of indolence and vice, to teach the principles of self-reliance, of co-operation, of manliness, and the grand and noble elements of truth and virtue.
Do individuals have instincts?

We are taught that reason is the great faculty by which man becomes the image of his Creator, and that instinct is nature's gift to the lower orders of creation. To us this teaching, although grand and noble in thought and purpose, does not make the true distinction between man and the brute. Man, although endowed with the power of reason, often displays another element of his being. He instinctively laughs, he crys, he mistrusts, he is warned by some influence, and in many ways receives impressions which, like the instincts of animals, work without knowledge or reason. The same may be applied to the power of reason with the ape, the dog, the horse, and other animals by which the dividing line between reason and instinct cannot be measured.
What should be the greatest accomplishment of all human beings?
To strive to control his passions; to speak like a lady or gentleman; to rise above the vulgar and indifferent, and to be a Christian in thought, deed and purpose.
How can these accomplishments be acquired?
By the right cultivation of the mind while young. "As the twig is bent so the tree is inclined."
How shall we judge a nation in the scale of civilization?

We can judge them by the history of their schools, the universal distribution of the same general knowledge. Knowledge is the lever that controls the progress of the world. It is the sword of success, the armor of defense, and the power that prevails.
What does the theory of practice of teaching embrace?
It embraces: School Economy, Methods of Instruction, Methods of Culture, The History of Education.
Of what does school economy treat?
It treats of the best modes of the organization of the school, and of utilizing the means of instruction.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Theory And Practice Of Teaching. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Define methods of instruction.
The best means of imparting knowledge.
What do you understand by methods of culture?
The study of the physical and mental character of man by which his faculties may be developed and strengthened.
Of what should we treat in the history of education?
We should compare the past with the present; show the growth, the advancement and advantages which now mark the progress of that education.
To what must the American people give the praise for the wonderful development of the country's industries, and its arts and sciences?
To the free gift of a national education.
What should we take into consideration in making preparations for the school?

There should be a suitable location, properly graded, drained and shaded. The house should be built for comfort, health and pleasure. The furniture, apparatus, records and studies should be carefully arranged and selected. The whole should be homelike and attractive.
What would embrace a suitable location?
A spot that is convenient of access. Suitability of the grounds and surroundings. Healthiness of the neighborhood. Beauty of the location.
What should be adopted as a proper arrangement for school-grounds?
They should be thoroughly drained, dry, level, sodded, ornamented with shade trees, house for fuel, neatly fenced and supplied with pure water.
What care should you exercise over the school house, the furniture, the apparatus, the grounds?
The teacher should consider the whole as his own; he should be thoughtful and vigilant; he should caution when necessary, and always feel the deep responsibility which has been placed upon him.
Should the teacher go to his boarding place for dinner?

Under no consideration should he leave the school grounds, as his presence is required to keep peace, order, prevent teasing, protect property and strive to impress upon all the value of being ladies and gentlemen.
State the objects of graded schools.

They economize the labor of instruction; lessen the cost; make teachers more effective; promote good order in school; prompt the ambition of pupils; provide instruction in the higher branches of learning; and remove the necessity of children's leaving home to obtain a good education.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Theory And Practice Of Teaching. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How are schools graded?
Into the Primary, the Grammar and the High School.
What branches are taught in the higher department of these graded schools?
The High School Course embraces algebra and higher mathematics, rhetoric, political economy, history, physiology, and the studies of nature, business and language.
Upon what should the size of a school-house depend?

Upon the number of pupils in attendance. An ungraded school, to be taught by one teacher, should contain at least nine hundred square feet. A house designed for fifty to eighty scholars and one recitation room, fifteen hundred square feet. One for eighty to one hundred and twenty scholars and two recitation rooms, two thousand square feet.
Describe a suitable school-house for rural districts.

It should be rectangular in form, the door entering at the south end, while the north end should be without entrance or windows. The room should be from twelve to sixteen feet high. It should contain an apartment for clothing and necessaries. It should be well lighted, warmed and ventilated.
How should a school-house be warmed?

In rural districts it would be useless to recommend the modern appliances for heating, but we can improve the stove often in use. Provide a good stove, pipe and chimney. Surround the stove with a tin casing made to extend from the floor to about one foot above the top. There should be a door in the casing for putting in fuel; and a trunk for the conveyance of fresh air should start outside of the building, run under the floor and communicate directly with the door. The object of a tin casing and trunk for conveying fresh air must be apparent to all, as one will prevent the burning of clothing and the other to supply a pure current of air upon which combustion depends.
How should a school-room be ventilated?

Ventilation should be regulated by the lowering of the windows from the top instead of raising the bottom sash, especially in cool weather. This will prevent a direct draft upon the heads of the scholars. Openings in the ceiling and doors will also be a means of relieving the heated pressure.
What should be the furniture of a school-room?

The desks should be of modern approval and set so as to enable all pupils to pass to and from their seats without creating confusion. There should be a library of reference books; a complete dictionary; a geographical gazetteer; a biographical dictionary; a popular encyclopedia; sets of historical and physiological charts; outline maps.
How should the teacher's desk be arranged?
It should embody a plain business office desk, with drawers, shelves, closets and book-rack.
With what apparatus should schools be supplied?

The most approved are: A set of cards for teaching the alphabet, pronunciation, spelling, and elementary reading, with a stand to hang them upon; letter-blocks, and a chart of elementary sounds; writing charts; drawing cards and material; a numerical frame, and sets of square and cube root blocks; a globe, a set of outline maps, and a Tellurian; charts of history; a case of minerals and curiosities; engravings; a thermometer.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Theory And Practice Of Teaching. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What should be the temperature of a school-room?
In our estimation it should be 72° Fahr., although many claim this temperature is too high, but we must remember that the moving teacher cannot judge by his feelings what would please his scholars.
Of what use are school-records?
To aid the teacher in his work; to give information to parents and school officers; to furnish educational statistics; to exert a beneficial influence upon the pupils.
What should be the first step in the organization of a school?

To produce a good impression. The language should be mild, but interesting. There should be no show of superiority in power or knowledge. You should arrange so as to obtain the confidence and respect, holding at the same time a deep interest for the instruction of those placed in your charge.
Give an outline of work preliminary to the organization of a school?

The young teacher should consider well his adaptation to a particular school before engaging it. He should study the difficulties which he might encounter; he should lay his plans before the school officers and ask their advice; the contract should be made in writing, and if possible, the teacher should make the acquaintance of parents before opening of school.
What benefits will arise by forming these acquaintances?

It will convince the people of an interest in the school, and draw out their sympathies and general interest. It will assist in forming calculations and how to organize. It will reflect the characters of those who are most foremost in mischief and good behavior, and it will give a better chance for the study of human character. 44. It is to test the knowledge, the power of speech, of explanation and the development of expressions. It gives confidence and demands a careful study of the subject under discussion. It induces thought and quickens perception.
How should the recitation be conducted?

It should cover the general points in the lesson by brief and comprehensive statements. The lesson preceding should be carefully reviewed, and in carrying this preceding action to the point of commencement a new examination of to-day's lesson should again be given and this carried into the study of to-morrow.
What is necessary for a good recitation?
A live, intelligent teacher; recitation seats; blackboards; apparatus; reference books; call bell; ventilation and proper temperature.
What preparation should the teacher make for the recitation?
He should have a thorough knowledge of the subject, and depend upon a general discussion rather than quote the words of the book.
How would you teach grammar?

Of all studies grammar is open to general explanations more than others. Books should be used only as a source from which to draw knowledge. No fixed plan of recitation should be followed. The elements of grammar should be given first and carefully illustrated. Definitions should be learned only as applied. There should be no rotation, no fixed laws of advancement. Analyzing should be introduced as soon as we have asked the first question. We should teach from observations, simple at first, or until the main principles are thoroughly comprehended. Do not commence too young, as grammar requires such comparisons that the mind must be able to see them.
How would you teach geography?

Quite similar to that of grammar. Give lessons which will compare one section with another. It is not necessary to be confined to one book, as the same principles are embodied in all of them. Have a variation of topics. Call for the various productions of South America and where produced. This is enough for one lesson, unless you prove its climate and position. Better have a full knowledge of a little than fail in big attempts. Follow with other questions of interest of the same country. The author once conducted a large class without a book for his reference, but in the class there were eight different geographies. The plan worked admirably, although a case of necessity. Please bear this in mind; do not give long and difficult lessons. Do not forget the apparatus.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Theory And Practice Of Teaching. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is a good plan for studying history?

On this subject the teacher should be well posted. He should map out the points of greatest interest, and as the class advances, give the subdivisions. Do not be afraid to call up a subject at the beginning, the middle, or the end. Review is the great secret of success.
Why is it necessary to take exercise?
Because it is necessary to health, strength and study.
Define school government.
It is a proper organization of order, thought and action. It is the means of developing a love for study and improvement.
Under what heads may school government be embraced?
As School Ethics, Retributions, Legislation and Administration.
Define ethics.
The science which treats of human rights and duties.
Define school ethics.
It relates to the rights and duties of persons connected with the school.
Who are the important factors in the school ?
The teacher. The general school-officers. The communities that found and support schools. The pupils.
What may be considered as some of the most important qualifications of the teacher ?
He should have common sense, knowledge, teaching power, government, and love of the work.
What may be considered as the school-duties of pupils ?
It is given as duties to themselves, to one another, to the school property, to the teacher, to the school-officers, to visitors, to society, and to God.
What is discipline ?
A close or thorough application to some particular subject or subjects. In school life it is often applied to the correction of particular errors and faults.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Theory And Practice Of Teaching. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are the general species of school government ?
They are considered as force, authority, and love.
What may be considered as proper penalties ?
Privation of recitations, of recess, of position in class, reproof, reports to parents, suspension, expulsion, punishment, withholding favors, special penalties.
What may be considered improper penalties?
Threatening, scolding, asking for excuses, whipping in anger, compulsory study as a punishment, physical or mental torture beyond the absolute demand of the case.
What actions of the teacher should be specially denounced ?
Any sudden or violent action, as throwing rulers or slapping the head. Care should be taken not to punish for offenses done beyond the jurisdiction of the school-yard.
What things should every teacher perform ?

Convince your scholars by your conduct that you are their friend; take special care that the school-house and appendages are kept in good order; be accurate; punctual; cheerful; study the art of aptly illustrating a difficult subject; teach honesty and morality, and strive at all times to win the esteem of your pupils.
What things should the teacher avoid ?

He should guard against prejudices; he should ride no "hobbies;" never lose his patience while reason- ing with parents; avoid wounding the feelings of a dull scholar; never compare one child with another; avoid making excuses to visitors; have nothing to do with other business in school hours; do not attempt to teach too many things; do not allow pupils to direct their own studies.
What is good education ?
It is the cultivation of the nobler sentiments of man. It is the art that commands understanding, that subdues his passions, that extends the power of reason and begets a pure conscience.
What is teaching?
It is to communicate knowledge—to give instruction.
What is learning ?
It is to acquire knowledge—to be instructed.
Who will be successful in teaching ?
Those who can comprehend human character, who can control their passions, and who have a love for the business.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Theory And Practice Of Teaching. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Who will fail as a successful teacher ?

Those who teach because of the wages, and at the same time have their mind on other matters of business. A teacher cannot divide his thoughts and feelings. It is a business, or profession, that requires the whole response of the faculties and will admit of no delays or thoughtless acts.
In what divisions are the faculties of the mind comprised?
They comprise three general divisions—the Intellect, the Sensibility and the Will.
What faculty comes first in the natural order of development ?
The development of the intellect.
How is the intellect developed ?
By the acquisition of knowledge.
What are the sources of knowledge ?
They are the Senses and the Reason.
What is empirical knowledge ?
The knowledge of experience, derived through the senses, which includes all that we know through the senses of seeing, hearing, touching, tasting, smelling, and through the emotional feelings.
What is that knowledge called which is derived through reason ?
It is called rational knowledge.
What does this knowledge include ?
All that comes from the power of study and thought. It is the development of the mind and understanding.
What is the difference between teaching and talking ?
One communicates knowledge by a course of reasoning, while talking is familiar or unrestrained conversation.
What is a remedy for whispering ?
Whispering usually originates from a lack of interest. Produce a love for school and its advancement and whispering will be easily controlled.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Theory And Practice Of Teaching. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What benefits arise from the practice of music ?
Music feeds upon the chords of love and builds up a course of friendship and respect. It softens the thoughts and inspires deeper and more sacred feelings.
What are the purposes of examinations ?
To test the knowledge of the pupil and determine if he has made real progress in his studies. It is a review.
What relation does the school hold to the state ?
It is the agent appointed by the state for the purpose of preventing ignorance, raising the standard of moral and literary culture, and increasing the general prosperity.
What conditions does teaching presuppose ?
A certain degree of capacity on the part of the pupil, a degree of knowledge and skill on the part of the teacher, and a knowledge to be acquired.
What are the practical points to be attained by an education ?

It should induce each individual to be more industrious ; more ambitious ; more trustworthy ; more active and systematic ; more cheerful ; more far-sighted ; more economical as producers or preservers of property.
What does education do for a people ?
It tends to make them more orderly ; more submissive to good laws ; more independent in political thoughts ; more charitable ; more refined, and creates a higher plane of civilization.
How should pupils be dealt with at the beginning of their school experience ?
They should be received kindly, and every effort possible should be made to obtain their confidence.
How secure a full, prompt and regular attendance?
By making the school-room pleasant and attractive ; by preserving good order ; by genuine interest on the part of the teacher.
What benefits arise by reciting in concert ?
It stimulates the timid to overcome their diffidence ; it cultivates the voice, and produces uniformity of expression.
How pronounce a word for spelling ?
Exactly as it should be pronounced in reading.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Theory And Practice Of Teaching. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are some of the most common evils of our schools ?
A want of interest on the part of parents and others ; frequent change of teachers ; diversity of class-books ; teachers not qualified ; defective supervision.
What should form the foundation for a system of teaching ?
It should be the capability of the pupil ; the educational means to be employed, and the manner of performing the work.
What is meant by Language Lessons ?
A simple means of expressing the fundamental principles of some line of knowledge.
What is method ?
It is the way of performing an act.
How many methods are there in teaching ?
Six: The Text Book, Oral, Socratic, Topical, Discussion, and Lecture.
What is the Socratic method of instruction ?
A system of questioning.
When should a pupil commit to memory ?
Not until the subject is thoroughly understood. If it cannot be understood it will be folly to commit to memory.
What is the great end of school training ?
It is mental development ; the training of thought towards the practical pursuits of life ; to disperse ignorance and superstition, and to encourage a better mode of living.
Should a teacher confine himself to the text-book during recitation ?

The book should be used as a guide for conducting the recitation, but under no consideration should he depend upon it for the interest which should be given. As a general rule, a book teacher is an uninteresting individual, a telephone that only communicates what the sentence reads.
How much review work is necessary ?
This branch of study cannot be measured. It is too important to be limited, and should be continued in as much as circumstances will allow.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Theory And Practice Of Teaching. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How do you wish to have your class located when reciting ?
They should be brought as closely together as possible, and in as convenient a position to the apparatus of the school-room as is practicable.
What should a person do who contemplates teaching for the first time ?
He should lay out plans in accordance with the best efforts which he can recall in his experience as a scholar, or from other observations.
How do you classify the elements of Pedagogics ?
The special elements are classified as: The Physical, the Intellectual, and the Practical.
What other names are sometimes applied to these elements ?
They are known as Gymnastics, Didactics, and Pragmatics.
Of what does gymnastics treat ?
Of athletic exercises. The training of the muscular system.
Of what does didactics treat?
It is the art or science of teaching.
Of what does pragmatics treat ?
It is the science of business.
Into what divisions is intelligence divided?
We may divide it into Preception, Conception, and Thinking.
What is the preceptive faculty ?
It is the directing of the moral conduct, and is most sensitive to its teachings in the youth.
What is the conceptive faculty ?
The faculty of comprehending an idea.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Theory And Practice Of Teaching. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is thinking ?
It is the faculty of thought, of calculation, of plans and of reasoning.
What are the elements of success in teaching?
It is the executive power, self-control, thorough knowledge, and the faculty of imparting that knowledge.
Should a pupil be required to report on his own conduct? Why?
He should not; it is a strong incentive to report incorrectly and might lead to untruthfulness.
What important branch of study is often neglected in our schools ?
It is spelling.
What common branches should receive special attention ?
Reading, spelling, mental arithmetic and writing.
What constitutes good reading ?
A clear, distinct utterance, interspersed with proper emphasis and expression.
Why would you place mental arithmetic as of special attention ?
Because it requires the use of language to demonstrate; it is a mental education; it cultivates memory and is one of the necessary business principles of life.
How should primary instruction be given ?
Largely by oral explanations.
How should parents talk to their children in infancy ?
Their language should be as simple and as correctly spoken as possible. They should not exaggerate nor be deceiving. Whatever is said, let it be the truth.
What is Kindergarten instruction?

It is the teaching of young children by the use of objects, solids, surfaces and outlines. It is a study by which the eye conveys the idea, rather than the thought. It is much practiced in cities, and considered a fine mode of instruction.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Theory And Practice Of Teaching. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What does the Theory and Practice of Teaching embrace?
It embraces the whole science of education. It trains the mind, develops thought, induces activity and builds up strength and harmony.
Give the names of some of the most important educational reformers.
Aschan, Montaigne, Ratich, Milton, Locke, Rousseau Pestalozzi, Arnold, Mann, Huxley, Spencer and others.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Alcohol And Tobacco. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How and by whom was alcohol discovered?

It is claimed that Paracelsus, a chemist of the fourteenth century, accidentally discovered alcohol, and upon testing its power boasted of having found the essence of life, the power to cure the weak and the great benefactor of mankind.
What is alcohol?
A liquid obtained from the fermentation and distillation of fruits and grains.
What must be the condition of fruits or grains to produce alcohol?
They must pass into a stage of decomposition or rot. It occurs only where sugar or starch is one of the ingredients.
What are the chemical properties of alcohol?

It will not freeze; it burns without smoke; it readily dissolves gums, oils and forms various mixtures; it is used in preserving meats, in perfumery, in thermometers, in varnishes, in spirit lamps, and in various forms of the arts and medicines.
How does alcohol preserve meats, insects or animals?
It absorbs the water in the meat and prevents decay.
What are the various names of liquids which are largely composed of alcohol?
Whisky, brandy, rum and gin. These are the principal forms of distillations, although scores of mixtures are formed.
What are some of the liquids not so largely composed of this fluid?
They are wine, beer, ale and cider.
What is the difference between alcohol and whisky?
Alcohol is the pure spirit and too strong for human drink, while whisky is doctored alcohol to meet the taste.
What is intoxication?
It is drunkenness, a loss of brain power, a condition of mental stupor, a momentary fool.
What is the difference between food and alcohol?
Food nourishes and sustains the body; alcohol excites the nervous system, clouds the intellect, poisons the blood and injures the body.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Alcohol And Tobacco. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the difference between fermentation and distillation?

Fermentation is the simple process of nature on the juices of fruits and grains. Distillation is the process of taking out the alcohol found in fermentation and is much stronger. sciousness they were very sick, and an emetic was given to them which brought up their Saturday's dinner just as it had gone into their stomach. The food had been preserved by the alcohol and relief could only come by taking an emetic.
Why do some people take a glass of liquor before eating?

Simply to gratify their taste. The idea that it is beneficial to the appetite is absurd and ridiculous. The plea is an excuse to cover up a practice they know to be not only dangerous morally, but a crime against the physical system.
What disease is liable to attack the person who continues in drinking? 25. What effect do contagious diseases have upon habitual drinkers ? Why ?
It is the terrible affliction, dyspepsia.
What causes the redness of the nose and skin ?

The skin is called into greater action to throw off a part of the alcohol taken into the system. The pores and blood-vessels of the skin become larger in this exertion, and the quantity of blood gives the color which is hung outside the body as a danger-signal against the use of the fiery poison which burns within.
What does alcohol do to the heart ?
It overworks it, makes it tired, loads it with fat and weakens the power of action.
What does it do to the blood-vessels ?
It stimulates the circulation, hurries the blood in its course, stretches the small arteries and makes them unfit for work.
How does it act upon the lungs ?

It causes them to work too fast, it heats and inflames them, it hardens the walls of the air-cells, it prevents the air from purifying the blood and like all other parts of the body this organ is polluted with the presence of this slow but deadly poison.
Describe how it affects the brain ?

Portions of the nerves, spinal cord and brains are composed of a white substance called albumen (the white of an egg); when alcohol comes in contact with this substance it absorbs the water and thickens the albumen, which action paralyzes these organs and they lose their power of action, the limbs cannot carry the body, the brain refuses to understand, and he who was created in the image of his Creator sinks into a drunken stupor.
Why does a drunken man have imperfect vision ?
The nerves of the eye are strained by the excitement and stupor and do not report to the brain a correct vision.
How does alcohol injure the liver ?
By hardening and preventing its action in removing the waste matters that should be carried off.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Alcohol And Tobacco. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How does it affect the kidneys ?
The kidneys become fatty in structure, wax-like in condition, and other conditions leading to dropsy.
How does it injure the mind ?
Whatever injures the brain, weakens the power of thought and destroys the intellect.
How do we know that alcohol is a poison ?

If taken in sufficient quantities it almost immediately produces death. In such cases the animal has all of the appearance of poison. Nature declares that it is a poison, although by slow and constant use the system becomes used to its presence, holding in check the slow fire that is impairing the constitution, producing a poisoned blood, diseased kidneys, indigestion and the slow destruction of the mind, the brain, the heart, the nerves and the very soul of man.
Do habitual drinkers crave for the taste of liquors ?

It is often the case that the first glass in the morning is hard to swallow; it is nauseating to the stomach, and the old toper braces his physical powers and with a gulp it is gone. Why, you may ask, does he seek to do that which his system loathes and often rejects? It is for the effect; no longer is it a question of the palate and taste. There is a demand to rouse the sinking forces, to stimulate, to overcome the deadness of the heart and to produce a new excitement.
Does alcohol protect from cold ?

It imparts a warmth only by imagination. No new heat is infused into the system, but by quickened pulsation the blood rushes over the body and whatever heat is imparted to the outer surface is at the general expense of the internal heat and when this quickened pulsation returns to its normal condition the body has lost more heat and has become weaker in condition than it would have been if the excitement had not been given.
What is the experience of sailors in cases of ship-wreck and great exposure ?
It is the universal verdict that those who take liquor under these circumstances do not withstand the cold equal to those who refuse to drink.
How could alcohol be used under such circumstances and become a benefit to those thus exposed ?
By turning it into their boots or against the parts liable to freeze. This would be a means of outward protection by which the internal was not disturbed.
Knowing the evil effects of drink, why do people continue to harbor its presence, protect its sale and defend its influence ?

It is a mystery which remains to be explained, but whatever view may be taken in regard to this evil, the fact remains that its influence is increasing, its effects are more dangerous, and its power more grasping. It is insolent in character, selfish in desires and damning in its touch. It is a curse that follows, more blighting than fire and more terrible than disease. It breathes desolation, misery and despair. It is without love, home or friendship. Its aim is to destroy the soul of man and to mock at virtue, truth and manhood. It smiles to deceive, and its deceit is but the sting of death. It is robed in the blood-money of all the crimes of the earth. It spares none who yield to its embrace, and it seeks to embrace all. It is the hell of all hells, the monster of all monsters and the fire of all fires. Its curse cannot be comprehended, nor the innocent tears of its victims be measured. But, however sad may be the picture of its pathway, man, the being it brings to the lowest depths of degradation, still gives its infamy license, and as his pen signs the right to scatter woe, misery and destruction, he lifts his eyes and cries unto his Maker, "O Lord, thy will be done!" Such mockery! Such sacrilege to the Good of all Goodness! Why, oh why, does not the wrath of God blot it from the face of the earth?
When was tobacco discovered and when introduced to the people of civilization ?
It was discovered by Columbus in 1492. It was taken to Spain in 1496; to France in 1560; to England in 1586.
What is the difference in effect between alcohol and tobacco ?
One excites, while the other soothes the nerves and often produces sleep.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Alcohol And Tobacco. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What poisonous elements are found in tobacco smoke ?
They are carbonic acid, carbonic oxide, ammonia and nicotine.
What is the effect of the carbonic acid ?
It causes sleepiness and headache.
What are the effects from carbonic oxide ?
It causes trembling of the muscles and heart.
What does the ammonia produce ?
It bites the tongue and makes too much work for the salivary glands.
What is nicotine ?
It is a very poisonous element, strong in odor, bitter, and is the principal pain-soothing and sleep-producing portion of tobacco. It is a narcotic.
What does nicotine produce ?
It produces weakness, nervousness, dizziness, nausea, faintness, loss of strength, stupor, and if taken in large quantities it produces convulsions and death.
Give some of the actions of tobacco on the human body ?

It poisons the saliva. Injures the sense of smell, taste, sight and hearing. Causes "smokers' sore-throat." Injures the stomach, causing dyspepsia, etc. Often takes away the appetite for wholesome food. Irritates the air-cells of the lungs. Causes palpitation of the heart. Weakens the muscles, causing trembling. Injures the eyes. Stupefies the brain and nerves.
Give some of its actions on the mind ?
It makes the memory poor. Lessens the power to think. Weakens the will. Makes people grow in selfishness and impoliteness. Often leads to bad company. Sometimes causes insanity.
What forms of smoking are most injurious ?
The habit of smoking cigarettes. The reason for this is that they contain more nicotine and often opium.
What is opium ?
It is the juice obtained from the seed-vessel of the white poppy before they are ripe. When dried, it is somewhat dark in color and bitter to the taste.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Alcohol And Tobacco. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Is it dangerous to use ?

It is the most dangerous of drugs when used as a habit, as few can resist its influence when once the system has become accustomed to it. Its first effects are pleasant and exhilarating but the after work is one of wretched misery and can be relieved only by another portion; thus the habit becomes a cruel and unrelenting slavery.
What is laudanum and morphine ?
They are made from opium, and should be used only under the care of a physician.
What is hashish ?
It is a very powerful narcotic, prepared from the hemp plant grown in hot countries. It is used quite extensively among the people of warm climates, and is a terribly exciting poison.
What is the areca nut ? Where found ?

It is the seed from a kind of palm, and resembles a nutmeg. It is mixed with quick-lime and wrapped in a betel-leaf, which grows on a vine belonging to the pepper family. This mixture reddens the saliva and lips and blackens the teeth. It is chewed by millions of people in India.
What caution should be observed in regard to the use of these liquids and drugs ?
Touch not, taste not, handle not. They are all poisonous and mean the same thing— A little pleasure, disease, and DEATH.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Civil Government. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is a constitution ?
The established form of government in a state, kingdom or country.
What is a preamble ?
A formal and explanatory introduction.
What are legislative powers, and in what are they vested ?
The right to make and enforce laws, and are vested in a Congress of the United States.
What is Congress ?
An assemblage, especially applied to the two houses of legislative power in the United States.
What is the Senate ? House of Representatives?
The Senate is the upper house of a legislature, or Congress. The House of Representatives is the lower branch of a legislature, or Congress.
How are senators and representatives elected, and for how long a term of office ?

Senators are elected by the legislatures of the states for a term of six years, each state being entitled to two; representatives are elected by the people for a term of two years, each state being represented according to its population.
How old must a representative be, and how long a citizen of the United States?
Twenty-five years, and seven years a citizen.
What is an elector ?
One entitled to vote.
How are representatives apportioned among the states ?
According to the population of the states.
What is census ? How often taken ?

An official enumeration of the people, with the statement of their property, pursuits, age, etc. It is to be taken once in ten years, which forms a new basis of apportioning the number of representatives to the several states.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Civil Government. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
If vacancies happen in the representation of any state, how is that vacancy filled ?
By the executive authority of the state.
What are writs of election ?
A writing issued to an officer or officers to give notice of some election to be held.
What officers does the House of Representatives choose ?
The speaker, chief clerk, and sergeant-at-arms.
What sole power has this branch of Congress ?
The sole power of impeachment.
What is impeachment ?
An accusation or charge brought against a public officer for misconduct in office.
Into how many classes are the senators divided ?
Three: One class being elected every two years.
Who is president of the Senate? Can he vote?
The Vice-President of the United States, and is entitled to the casting vote.
Should a vacancy occur in the Senate, what is to be done ?
The executive of the state may make a temporary appointment, until the next meeting of the legislature.
How old must a senator be, and how long a citizen of the United States ?
Thirty years of age, and nine years a citizen.
Who becomes president of the Senate when the Vice-President is absent ?
The Senate elects one pro tem.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Civil Government. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What sole power belongs to the Senate?
The sole power to try all impeachments.
How may the President of the United States be punished for crime in office?

He shall be impeached by the House of Representatives, and tried by the Senate, sitting for that purpose, on oath or affirmation, and the Chief Justice shall preside. The conviction shall be by the concurrence of two-thirds of the members present.
What judgment can the Senate declare against a convicted President?
It shall not extend further than to removal from office, and disqualification to hold and enjoy any office of honor, trust, or profit under the United States.
If an officer is removed from office by an impeachment trial, is he subject to the common law?
He is liable and subject to indictment, trial, judgment, and punishment, according to law.
What is a judgment? Indictment?
Judgment is the sentence pronounced by the judge or court by which the person is tried. Indictment is a written accusation of crime made by a grand jury.
What is an oath? An affirmation?
An oath is a solemn declaration, made with an appeal to God for the truth of what is affirmed. An affirmation is a solemn declaration, made by one who is unwilling to take an oath.
What officers does the Senate choose?
They choose all of their officers except the president of the Senate, but when absent or exercising the office of President of the United States, one shall be chosen pro tempore.
What is the meaning of pro tempore?
For the time being.
Who appoints the times, places, and manner of holding elections of senators and representatives? What may Congress do?
It is prescribed in each state by the legislature thereof; but Congress may at any time by law make or alter such regulations, except as to the places of choosing senators.
If disputes arise in election returns, how are they to be decided?
Each House shall be the judge of the elections, returns, and qualifications of its own members.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Civil Government. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How many does it require to do business? What may a smaller number do?
A majority; but a smaller number may compel the attendance of absent members, in such a manner and under such penalties as each House may provide.
What power has each House over its members?
It may punish its members for disorderly behavior, and with the concurrence of two-thirds, expel a member.
Can either House adjourn at pleasure?
Not for more than three days, without the consent of the other House.
Shall each House keep a journal of its proceedings?
It shall, and print the same, unless secrecy is required.
When shall the yeas and noes be entered on the journal?
When one-fifth of those present shall require it.
When shall Congress assemble? Can it assemble at any other time?
The first Monday in December; but it may be convened by the President, when necessary, or Congress may adjourn from time to time.
How do members receive compensation?
By law; and are paid out of the treasury of the United States.
Can a member be arrested while Congress is in session?
Only for felony, treason, or breach of the peace.
For what shall they not be questioned in any other place?
For any speech or debate in Congress.
Can a person hold an office under the United States and be a member of Congress?
He cannot.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Civil Government. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Where shall bills for raising revenue originate?
In the House of Representatives.
What is revenue? What is a bill?
Revenue is the income of government, arising from taxes, duties, etc. A bill is a formal writing presented to the legislature for enactment.
When does a bill become a law?

By passing both Houses of Congress, and receiving the sanction of the President; but if returned within ten days, Sundays excepted, with his objections, it does not become a law, unless reconsidered and passed by a two-thirds vote of both Houses.
What question does not require the sanction of the President?

The question of adjournment, except in case of disagreement between the two Houses with respect to the time of adjournment, when the President can adjourn them to such a time as he shall think proper.
What are the powers of Congress?

Section 8. The Congress shall have power to lay and collect taxes, duties, imposts, and excises, to pay the debts and provide for the common defense and general welfare of the United States, but all duties, imposts, and excises shall be uniform throughout the United States; to borrow money on the credit of the United States; to regulate commerce with foreign nations, and among the several states, and with the Indian tribes; to establish a uniform rule of naturalization, and uniform laws on the subject of bankruptcies throughout the United States; to coin money, regulate the value thereof, and of foreign coin, and fix the standard of weights and measures; to provide for the punishment of counterfeiting the securities and current coin of the United States; to establish post-offices and post-roads; to promote the progress of science and the useful arts, by securing for limited times to authors and inventors the exclusive right to their respective writings and discoveries; to constitute tribunals inferior to the Supreme Court; to define and punish piracies and felonies committed on the high seas, and offenses against the law of nations; to declare war, grant letters of marque and reprisal, and make rules concerning captures on land and water; to raise and support armies, but no appropriation of money to that use shall be made for a longer term than two years; to provide and maintain a navy; to make rules for the government and regulation of the land and naval forces; to provide for calling forth the militia to execute the laws of the Union, suppress insurrections, and repel invasions; to provide for organizing, arming, and disciplining the militia, and for governing such part of them as may be employed in the service of the United States, reserving to the states respectively, the appointment of the officers, and the authority of training the militia according to the discipline prescribed by Congress; to exercise exclusive legislation in all cases whatsoever, over such district (not exceeding ten miles square) as may, by cession of particular states, and the acceptance of Congress, become the seat of government of the United States, and exercise like authority over all places purchased by the consent of the legislature of the state in which the same shall be, for the erection of forts, magazines, arsenals, dockyards, and other needful buildings;—and to make all laws which shall be necessary and proper for carrying into execution the foregoing powers and all others vested by this constitution in the government of the United States, or any department or officer thereof.
What is meant by high seas? By law of nations?
The law of nations, the accepted or universal laws of all nations.
What are letters of marque and reprisals?
Written authority from government to seize the property of an enemy, especially vessels and cargoes.
What are duties, imports, and excises?
Duty, a tax levied on imports. Imports, goods brought from other countries. Excise, a tax on anything made, used, or sold.
What is the militia?
The citizens of a state liable to military duty enrolled for service.
Over what district may Congress exercise exclusive legislation?
The District of Columbia.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Civil Government. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are forts? Magazines? Arsenals? Dock-yards?
Fort, a place of defense. Magazine, a store where ammunition is kept. Arsenal, a repository for arms and military stores. Dockyard, a place for containing all kinds of naval stores and timber.
What is the writ of habeas corpus? When can it be suspended?
A writ for delivering a person from false imprisonment, and can only be suspended in cases of treason, or when the public safety requires it.
What is a bill of attainder? Is it allowed?

A special act of the legislature inflicting capital punishment upon persons supposed to be guilty of high crimes, without the process of law. It is not allowed by the constitution of the United States.
What is an ex post facto law?
A law making an act criminal which was not so when done. It is not allowed.
Can a tax or duty be laid on articles exported from any state?
It cannot.
What is prohibited in regard to nobility and the receiving of presents?

No title of nobility shall be granted by the United States; and no person holding an office of profit or trust under them shall, without the consent of Congress, accept of any present, emolument, office or title of any kind whatever, from any king, prince or foreign state.
Can any state enter into any treaty, alliance, or confederation?
It cannot.
Can a state engage in war?
Not unless in imminent danger from invasion.
What are the states forbidden to do in regard to imposts or duties on imports or exports?
No state shall, without an act of Congress, lay any imposts or duties on imports or exports, except what may be absolutely necessary for executing its inspection laws.
In whom is the executive power of the United States vested?
In a President.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Civil Government. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Who are eligible to this office?
Any citizen born in the United States, thirty-five years of age, and fourteen years a resident.
How are the President and Vice-President elected?

By electors chosen by the people. The electors shall meet in their respective states and vote by distinct ballots for President and Vice-President. These votes are sealed and transmitted to the seat of government of the United States, directed to the president of the Senate. The president of the Senate shall, in the presence of the Senate and House of Representatives, open all the certificates, and the votes shall then be counted; the person having the greatest number of votes for President shall be President, if such number be a majority of the whole number of electors appointed; and if no person have such majority, then from the persons having the highest number, not exceeding three on the list of those voted for as President, the House of Representatives shall choose immediately, by ballot, the President. The person having the greatest number of votes as Vice-President shall be the Vice-President, if such number be a majority of the whole number of electors appointed, and if no person have a majority, then from the two highest numbers on the list, the Senate shall choose the Vice-President.
Supposing the House of Representatives fail to elect a President whenever the right of choice shall devolve upon them, who shall act as President?
The Vice-President shall act as President in the case of the death or other constitutional disability of the President.
What may Congress determine in regard to the time of choosing the electors?
Congress may determine the time of choosing the electors, and the day on which they shall give their vote; which day shall be the same throughout the United States.
When does the Vice-President act as the chief magistrate?
In case of the removal of the President from office, or of his death, resignation, or inability to discharge the powers and duties of the said office.
How is the President compensated for his services?

The President shall, at stated times, receive for his services a compensation which shall neither be increased nor diminished during the period for which he shall have been elected, and he shall not receive within that period any other emolument from the United States, or any of them.
What oath must he take before he enters upon the execution of his office?

"I do solemnly swear (or affirm) that I will faithfully execute the office of President of the United States, and will, to the best of my ability preserve, protect, and defend the Constitution of the United States."
Who is commander-in-chief of the army and navy?
The President.
What offense is he not permitted to pardon?
Cases of impeachment.
Does he have a right to make treaties, appoint ambassadors, judges of the Supreme Court, and officers of the United States?

He has the right, with the advice and consent of the Senate, to make treaties, provided two-thirds of the senators present concur, and with the advice and consent of the Senate he shall appoint ambassadors, ministers, judges, and officers of the United States, and all appointments which shall be established by law.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Civil Government. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
If a vacancy occurs during a recess of Congress, how is it filled?
By an appointment by the President.
What is the President's message?
An article directed to Congress, stating the condition of the country, and proposing such measures as he may judge necessary and expedient.
For what crimes can the President, Vice-President, and all civil officers of the United States be impeached?
For treason, bribery, and other high crimes and misdemeanors.
What is treason? Bribery?

Treason against the United States shall consist only in levying war against them, or in adhering to their enemies, giving them aid and comfort. Bribery is giving money or some other consideration to procure desired legislation, a judicial decision, or other official action.
In what is the judicial power of the United States vested?
In one Supreme Court and such inferior courts as Congress may from time to time establish.
How long do the judges of the Supreme Court hold office?
During good behavior, or life.
To what cases does their power extend?
In all cases arising under the Constitution, the laws of the United States, and cases that cannot be settled in the Supreme Court of any state.
In what cases has the Supreme Court original jurisdiction ?

In all cases affecting ambassadors, other public ministers and consuls, and those in which a state shall be a party. In other cases, the Supreme Court shall have appellate jurisdiction, or jurisdiction by an appeal from lower courts.
Where shall a criminal be tried ?
In the state where the crime is committed.
If the crime is not committed in the United States, where must the trial be held ?
At such a place or places as Congress may by law have directed.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Civil Government. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What crimes are not tried by jury ?
Crimes subject to impeachment.
What power declares the punishment of treason ? How shall a person be convicted ?
Congress. No person shall be convicted of treason unless on the testimony of two witnesses to the same overt act, or on confession in open court.
What is meant by, "but no attainder of treason shall work corruption of blood or forfeiture, except during the life of the person attainted" ?
That no postity or person shall be denied any rights because of the punishment or conviction of another.
What faith and credit shall be given each state ?
Full faith and credit shall be given in each state to the public acts, records, and judicial proceedings of every other state.
Can a person charged with crime be taken from another state ?
On demand of the executive authority of the state where the crime was committed.
How are new states admitted into the Union ?
By consent of Congress.
How can new states be formed ?
From territories having 30,000 inhabitants, or from states or parts of states with the consent of the states concerned.
What power has Congress in regard to the territory and other property belonging to the United States ?
Congress shall have power to dispose of and make all needful rules and regulations respecting it.
What shall the United States guarantee to every state in the Union ?
A republican form of government and protection.
What is the supreme law of the land ? Who are bound by oath to support it ?
The constitution and laws of the United States. All government officers and representatives are bound by oath to support it.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Civil Government. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Is a religious test required as a qualification to any office or public trust ?
No religious test shall ever be required as a qualification to any office or public trust under the United States.
How may the constitution be amended ?
By two-thirds vote of both Houses of Congress, or by the action of the legislatures of two-thirds of the states, ratified in either case by three-fourths of all the states.
How many amendments to the constitution ? When were the first ten adopted ?
Fifteen; the first ten were adopted at the first session of Congress.
What did Congress grant in the first amendment to the constitution ?
Freedom of religion, of speech, of the press, the right of the people peaceably to assemble, and to petition the government for a redress of grievances.
What privilege was given the states in the second amendment?
The right of the people to keep and bear arms.
What is the law respecting the quartering of soldiers in any house in the time of peace ?
No soldier shall, in the time of peace, be quartered in any house without the consent of the owner, nor in time of war, but in a manner to be prescribed by law.
What rights are secured to the people in the fourth amendment?
The right of the people to be secure in their persons, houses, papers, and effects, against unreasonable searches and seizures.
Can a person be twice put in jeopardy of life ?
Nor shall any person be subject for the same offense to be twice put in jeopardy of life or limb.
Can a person be compelled to be a witness against himself?
He cannot.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Civil Government. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the law respecting the taking of private property ?
If taken for public use, he shall have just compensation.
In the sixth amendment, what right shall the accused enjoy in all criminal prosecutions ?

A speedy and public trial, by an impartial jury; to be confronted with the witnesses against him; to have compulsory process for obtaining witnesses in his favor, and to have the assistance of counsel for his defense.
When shall the right of trial by jury be preserved ?
In suits at common law, where the value in controversy shall exceed twenty dollars.
What shall not be required, nor imposed, nor inflicted, in the eighth amendment?
Excessive bail shall not be required, nor excessive fines imposed, nor cruel and unusual punishment inflicted.
In the tenth amendment, what powers are reserved to the states respectively, or to the people ?
The powers not delegated to the United States by the constitution, nor prohibited by it to the states, are reserved to the states respectively, or to the people.
What is the thirteenth amendment ?

Neither slavery nor involuntary servitude, except as a punishment for crime, whereof the party shall have been duly convicted, shall exist within the United States, or any place subject to their jurisdiction.
In the fourteenth amendment, who is forbidden from holding certain offices ? How may the disability be removed?

Persons who have held office under the United States, or of any state, and shall have been engaged in insurrection or rebellion against the same, or given aid or comfort to the enemies thereof. Congress may, by a vote of two-thirds of each House, remove such disability.
What is said of the public debt ? What shall be held as illegal and void ?

The validity of the public debt of the United States, authorized by law, shall not be questioned. Any debt or obligation incurred in aid of insurrection or rebellion against the United States, or any claim for the loss or emancipation of any slave, shall be held illegal and void.
What is the fifteenth amendment ?
The right of the citizens of the United States to vote shall not be denied or abridged by the United States or any state on account of race, color, or previous condition of servitude.
What two important measures have been passed by the 48th and 49th Congress to fill a possible deficiency in the constitution ?
A bill for counting the electoral vote, and the Presidential succession bill.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Civil Government. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the law in regard to the Presidential succession ?

"In case of removal, death, resignation, or inability of both the President and Vice-President, a member of the Cabinet shall, in the following order, act as President until the disability is removed or a President elected: The Secretary of State, Secretary of the Treasury, Secretary of War, Attorney General, Postmaster General, Secretary of the Navy, Secretary of the Interior; provided, that whenever the powers or duties of the office of President of the United States shall devolve upon any of the persons named therein, if Congress be not then in session, or if it would not meet in accordance with law within twenty days thereafter, it shall be the duty of the person upon whom the said powers and duties shall devolve, to issue a proclamation convening Congress in extraordinary session, giving twenty days' notice of the time of meeting." (Approved January 19, 1886.)

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physiology, Anatomy And Hygiene. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is Physiology?
The science of the organic functions of animals or plants.
Illustrate the value of understanding the knowledge of physiology.

By a proper knowledge of this subject lives may be saved, strength preserved, and life lengthened. In case of accidents, as of poison, drowning or bodily injuries, this knowledge may be of great value to us in relieving sickness and distress.
Into what two bodies is nature divided?
Into organic and inorganic bodies.
Define organic bodies.
They are those bodies which possess organs, on whose actions depend their growth and perfection. It includes minerals and plants.
Define inorganic bodies.
They are devoid of organs, or instruments of life, as the earth's minerals, etc.
What is Anatomy?
The science of the structure of animal bodies.
What is Hygiene?
That branch of medicine which treats of the preservation of health.
What law is impressed on all animal beings?
The law of constant change.
What is the difference between food and living flesh?
Food is the material we eat, changed by the action of the system into living flesh.
How are plants nourished?
They are nourished by the inorganic elements found in the earth and air around them. This material is received in the form of liquid or a gas, already prepared for their use.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physiology, Anatomy And Hygiene. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How are animals nourished?
They are nourished by the organic materials of vegetables or of other animals.
What do animals always possess?
They possess a stomach or digestive cavity, in which the food is prepared before it can be absorbed by the machinery of the body.
What functions are peculiar to animals alone?
Sensation and voluntary motion, and are, therefore, called animal functions.
What organs constitute the digestive apparatus?
The mouth, pharynx, œsophagus, stomach, pancreas, and intestinal canal.
What difference in the teeth of the carnivorous and herbivorous animals?

The carnivorous have sharp front teeth, and long, sharp and pointed canine teeth, and grinders with high and sharpened points. The herbivorous have short, blunt, and strong front teeth, small canine teeth, but their molars are very large, broad and flat, with slightly raised lines on their surface.
How many teeth has man, and how divided?
Sixteen in each jaw : four incisors in the center ; one canine next to the incisors, on each side ; then two bicuspids, and three molars.
Of what are teeth composed?
They are composed of soft bone within, but are covered on the outside with an enamel of very hard texture, which admits of an exquisitely fine polish.
How are the teeth set in the jaw?
With long fangs, so they are not easily started from their places.
What are salivary glands? What is their function?
They are glands which empty their fluids into the mouth. Their function is to secrete fluid which moistens the food during mastication, and assists, in a measure, in digestion.
Where are the salivary glands situated?

The two larger ones, situated between the ear and upper part of the jaw, are called parotid glands. Two smaller ones, situated about half way between the symphysis, or center, are called submaxillary. Smaller ones still, underneath the tongue, are called sublingual.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Physiology, Anatomy And Hygiene. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Describe the pharynx, or second chamber of the mouth.

It is separated from the mouth by the palate. Four passages open out of it : one leads forward to the mouth ; a second, upward to the nose ; a third, downward to the lungs ; and a fourth, backward to the stomach.
What is the epiglottis? 23. Describe the oesophagus.
A small valve placed over the mouth of the trachea, admitting air, but excluding all foreign substances.
How does food find its way to the stomach ?

Upon being forced back in the mouth to the pharynx it causes, by its presence, the muscles of the pharynx to contract upon it. This is the act of swallowing, and is beyond the control of the will. The circular fibers of the muscles of the oesophagus seize upon the morsel of food, and contracting, force it downward until it reaches the stomach.
Describe the stomach.

It is an oval-shaped sac, situated below the dia-phragm on the same plane with the liver, and lies mostly on the left side of the spine. It has two openings; an up-per, or cardiac orifice, where the oesophagus enters; and a lower, or pyloric orifice, which enters into the duodenum. It is composed of three coats: an outer or serous coat, which is the same as that which lines the whole abdomen; a middle, or muscular coat, composed of two layers of fibers, longitudinal and circular, the contraction of which move the food about the stomach during digestion; and an inner, or mucous coat, containing the gastric tubules, which secrete the gastric juice.
By what is the food dissolved in the stomach ?
A powerful fluid called the gastric juice.
Describe the process of digestion in the stomach ?

The presence of food in the stomach causes the gastric juice to be thrown out. The muscles of the stomach move the food about, which causes the gastric juice to be thoroughly intermingled with it. The gastric juice re-duces the food to a pulpy mass, which occurs usually in three or four hours, and is then passed out of the stomach at the pyloric orifice.
Of what is gastric juice composed?
It is composed of an acid, supposed to be lactic or hydrochloric acid, and an organic substance called pepsin.
Name the fluids of the body.
They are the blood, chyle, lymph, saliva, gastric juice, pancreatic juice, synovia, mucus, and serum. Bile, sweat, and urine are excretions.
Name the solids of the body.
The solids are the bones, teeth, cartilages, ligaments, muscles, nerves, vessels, viscera, membranes, skin, hair, and nails.
Give the list of chemical elements in the human body.
They are oxygen, hydrogen, carbon, sodium, potas-sium, chlorine, nitrogen, sulphur, phosphorus, calcium, magnesium, fluorine, silicon, iron, manganese.
