The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Natural Philosophy. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Why can a fly walk upon the ceiling of a room ?
The feet of the fly are so arranged as to produce a vacuum by which the pressure holds the foot until another step is taken.
Define Acoustics.
Acoustics treat of the philosophy of sound.
How is sound transmitted ?
It is transmitted by the vibrations of the air.
How do we prove that air is necessary to transmit sound ?
A bell rung in a vacuum will make no noise.
Upon what does the velocity of sound depend ?
It depends upon the elasticity and density of the medium through which it passes.
Explain why an elastic medium will carry the sound waves faster than one not so elastic.
The higher the elasticity the more promptly and rapidly do the wave sounds transmit its motion from one set of molecules to another.
How fast does sound travel ?
At the freezing point sound travels at the rate of 1,090 feet per second. In a rise of temperature it travels a little faster.
How fast does sound travel in water?
It travels through water at the rate of 4,700 feet per second.
Why does it travel so much faster in water?
Because of the greater elasticity of water.
What is refraction of sound ?
When a sound-wave goes obliquely from one medium to another it is bent out of its course, or refracted.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Natural Philosophy. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the reflection of sound?
Sound is reflected when the sound-waves strike against the surface of another medium, and a portion goes on while the rest is reflected.
What are echoes ?
They are simply reflected sounds.
How can two sounds produce silence?

If two sounds meet in exactly opposite phases, and the two forces are equal, they will balance each other, and silence will ensue. Thus, a sound added to a sound will produce silence, and in the same way, two motions may produce rest; two lights may cause darkness; and two heats may produce cold.
How is the sound produced in wind instruments ?
In wind instruments the sound-waves run backward and forward through the tube, and act upon the surrounding air like the vibrations of a cord.
What are Harmonics ?

Whenever a cord vibrates it separates into segments at the same time. Thus, we have the full or fundamental note of the entire string, and superposed upon it the higher notes produced by the vibrating parts. These are called overtones or harmonics.
What is the Phonograph ?
It is an instrument for recording sound vibrations.
What is the limit of vibrations for musical sounds ?
It is fixed at 38,000 vibrations per second for the highest, and 16 for the lowest. Below this the sounds are separate.
Define Optics.
Optics is that science which treats of light and visions.
What is a luminous body ? A non-luminous?
A luminous body is one that sends forth light. A non-luminous body is one that reflects light, but is visible only in the presence of a luminous body.
Define a medium. A transparent body.
A medium is any substance through which light passes. A transparent body is so clear that it does not obstruct much of the light which passes through it.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Natural Philosophy. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Give three laws of light.
Light passes off from a luminous body equally in every direction. Light passes through a uniform medium in a straight line. The intensity of light decreases as the square of the distance increases.
What is the velocity of light ?
It is estimated at 186,000 miles per second.
Define a translucent body. An opaque body.
A translucent body is one that lets some light pass through it.
Explain the undulatory theory of light.

There is supposed to be a fluid termed ether, constituting a kind of universal atmosphere, diffused through all space. It is so subtle that it fills the pores of all bodies, eludes all chemical tests, passes through the glass receiver, and remains even in a vacuum.
Name and define the three kinds of mirrors.
They are plane, concave, and convex. The first has a flat surface ; the second is raised on the inside ; the third is raised on the outside, like a watch crystal.
What is the general principle of mirrors ?
The general principle is that the image is always seen in the direction of the reflected ray as it enters the eye.
What is the effect of looking through a concave mirror ?

If standing near the glass the face or object is magnified, but walk backwards and the size decreases until we reach the focus, when it disappears ; but still receding from the focus, the image again appears, but inverted.
What is mirage ? Give the cause of mirage.

Mirage is an optical delusion, by which the mind really believes certain objects, as water, trees, vessels, etc., are in sight, when in reality the refraction and reflection of the rays of light traversing layers of air of unequal density, bring a reflection of these objects before our vision, although many miles away. This phenomena has often been observed by mariners and travelers on large plains or deserts, and because of the delusion which it brings forth it is pronounced an optical delusion, when it is only an image reflected from beyond.
Name the seven primary colors of the solar spectrum.
They are violet, indigo blue, green, yellow, orange red.
What is the spectroscope?

It is an instrument for examining a ray of light. The ray of light passes through a tube striking a prism, which in turn is taken up by an object glass, and being thus largely magnified, is easily analyzed by our vision.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Natural Philosophy. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How is the rainbow formed?
It is formed by the refraction and reflection of the sunbeam in drops of falling water. The water is a prism which gives out the reflection.
How are halos formed ?

Halos, coronas, sundogs, circles about the moon, the display of the reflection of sunrise and sunset, are produced by the refraction and reflection of the sun's rays by the clouds. The familiar phenomenon of the " sun's drawing water" consists merely of the long shadows of broken clouds.
Explain spherical aberration.

Rays which pass through a lens near the edge are brought to a focus sooner than those nearer the center ; therefore, when an image is clear around the edge, it will be indistinct at the center, and vice versa. This wandering of the rays from the focus is termed spherical aberration.
Explain chromatic aberration.

Chromatic aberration is caused by the different refrangibility of the several colors which compose white light. The violet being bent most, tends to come to a focus sooner than the red, which is bent least. This causes the play of colors seen around the image produced by an ordinary glass.
What is the remedy?
It is remedied by using a second lens of different dispersive power, which counteracts the effects of the first.
Describe the microscope.
Microscopes are of two kinds—simple and compound. The former consists of a double convex lens; the latter contains at least two lenses.
Describe the telescope.

Telescopes are of two kinds—reflecting and refracting. The former contains a large metallic mirror which reflects the rays of light to a focus ; the latter, like the telescope, contains an object-lens, which forms an image.
Describe the opera glass.

The opera-glass contains an optic-glass and an eye-piece. The latter is a double concave lens ; this increases the visual angle by diverging the rays of light, which would otherwise come to a focus beyond the eye-piece.
Describe the camera.
The camera used by photographers contains a double convex lens which throws an inverted image of the object upon the ground-glass screen.
Why does a blow on the head make one "see stars?"
Because the blow excites the optic nerve, and so produces the sensation of light.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Natural Philosophy. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Define luminous heat. Obscure heat.
Luminous heat is that which radiates from a luminous body. Obscure heat is that from a non-luminous source.
Define diathermanous body. What is cold?
A diathermanous body is one which allows the heat to pass through it readily. Cold is a term indicating the absence of heat in a greater or less degree.
What are the sources of heat?
They are the sun, stars, mechanical and chemical forces.
Can force be destroyed?
It cannot. If destroyed in one form it reappears in another without loss.
Give the theory of vaporization.

If heat be applied to a liquid, the temperature rises until the boiling point is reached, when it stops. The expansion, however, continues until the motion is so violent as to overcome the cohesive force and to throw off particles of the liquid.
Why does it require a higher temperature to boil salt water than it does fresh?
Anything which adds to the cohesive power of water elevates the boiling point.
Define evaporation.

Evaporation is a slow formation of vapor, which takes place at all ordinary temperatures. It is hastened by an increase of surface and heat. This principle is made useful in the arts for separating a solid from the liquid which holds it in solution.
What is a vacuum-pan?

It is a mode of boiling liquids at a low temperature. The pan is made with an air-tight cover and the air is exhausted above the liquid, by which means, the pressure being taken from off the liquid, it commences to boil at a low temperature, and but little danger of burning. This is used in making sugar, etc.
What is the relation between absorption and reflection?

Absorption and reflection are intimately connected with radiation. A good absorber is also a good radiator, but a good reflector can be neither. Snow is a good reflector, but a poor absorber or radiator. Light colors absorb less and reflect more than dark colors. White is the best reflector, and black the best absorber and radiator.
Why does ice sometime form at night on the Desert of Sahara?
The extreme dryness of the air allows the heat to escape so fast that cold is produced. This principle is known to chemists, and is one of the agents to produce artificial ice in our ice-machines.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Natural Philosophy. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Describe the steam-engine.

There are two classes of engines—the high pressure and the low pressure. In the first mentioned the steam, after being used as a direct power, escapes to the open air; in the low pressure the steam is condensed by a spray of cold water and a vacuum formed behind the piston. Engines of the high pressure are used on railroads and wherever there is a plentiful supply of water. The other is used where it is economy to condense the steam and save the water for using again.
Describe the governor.

The governor is an apparatus for regulating the supply of steam. When the machine goes too fast, the balls fly out by centrifugal force and shut off a portion of the steam; when too slowly, they fall back, and, opening the valve, let on the steam again.
What is the object of a fly-wheel?
It serves to give a uniform motion to the machinery.
What is meteorology?
It is that department of philosophy which treats of the atmosphere and its phenomena, particularly in its relation to heat and moisture. 147. They differ only in their elevation in the atmosphere.
What form of cloud precipitates rain?
It is the nimbus.
At what temperature does water expand, either by heat or cold?

At 39° Fah. water commences to expand until it reaches 32°, when it freezes, or if we apply heat it will also expand. Water cooled from any considerable heat contracts until it reaches 39°, when it then expands, as first stated.
What causes the singing of a tea-kettle?
The escaping steam is thrown into vibrations by the peculiar shape of the spout.
What causes the snapping of wood when laid on the fire?
It is caused by the expansion of the air in the cells of the wood.
What gives the blue color to the air?
It is the vapor which it contains, reflecting the blue light of the sunbeam.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Natural Philosophy. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Define electricity.
Electricity is a force of nature, termed the electric fluid. As a science, it unfolds the phenomena and laws of that fluid.
In what forms does this electric force manifest itself?
In the form of magnets, frictional, galvanic, thermal and animal electricity.
Define magnetism. A magnet.

Magnetism is that branch of science which treats of the properties of the magnet. A magnet is a term applied to certain specimens of iron ore, which have the property of attracting other portions of iron, and, when freely suspended, point to the poles.
Define magnetic induction.

It is the power which a magnet possesses to develop magnetism in iron. If a piece of soft iron be brought near a magnet, it immediately assumes the magnetic state, which, however, it loses on being removed.
Describe the compass.
It is a magnetic needle, resting upon a pivot, enclosed in a circular box, and points nearly north.
Is the needle true to the pole?
It is; although it shifts thus every hour in the day, and does so only in obedience to the laws which control its action.
Why are portions of country, especially Missouri, difficult to survey?
It is because of great bodies of iron ore which nature has stored in the ground.
Define frictional electricity.

Frictional electricity is developed by friction. This is often seen by combing the hair, by rubbing the back of a cat, etc. It is said that a person, in cold, frosty weather, by shuffling about in his stocking-feet upon the carpet, can develop so much electricity in his body that he can ignite a jet of gas by simply applying his finger to it.
Define the electroscope.
The electroscope is an instrument to detect changes in the electric state of bodies.
What is the theory of electricity?

It is a power of nature which contains a positive and negative force, and exists in every body in a state of equilibrium, but when disturbed by friction, chemical action, etc., these forces are set free, but we cannot develop one without the other.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Natural Philosophy. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Is it a polar force?
They are called a polar force, but the slightest cause disturbs the electric equilibrium.
Define a conductor. An insulator.
A conductor is a body which allows the electric force to pass freely through it. An insulator is a non-conductor.
What is the best conductor? Best insulator?
Copper is the best conductor, and is used in all electrical experiments. Glass is one of the best insulators.
Can electricity be collected from an iron rod?

It can be collected only by means of insulation. It can be developed by rubbing an iron rod, but is lost as fast as formed by passing off through the metal to the hand. A glass rod does not conduct it to the body, so it is retained until it gradually dissipates in the air.
Define electrical induction.
It is the influence of an electrified body over other bodies.
Describe the Leyden jar.

It consists of a glass jar, coated inside and outside, nearly to the top, with tin-foil, which is fitted with a cover of baked wood, through which passes a wire with a knob at the top and below, a chain extending to the inner coating.
How is the jar charged ?

The jar is discharged by holding one knob of the discharger upon the outer coating, and the other upon the knob of the jar. The equilibrium will be restored with a sharp snap and a brilliant flash, while minute particles are detached from the solid conductors and, burning, give color and brilliancy to the spark.
What is the cause of lightning ?

Lightning is only the discharge of a Leyden jar on the grand scale upon which nature performs her operations. Two clouds charged with opposite electricities, and separated by the non-conducting air, approach each other. When the tension becomes sufficient to overcome the resistance, the two forces rush together with a blinding flash and terrific peal.
What is the cause of thunder?
Thunder is the clashing of the atoms of displaced air, caused by its sudden separation as the lightning passes through it. The same principle exists in the firing of a gun.
Is there any relation between the aurora borealis and magnetism ?
As the magnetic needle is disturbed while the aurora is visible, there seems to be an intimate relation between them.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Natural Philosophy. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the value of lightning rods ?

Outside of the possibility of saving a building from a stroke of lightning, it restores the equilibrium between the earth and the clouds by quietly conducting the fluid from the overcharged atmosphere to the earth.
Does the lightning ever pass upward from the earth ?
It does, both quietly and by sudden discharges.
Has nature provided any lightning rods ?

It has. In the laws of nature we find that every leaf, twig and spire of grass is a harmless conductor. A common blade of grass, pointed by nature's exquisite workmanship, is three times more effectual than the finest cambric needle, and a single pointed twig than the metallic point of the best constructed rod.
What is the velocity of electricity?
It is estimated that through a copper wire its velocity is 288,000 miles per second.
What are the marked differences between frictional and galvanic electricity ?
Frictional electricity is noisy, sudden and convulsive; galvanic is silent, constant and powerful.
What is the effect of a voltaic current on a magnetic needle ?

If a current of electricity be passed over a magnetic needle, the needle will turn and tend to place itself at right angles to the wire. If the wire be brought over and beneath the needle, it doubles the effect, and the play of the needle becomes a very delicate test of the presence and direction of the electric force.
What is a galvanometer ?
It is an instrument for measuring the force, or detecting the presence of minute quantities of galvanic electricity.
What animals have a large amount of electricity ?
Electric fish are the most common, and among these the most noted are the torpedo and electric eel. These are so powerful that their shock is a means of defense.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Made Easy (Participles And Infinitives). Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What construction have Infinitives and Participles ?
All Infinitives and Participles have the construction and use of the noun, or the adjective, or the adverb.
When has an infinitive the construction of a noun ?
When used as subject of a verb, or in apposition with a noun; as a noun in the absolute case; as the object of a verb or preposition.
Illustrate by example.
To play is natural. Real labor, to do good, is conducive to happiness. To lie, oh how base! They love to laugh. What went ye out for to see?
When has the infinitive the construction of an adjective?
When used to limit or modify the meaning of a noun.
Give examples.
A desire to play is natural. He gave orders to march. This rule is to be observed—adjective in predicate. This to-be-observed rule is, or exists.
When has the infinitive the construction of an adverb ?
When used to limit the meaning of a verb, adjective, adverb, or other infinitive.
Give examples.
They came to see; i. e., for the purpose of seeing. They were anxious to go. That horse is strong enough to draw any load. I wish to go to town to trade to-day.
Have infinitives case ?
They have not. Case belongs alone to nouns and pronouns.
Has the infinitive always a subject ?
It has, but not always expressed.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Made Easy (Participles And Infinitives). Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is the subject of "to go," in "I wished to go home?"
The subject of "to go" is "I," understood; the meaning evidently is, I wished that I might go home.
In what case is the subject of the infinitive?
In the objective case, except when its subject is, or refers to, the same person or thing as the subject of the finite verb, or when the subject is any word but a noun or pronoun.
Has the participle always a subject?
It has, either expressed or implied.
In what case is the subject of the participle?
Usually in the possessive case; sometimes in the objective; as: There is no harm in children's playing by the river (subj. poss.). There is no harm in children playing by the river (subj. obj.).*
What is the rule for the construction of participles ?
It is used in all cases like the infinitive.
In the sentence, "To be honest with you, I was in error," parse "to be."
To be: verb; irreg., intrans., act., pres., infin., with the construction of an adverb limiting the verb confess, understood—[I confess] (in order) to be honest, etc.
In what manner do we determine whether the infinitive has the construction of the noun, or the adjective, or the adverb ?

First, and mainly, by the sense or meaning; when the infinitive has the construction of an adverb, the words "in order" can be placed immediately before the infinitive without destroying the sense; as: They came [in order] to see. When it has the construction of an adjective it can be changed to its present participle, placed immediately before the word it limits and make good sense; as: He asked for money to spend, i. e., spending-money. I want a place to study, i. e., studying-place.
How do we determine what is the subject of the infinitive, when that is not apparent, as in "He was eager to depart?"

Change the infinitive to its equivalent subordinate clause, and the subject will appear. He was eager to depart, i. e., that he might depart. He is the subject. Be so good as to go, i. e., that you should go. John was told not to go, i. e., that John should not go.
In the sentence, "To die is gain," what is the subject of "to die?"

Person, understood. Expanded, That any person should die is gain. *There is much dispute about this, many claiming that the possessive pronoun "their," understood, is the subject of "playing," in the second sentence.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Writing. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Define Writing.
The art of expressing thought in legible characters; to "form for the conveyance of meaning."
What are the principal subjects to which attention should be given in teaching or learning the art of writing?
Position; manner of holding pen; movement; form; rate; spacing and shading.
How many kinds of positions are recommendable, and what are they?
Four — "left," "front," "right-oblique," and "right."
Describe the "left" position.

Sit, or stand, with the left side inclined toward the desk, the left arm lying on the book or paper, weight of body supported mainly by the left side and right arm, as well as book or paper at right-angles to the desk.
Under what circumstances is the left position generally used, and used with advantage?
When writing in large books, or when it is impossible or inconvenient to turn the paper obliquely.
Describe the "front" position.

Sit, or stand, directly facing the desk, near it, but not leaning over it; the forearms both on the desk and nearly at right angles to each other, the left holding in place the book or paper, which should be inclined so that the lines meet the right forearm at right angles.
What particular advantage has the front position?
The weight of the body is not thrown to either side (in any other it is thrown upon one side or the other); and hence this position is best adapted to resist fatigue of the bodily muscles.
Describe the "right-oblique" position.

Sit, or stand, with right side inclined towards the desk, right forearm resting wholly on the desk, left partially, and the copy placed obliquely on the desk. The weight of the head and shoulders is thrown on the left side.
Describe the "right" position.

Sit, or stand, with right side turned to the desk, but not in contact with it, the right forearm as well as the copy being on the desk parallel with its edge; copy held in place by the fingers of the left hand.
What special caution should be kept in view in each and all of the above-mentioned positions?
Keep the body erect; by no means permit the head to drop forward over the paper. Teachers should exercise the greatest care in this respect over those placed in their charge.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Writing. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Describe the manner of holding the pen.

There is considerable dispute in regard to this, but the manner most practiced by businessmen and taught in most of our commercial colleges is the one given by Mr. Spencer, viz., hold pen between first two fingers and thumb, the holder crossing the forefinger in front of the knuckle-joint and the second finger at the root of the nail; the end of the thumb touching the holder opposite the lower joint of the first finger. The other fingers should separate from first two at the middle joint and curve so that the ends of the nails rest upon the paper.
What is meant by movement?
The proper motion of the muscles of the shoulder, arm and hand in writing.
How many kinds of movements are usually employed in writing, and what are they?
Two; combined and whole-arm.
Give a description of each, and state under what circumstances employed.

The combined movement is the motion of the muscles of the forearm and fingers together, resting upon the elbow and the nails of the third and fourth fingers; used in ordinary writing. The whole-arm movement is a motion of the muscles of the whole arm and shoulder, resting upon the nails of the third and fourth fingers, and upon the point of the pen; used in making large capitals, flourishes, and in blackboard exercise. The wrist joint should in all cases be kept free.
What is meant by form?
The exact shape and appearance of a character representing a thought preconceived in the mind.
In what manner does a careful study of form aid in making a good penman?

The mind directs the action of the muscles, and as writing is a result of muscular action, the mind must have a clear, concise and ready conception of what it wishes the muscles to do, before the action can be realized.
What is a line?
A point moving in any direction leaves a path called a line.
How many kinds of lines are used in writing, and what are they?
Two : straight and curved. A straight line is the path of a point moving in but one direction ; a curved line is the path of a point constantly changing its direction.
How many kinds of curves are used in writing, and define each.
Two : right and left. A right curve bends to the right of a straight line connecting its extremities, while a left curve bends to the left.
Define horizontal, vertical and oblique lines.
A line parallel to the horizon; a line at right angles to the horizon ; a line neither horizontal nor vertical.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Writing. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What space is allowed between capitals used as initial letters?
One space.
What space is allowed between words?
Two spaces.
What space is allowed when following word begins with a capital?
The extreme left point of the capital should be one and one-half spaces from the last downward stroke of preceding word.
When one word ends with a right curve and the next begins with a left, or vice versa, what space is left?
Between the straight lines, two and one-third spaces; between curves, one and one-third spaces; between final downward stroke of first and beginning of curve for next word, one and one-half spaces.
What space is allowed between sentences?
Three spaces.
What space is allowed between figures?
One-third of a space.
What is meant by shading?

A proper use of light and heavy strokes, the latter formed by a gently-increasing pressure upon the points of the pen, being held and moved so that the pressure is constantly alike on both points, until the middle of the stroke desired to be shaded is reached, and then as gently release the pressure as it was increased until the stroke is complete.
What letters should not ordinarily be shaded?
All the letters but one space in height except a, which should receive a slight shade on first downward curve.
Where should the shade occur in t and d?
The heaviest shade should be at the top of the last downward stroke.
How should p be shaded?
Shade should commence at base line and gradually increased to the end of the downward stroke.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Writing. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How should f be shaded?
Only on that part of the downward stroke which is below the line.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Algebra. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Define Algebra.
Algebra is that branch of mathematics in which the operations are indicated by signs, and the quantities are represented by letters.
Why is algebra called universal arithmetic ?
Because the object of algebraic notation is to abridge and generalize the analysis of mathematical problems.
Define mathematics.
Mathematics is the science which treats of the relations of quantities.
Define quantity.
Quantity is anything that can be measured or compared.
Define algebraic quantity.
An algebraic quantity is a quantity expressed in algebraic language.
How many kinds of algebraic quantities are there?
Two kinds—known and unknown.
What are known quantities ?
Known quantities are those whose values are given; when these are not expressed by figures they are represented by the leading letters of the alphabet, as a, b, c, d.
What are unknown quantities ?
Unknown quantities are those whose values are to be determined, and are represented by the final letters of the alphabet, as u, x, y, z.
How many kinds of symbols are there ?
Four kinds—symbols of quantity, operation, relation and aggregation.
What are symbols of quantity ?
In arithmetic numbers are represented by the figures 1, 2, 3, 4, etc. In algebra numbers or quantities are more frequently represented by the common letters of the alphabet.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Algebra. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are the symbols of operation ?
The signs of addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, involution, and evolution.
What are the symbols of relation ?
The signs of equality, inequality, ratio, and proportion.
What are the signs of aggregation ?
The parenthesis ( ), brackets [ ], brace { }, vincu- lum —, and bar |. They indicate that the quantities within are to be taken together.
What are the terms of an algebraic quantity?
The divisions made by the signs + and —.
What are positive terms ?
Those that have the plus sign.
What are negative terms?
Those that have the minus sign.
Distinguish between similar and dissimilar terms.
Similar terms are terms containing the same letters affected by the same exponents, while dissimilar terms are those which have different letters or exponents.
Define coefficient.
The coefficient of a term is that factor which is considered as denoting the number of times the remainder of the term is taken.
Define exponent.
An exponent is a small figure or letter written at the right, and a little above another figure, or letter, to denote how many times the latter is to be used as a factor.
What are literal quantities ?
Literal quantities are those whose numbers or values are expressed by letters.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Algebra. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Define monomial.
A monomial is an algebraic quantity consisting of but one term.
Define polynomial.
A polynomial is an algebraic quantity consisting of more than one term.
Define binomial.
A binomial is a polynomial of two terms.
Define residual.
A residual is a binomial, the terms of which are connected by the minus sign.
What is a trinomial ?
A polynomial of three terms.
What is meant by the degree of a term ?
The degree of a term is the number of literal factors it contains, and is found by adding the exponents of the several letters.
What is a homogeneous quantity?
One whose terms are all of the same degree.
Express the product of a and b, and explain.
ab. When two or more letters representing numbers are written side by side, their product is indicated.

Express algebraically : A man sold a farm which cost him a dollars for b times its cost ; after keeping back c dollars for himself, he divided the remainder equally among d children. How many dollars did he give to each
ab—c / d
In the following : a=4, b=3, c=2, d=1. a²+b²+c²+d² —————— + a b c d. Solve. a+b+c+d

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Algebra. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Define algebraic sum.
The algebraic sum of two or more quantities is a quantity which, taken with reference to its sign, is equivalent to the given quantities each taken with reference to its particular sign.
What is the method of finding the algebraic sum ?

The algebraic sum of two or more similar terms, having like signs, is the sum of their absolute values taken with their common sign. When with unlike signs, is the difference of their absolute values taken with the sign of the greater term.
Define unit of addition.
The unit of addition is the quantity whose coefficients are added.
Give method of algebraic subtraction.
Conceive the signs of the subtrahend to be changed, and unite terms as in addition.
How may a parenthesis be removed from an algebraic expression?
When preceded by the plus sign a parenthesis may be removed and the enclosed terms written with proper signs, but when preceded by the minus sign the signs of all the enclosed terms must be changed.
How may an algebraic expression be enclosed in a parenthesis ?

Any number of terms with their proper signs may be enclosed by a parenthesis and the plus sign written before the whole, or the minus sign may be written before the whole, provided that all the signs of the given terms be changed.
Give law governing the sign of a product in algebraic multiplication.
When the two factors have like signs the product is positive, and when the two factors have unlike signs the product is negative.
Give law of coefficients in multiplication.
The coefficient of the product is equal to the product of the coefficients of the two factors.
Give law of exponents in multiplication.
The exponent of any letter in the product is equal to the sum of the exponents of this letter in the factors.
Give formula for the square of a binomial.

The square of the first term, plus twice the product of the first and the second, plus the square of the second if both terms are positive; if, however, the binomial be a residual the sign of the second term in the square should be minus.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Algebra. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Give formula for the product of the sum and difference of two quantities.
The product of the sum and difference of two quantities is equal to the difference of their squares.
Give law of signs in algebraic division.
If the signs of terms are alike, prefix the plus sign to the quotient; if they are unlike, prefix the minus sign.
Give law of coefficients in division.
The coefficient of the quotient is found by dividing the coefficient of the dividend by that of the divisor.
Give law for exponents in division.
The exponent of any letter in the quotient is found by subtracting the exponent of this letter in the divisor from its exponent in the dividend.
Give general law relating to change of value resulting from change of dividend or divisor by multiplication or division.

A change in the dividend produces a like change in the quotient; but a change in the divisor produces an opposite change in the quotient. A like change in both dividend and divisor does not alter the value of the quotient.
Give principles relating to change of sign in dividend or divisor.
Changing the sign of either dividend or divisor changes the sign of the quotient, but changing the signs of both dividend and divisor does not alter the value of the quotient.
Prove that any quantity having a cipher for an exponent is equal to unity.
a/a = a^(1-1) = a^0 a/a = 1. Therefore a^0 = 1.
Prove that any quantity having a negative exponent is equal to the reciprocal of that quantity with an equal positive exponent?
What are the factors of a quantity?
Those quantities which, multiplied together, will produce that quantity.
A³+b³ is divisible by what? Explain.
By a+b. The sum of two equal odd powers is divisible by the sum of the quantities.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Algebra. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
A³—b³ is divisible by what? Explain.
By a—b. The difference of two equal odd powers is divisible by the difference of the quantities.
A⁴—b⁴ is divisible by what? Explain.
By a—b and by a+b. The difference of two equal even powers is divisible by both the sum and difference of the quantities.
A¹⁰+b¹⁰ is divisible by what? Explain.

By a^2+b^2. The sum of two equal even powers cannot be factored unless the exponent is composed of the factor 2 and an odd number, in which case it is divisible by the sum of the squares of the quantities.
32a⁵+1 is divisible by what? Explain.
By 2a+1. This comes properly under question 50, 1 being any power of itself.
Define common divisor ; greatest common divisor.
A common divisor of two or more quantities is a quantity which will exactly divide each of them. A greatest common divisor is the greatest quantity that will exactly divide each of them.
Define multiple, common multiple, and least common multiple.

A multiple of a quantity is a quantity exactly divisible by the given quantity. A common multiple of two or more quantities is one which is exactly divisible by each of them. A least common multiple of two or more quantities is the least quantity exactly divisible by each of them.
Give method for finding the greatest common divisor of two or more quantities.
Factor the given quantities, and affect each factor with the least exponent which it has in any of the quantities. The product of the factors thus obtained will be the greatest common divisor.
Give method for finding the least common multiple of two or more quantities.

Factor the given quantities and affect each factor with an exponent equal to the greatest which it has in any of the quantities. The product of the factors thus obtained will be the least common multiple.
What is substitution in algebra ?
The process of putting one quantity for another in any given expression.
What is a fraction?
A quotient expressed by writing the dividend above a horizontal line and the divisor below.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Algebra. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Give general law relating to change of value, resulting from change of numerator or denominator by multiplication or division.

The denominator, or quantity below the line, being the divisor, and the numerator, or quantity above the line, being the dividend, the principles here involved are the same as those given in answer to question 45.
What is an entire quantity ? A mixed quantity ?
An entire quantity is an algebraic quantity which has no fractional part. A mixed quantity has both entire and fractional parts.
Distinguish between real and apparent signs.

The apparent sign of a fraction is the sign written before the dividing line to indicate whether the fraction is to be added or subtracted. The real sign is the sign of its numerical value when reduced to a monomial, and shows whether the fraction is essentially a positive or a negative quantity.
How may the real signs of a fraction be changed ?
By changing all the signs of the numerator or denominator, or by changing the apparent sign of the fraction.
Changing all the signs of both numerator and denominator has what effect upon the real sign ?
The real sign remains unchanged.
What is reduction ?
Reduction in mathematics is changing the form of an expression without changing its value.
Give method for reducing a fraction to lowest terms.
Reject all common factors from both terms.
Give method for reducing a fraction from fractional form to an entire or mixed quantity.
Perform the division indicated.
Give method for reducing a mixed quantity to the form of a fraction.
Multiply the entire part by the given denominator, and annexing the numerator of the fractional part, write the sum over the given denominator.
How may fractions be reduced to a common denominator ?

The least common denominator is the least com-mon multiple of all the denominators. Divide this com-mon denominator by each of the given denominators, and multiply each numerator by the corresponding quotient. The products will be the new numerators.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Algebra. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Give method for adding fractions.
Reduce the fractions to forms having a common denominator, add the numerators and write the result over the common denominator.
Give rule for subtraction of fractions.
Reduce to common denominator, subtract the numerator of the subtrahend from that of the minuend, and write the remainder over the common denominator.
Give rule for multiplication of fractions.
Take the product of the numerators for a new numerator, and the product of the denominators for a new denominator.
Give rule for division of fractions.
Invert the terms of the divisor and proceed as in multiplication.
What is an equation ?
An equation is an expression of equality between two quantities.
What branch of mathematics treats most of the equations ?
Algebra treats of the nature and properties of the equation, and of its use as an instrument for conducting mathematical investigations.
What is the first member of an equation? The second member ?
The first member is the quantity on the left of the sign of equality. The second member is the quantity on the right.
Define arithmetical equation Algebraic equation.
An arithmetical equation is one which expresses the equality of numbers. An algebraic equation is one which contains one or more literal quantities.
Define numeral equations. Literal equations.
A numeral equation is one in which all the known quantities are expressed by numbers. A literal equation is one in which some or all of the known quantities are expressed by letters.
Define identical equation.
An equation in which the two members are the same, or can be reduced to the same expression by performing the operations indicated.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Algebra. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How is the degree of an equation denoted ?
By the highest exponent of the unknown quantity in the equation.
Define simple equation. Quadratic, and cubic.
A simple equation is one of the first degree. A quadratic equation is one of the second degree. A cubic equation is one of the third degree.
What is meant by the transformation of an equation ?
The process of changing its form without destroying the equality of its members.
How may an equation be cleared of fractions ?
Multiply each member by the least common multiple of all the denominators.
What is transposition?
Changing a term from one member of the equation to the other without destroying the equality of its members.
How may a term be transposed ?
By dropping it from the member in which it stands, and inserting it in the other member with its sign changed.
What is meant by the reduction or solution of an equation ?
Finding the value of the unknown quantity.
Define indeterminate equation.
An indeterminate equation is one which is satisfied by an infinite number of values of the unknown quantity. Every single equation containing two unknown quantities is indeterminate.
What are simultaneous equations ?
Those which must be satisfied by the same values of the unknown quantities which enter them.
When two or more simultaneous equations are given, by what process are the values of the unknown quantities determined?
By elimination, a process of combining equations in such a manner as to cause one or more of the unknown quantities to disappear.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Algebra. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How many methods of elimination are there?
Three : by addition or subtraction, comparison and substitution.
Give method of elimination by addition or subtraction.

Multiply or divide the equation by such numbers or quantities that the coefficients of the quantity to be eliminated shall be made equal in the two equations. these coefficients have like signs, subtract one of the prepared equations from the other; if unlike, add the two equations.
Give method of elimination by comparison.
Find the value of the same unknown quantity, in terms of the other, from each of the given equations. Form an equation by placing these two values equal to each other.
Give method of elimination by substitution.
Find the value of one of the unknown quantities in terms of the other, from either of the given equations. Substitute the value for the same unknown quantity in the other equation.
Give method of eliminating in simple equations containing more than two unknown quantities.

Combine one of the given equations with each of the others, eliminating the same unknown quantity by each combination. Then combine one of the new equations with each of the others, eliminating a second unknown quantity, and thus continue till a final equation is obtained containing but one unknown quantity. Substitute the value of this unknown quantity in an equation containing two unknown quantities, and thus find the value of a second. Substitute these values in an equation containing three unknown quantities, and find the value of the third, and so on until the value of all are found.
When only can the value of the unknown quantity be determined?
When its conditions furnish as many independent equations as there are unknown quantities employed in the notation.
What is an inequality? Express a is greater than b.
An expression signifying that one quantity is greater or less than another. a>b.
By what operation will the sign of an inequality be reversed?

If an inequality be subtracted from an equation member from member. If all the signs of the inequality be changed. If both members be multiplied by the same negative quantity. If multiplied by another inequality, subsisting in the same sense, member by member, when more than two of the members of both inequalities are negative.
What operations may be performed upon an inequality and the result still subsist in the same sense?

If another inequality subsisting in the same sense be added member to member. If another inequality established in a contrary sense be subtracted. If both members be multiplied or divided by the same positive quantity. If multiplied by another inequality subsisting in the same sense, when more than two of the members of both inequalities are positive.
What is a power? A root?

A power is the product obtained by taking a quantity several times as a factor. A root is the quantity repeated to produce a power. 101. Involution is the process of raising a quantity to a given power. Evolution is the process of determining any root of a given quantity.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Algebra. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is meant by the square of a quantity? The cube of a quantity?
The square of a quantity is its second power, and the cube is its third power.
Distinguish between perfect and imperfect power.
A perfect power is a quantity that can be exactly produced by taking some other quantity a certain number of times as a factor, while an imperfect power cannot.
How is evolution indicated? What is a surd?
By the radical sign √ . A surd is the indicated root of an imperfect power.
In raising a quantity to a given power, what are the principles relating to coefficients, exponents, and signs of the power?

The coefficient of the power is the coefficient of the quantity raised to the required power. The exponent of any letter in the power is the product of its exponent by the index of the required power. The odd powers of a negative quantity will have the minus sign; the even powers of a negative quantity, and all powers of a positive quantity, are positive.
How may a fraction be raised to a given power?
Raise both numerator and denominator to the required power.
Give formula for the square of a trinomial.
Write the square of each term with twice the product of each term by the sum of all the terms which follow it, and reduce the result, if necessary.
What is meant by leading terms? By following term?
The leading term is the first, and the following term is the second term in a binomial.
Of what value is Newton's Binomial Formula?
It enables one to write by inspection any power of a binomial, neither term of which has coefficients.
Give method of expanding by Newton's Binomial Formula.
Let (x+y) be any binomial and (x+y)ᵐ equal any power indicated, then xᵐ + mxᵐ⁻¹y + m(m—1)xᵐ⁻²y² / 2 + m(m—1)(m—2)xᵐ⁻³y³ / 3 etc. There will always be m+1 terms in the power.
How may a binomial having coefficients be expanded without successive multiplication, as (2a—3b)⁷?

Put the first term 2a equal to x and the second or 3b equal to y. Expand (x+y)ᵐ by Newton's Formula and restore the values of x and y; or use French's Binomial Formula, which shows that the coefficient of the first term in any power is equal to the corresponding power of the coefficient of the leading term in the root; and that the coefficient of any term may be found by multiplying the coefficient of the preceding term by the exponent of the leading quantity in that term and by the coefficient of the following quantity in the root, and dividing this result by the product of the coefficient of the leading quantity in the root multiplied by the number of terms from the first.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Algebra. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is a radical quantity?
A root merely indicated by the radical sign, or by a fractional exponent.
How is the degree of a radical quantity indicated?
By the radical index, or by the denominator of the fractional exponent.
What are similar radicals?
Those having the same quantity under a radical sign of the same index.
On what are the operations in radicals based?
On the most elementary principles of factoring.
When is a radical in its simplest form?
When it contains no perfect power corresponding to the degree of the radical.
By what method are radicals reduced to simplest form?

Resolve the quantity under the radical sign into two factors, one of which shall be a perfect power of the degree of the radical. Determine the root of that factor and place it before the radical sign as a coefficient to the other factor under the sign.
How may we reduce a rational quantity to a radical?
Involve it to the same power as the required index and write the result under its corresponding radical sign.
How may we reduce radicals of different degrees to a common radical index ?
Reduce the indices to a common denominator. Perform upon the quantities the operations represented by the numerators (involution) and indicate the operations signified by the denominator (evolution).
Give method of adding and subtracting radicals.

Reduce each radical to its simplest form. If the resulting radicals are similar add or subtract the coefficients as the case may be, and to the result annex the common radical; if dissimilar, indicate the addition or subtraction by the proper signs.
Give method of multiplying and dividing radicals.
If necessary, reduce the given radicals to a common radical index. Multiply or divide, as the case may be, the radical parts, and write the result under the common radical sign.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Algebra. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How may a radical sign be removed ?
Involve the radical quantity to a power corresponding to the radical index.
Give classes of quadratic equations.
Pure and affected.
Define pure quadratic equations.
A pure quadratic equation is one which contains only the second power of the unknown quantity.
What is an affected quadratic equation ?
One which contains both the second and the first powers of the unknown quantity.
What is meant by the root of an equation?
Such a value as when substituted for the unknown quantity will satisfy the equation.
Why may the sign ± be prefixed to the root of a quadratic equation ?

The second power of any quantity must be positive, as it is the product of like signs, either plus or minus, hence every pure quadratic equation has two roots equal in numerical value, but of opposite signs.
Speak of the roots of an affected quadratic equation.
Every affected quadratic equation has two roots unequal in numerical value.
Give a method of forming a quadratic when the roots are given.
Multiply together the binomial factors obtained by connecting each root with its contrary sign to an unknown quantity and place the product equal to zero.
How, then, may a trinomial in the form of x² + ax + b be separated into two binomial factors ?
Place the trinomial equal to zero and reduce the resulting equation. Connect each root with its signs changed to the lowest power of the literal quantity.
Give method for completing the square in equations reduced to the form of x² + 2 ax=b.
Add to both members of the equation the square of half the coefficient of the first power of the unknown quantity.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Algebra. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
Give method for completing the square in equations reduced to the form of ax² +bx =c.
Multiply the equation by 4 times the coefficient of x² and add the square of the coefficient of x to both members.
How may the equation x⁴—3x²=550 be solved?
Represent x⁴ as y² and x² as y ; solve and restore value of y.
What is meant by the absolute term of an equation ?
The term or quantity which does not contain the unknown quantity.
What is arithmetical progression ?
A series of members or quantities increasing or decreasing by a common difference.
What is geometrical progression ?
A series of numbers or quantities increasing or decreasing by a constant multiplier.
What are the extremes ? The means ?
The extremes are the first and last terms. The means are the intermediate terms.
Define ratio in geometrical progression.
The ratio is the constant multiplier.
What letters are used as symbols of the different terms in progression ?
a, represents the first term ; b, last term ; n, the number of terms ; d, the common difference ; r, the ratio ; and s, the sum of the series.
Give formulas for problems in arithmetical progression.
Give formulas for problems in geometrical progression.
l = arⁿ⁻¹; s = (rl-a)/(r-1)

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Algebra. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is ratio ? Proportion ?
Ratio is the quotient of one quantity divided by another of the same kind, regarded as the standard of comparison. Proportion is an equality of ratios.
Define couplet. Terms.
A couplet is the two terms which form the ratio. The terms of a proportion are the four quantities compared.
What are the antecedents ? Consequents ?
The antecedents in a proportion are the first terms of the two couplets. The consequents are the second terms.
Define extremes. Means.
The extremes in a proportion are the first and fourth terms ; the means, the second and third terms.
Define mean proportional.
A mean proportional between two quantities is a quantity to which the first of the two given quantities has the same ratio as the quantity has to the second.
Define proposition and give kinds.
A proposition is a statement of something to be done. Axioms, Theorems, Lemmas, Corollaries, Postulates, and Problems.
What is an axiom ?
A self-evident truth.
Define problem.
A problem is a proposition to do some specified thing and is stated with reference to developing the method of doing it.
What is a scholium ? 151. Prove that the product of the means equals the product of the extremes.
A remark made at the close of a discussion and designed to call attention to some particular feature or features of it.
If we have three terms of a proportion, how may the fourth be obtained ?
Either mean is equal to the product of the extremes divided by the other mean, and either extreme is equal to the product of the means divided by the other extreme.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Algebra. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What is a logarithm ?
The exponent by which a fixed number is to be affected in order to produce any required number.
What is a system of logarithms ?
A scheme by which all numbers can be represented either exactly or approximately by exponents by which a fixed number (the base) can be affected. Negative numbers can have no logarithms.
What are the important uses of logarithms ?
To facilitate the multiplication, division, involution and evolution of large numbers.
Give method of multiplication by the use of log-arithms.
Add the logarithms of the two factors and the result will be the logarithm of their product.
Give method of division.
Subtract the logarithm of the divisor from that of the dividend and the result will be the logarithm of the quotient.
Give method of involution.
Multiply the logarithm of the number by the index of the power and the result will be the logarithm of the power.
Give method for evolution.
Divide the logarithm of the number by the number expressing the degree of the root and the result will be the logarithm of the root.
What are the integral and decimal parts of a log-arithm called ?
The integral part is called the characteristic and the decimal the mantissa.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Algebra — Test Problems. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
The sum of two numbers is 12, and their difference is equal to ⅓ of the greater. What are the numbers?

The half sum of two numbers plus the half difference equals the greater. The half sum minus the half difference equals the less. Let 2x=difference. 6+x=greater. 6—x=less. 2x=6+x 3 Ans. Greater 7½. Less 4½.

A farmer made a mixture of barley, oats and peas, using two bushels of barley as often as 5 of oats and 1 of peas ; the whole number of bushels in the mixture was 32. How many bushels of each kind did he use?
Let x=No. bushels peas. 2x=No. bushels barley. 5x=No. bushels oats. 8x=32. Ans. 4 bushels peas, 8 bushels barley, 20 bushels oats.
A farm rents for 6 per cent. more this year than last. This year it rents for $1,060. What was the rent last year?
Let x=rent last year. x+6x ——=1060. Ans. $1,000. 100
A steamer whose regular speed is twelve miles per hour in still water, can make a trip up stream in 7 hours and down in 5 hours. At what rate does the river flow?
Let x=rate of river. Then 7(12—x)=5(12+x). Ans. 2 hours.
A can do a piece of work in 6 days, B in 4 days. In what time can they both do it, working together?
Let x=time required by both. x x Then — + — =1. Ans. 2⅔ days. 6 4
What time is it when ⅓ of the time past noon equals ¼ of the time to midnight?
Let x=time past noon. 12—x=time to midnight. x 12—x Then — = —— . Ans. Half past 4 o'clock P. M. 3 5
What fraction is that which if 1 be added to the numerator the fraction will be ⅓ but if 1 be added to the denominator the fraction will be ¼?
Let x=numerator of fraction. y=denominator. x+1 x Then —— = ⅓ and —— = ¼. Ans. ⅖. y y+1 8. Let x=greater ; n—x=less. Then x+a=(n—x)+b. Ans. Greater (n+b-a)/2 less (n+a-b)/2.
A says to B: "Give me one dollar and I shall have twice as much money as you." B says to A : "No; you give me one dollar and I shall have just as much money as you." How many dollars have each?
Let x=A's money ; y=B's money. Then x+1=2(y-1) and x-1=y+1. Ans. A's=$7. B's=$5.
What is the length of one edge of that cube which contains as many solid units as there are linear units in the diagonal through the opposite corners?
Let x=length of edge. sqrt(3x^2) =the diagonal. x^3 = sqrt(3x^2). Ans. sqrt(3).
The sum of two numbers is 100, and the difference of their square roots is 2. What are the numbers?
Let x=greater ; y=less Then x+y=100 and sqrt(x) - sqrt(y) =2. Square second equation and subtract. Ans. 64 and 36.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Algebra — Test Problems. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What number is that consisting of two digits, units and tens, which is equal to three times the sum of its digits, and if 45 be added to the number, the order of the digits will be reversed?
The local value of the digit in ten's place being ten times that in unit's place, if we let x=units and y=tens the number will be 10y+x. Then 10y+x=3(x+y) and 10y+x+45=10x+y. Ans. 27.
James is ⅓ as old as John; 5 years ago he was ⅕ as old? how old are each?
Let x=James' age ; y=John's age. Then x = y/3 and x-5 = (y-5)/5. Ans. James 10, John 30.
What two numbers are those whose product is 36, and the difference of their squares is 65?
Let x=one number and y the other. Then xy=36 and x^2-y^2=65. Find value of x^2 in each equation and compare. Ans. 9 and 4.
Divide the number 2000 into two such parts that the greater shall be to the less as 9 to 7.
Let x=greater number and y the less. Then x+y=2000 and x:y::9:7 or 9y=7x. Multiply first equation through by 7 and subtract. Ans. 1125 and 875.
There is a fish whose head is 9 in. long; the tail is as long as the head and ½ the body; and the body is as long as the head and tail together. What is the length of the fish?
Let x=length of body and 9+1/2x=length of tail. Then 9+9+1/2x=x, or x=18+1/2x. Ans. 72 inches.

A shepherd was met by a band of robbers who plundered him of ¼ of his flock and ¼ of a sheep over; he afterwards met a second band who took ⅓ of the remainder and ⅓ of a sheep over; and a third band took ½ of the last remainder and ½ of a sheep over, when he had but 25 sheep left. How many sheep had he at first?
Let 12x=number he had at first. 9x-1/4=first remainder. 6x-1/2=second remainder. 3x-3/4=third remainder. 3x-1=25. Ans. 103.
Simplify (√a + √b)(√a - √b) / (⁴√a + ⁴√b)(⁴√a - ⁴√b).
In both numerator and denominator we have the product of the sum and difference of two quantities. Ans.
A tree 80 feet high is broken during a storm; the upper part clinging to the stump strikes the ground 40 feet from the foot of the tree. How long is each part?
Let x=upper part and 80-x=lower part. Then x^2-42^2=(80-x)^2. Ans. Upper part 50 feet. Lower 30 feet.
A picture 12 feet by 18 feet is set in a frame of uniform width containing the same area as the picture. What is the width of frame?
Let x=width of frame. Then 24x+36x+4x^2=216. Transpose, unite and complete the square. Ans. 3 inches.
To find the area of a scalene triangle whose sides are a, b, and c.

From the vertical angle let fall a perpendicular to the base b. Let x=the segment adjacent to the side c ; b—x=the segment adjacent to the side a ; Then will c^2—x^2=perpendicular. Also a^2—(b—x)^2=perpendicular. Then will c^2—x^2=a^2—(b—x)^2. After finding the perpendicular multiply the base b, by ½ the perpendicular.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Algebra — Test Problems. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How are the above problems solved arithmetically?
Add the three sides and divide this sum by 2. Subtract each side separately from the half sum. Multiply the several remainders and the half sum together and determine the square root of the result.
How many strokes do the clocks of Venice, which go on to 24 o'clock, strike in a day?
A company of travelers at a hotel incurred a bill of $12. If there had been two more in the company each would have been charged 30 cents less. How many were there in the company?
Let x=number in the company. Then 1200/x - 30 = 1200/(x+2). Divide both members by 30. Clear of fractions and complete the square. Ans. 8.
The sum of three numbers in arithmetical progression is 27, and the product of the extremes is 77. What are the numbers?
The formulas will not apply in this problem so let y=common difference. Then x—y=first ; x=second, and x+y=third. 3x=27, x=9, x^2—y^2=77. Ans. 7, 9, 11.

A tract of land in the form of a square is surrounded by a board fence, four boards high, each board 16½ feet long; the number of acres in the tract equals the number of boards in the fence. How many acres of land are there?

Let x=side of the square in rods. Then x^2/160 =number of acres. 16x=number of boards, each board being 16½ feet or 1 rod long. Then x^2/160 =16x. After finding x, square it and divide by 160. Ans. 40960.
A boy being asked how many fish he had, replied: 11 fish are 7 fish more than ⅔ of the number. How many had he ?
Let x=the number. Then x/2+7=11. Ans. 10.
A man purchased a house with 10 doors, giving $1 for the first door, $2 for the second, $4 for the third, and so on. What did the house cost him ?
l=ar^(n-1), l=1x2^9. Substitute l in s = (rl-a)/(r-1). Ans. $1,023.
A man holds an endowment policy payable in 30 years, and ⅓ of the time past equals ¼ of the time to come. How much time has past?
Let x=time passed ; 30—x=time to come. x/2 = (30—x)/4. Ans. 10 years.
One kind of wine is 40 cents a quart, and another 24 cents. How much of each must be taken to make a quart worth 28 cents ?
Let x=the amount required at 40 cents and y=the amount required at 24 cents. Then x+y=1 and 40x+24y=28. Ans. ¼ at 40 cents, and ¾ at 24 cents.

A farmer has two flocks of sheep, each containing the same number. From one he sells 39 and from the other 93, and then finds just twice as many in one flock as in the other. How many did each flock originally contain?
Let x=original number in each flock. Then x—39=2(x—93). Ans. 147.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Algebra — Test Problems. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
A man being asked his age, replied : If to my age you add its half, and third, and then deduct 10, the result is 100. What was his age ?
Let x=his age. Then x + x/2 + x/3 - 10 = 100. Ans. 60 years old.
A merchant has sugar at 9 cents and 13 cents a pound, and he wishes to make a mixture of 100 pounds that shall be worth 12 cents a pound. How many pounds of each quality must be taken ?
Let x=number of pounds at 9 cents and y=number of pounds at 13 cents. Then x+y=100, and 9x+13y=1,200. Ans. 25 lbs. at 9c., and 75 lbs. at 13c.
A man was hired for a year for $100 and a suit of clothes ; but at the end of 8 months he left, and received his clothes and $60 in money. What was the value of the suit of clothes ?
Let x=value of clothes. Then (100+x)/12 = (60+x)/8. Ans. $20.
Determine the 5th root of 31152784.1 by means of logarithms, knowing that the logarithm of 31152784.1= 7.493497.
7.493497 ÷ 5 = 1.498699, and by reference to the table of logarithms 1.498699 is the logarithm of 31.52. Ans. 31.52.
A man sold a horse for $420, thereby gaining 25 per cent. What was the cost of the horse ?
Let x=cost of horse. Then x + 25x/100 = 420. Ans. $336.
Two men commenced trade together. The first put in $40 more than the second ; and the stock of the first was to the stock of the second as 5 to 4. What was the stock of each ?
Let x=what the first put in. Then x—40=what second put in. Then x : x—40 : : 5 : 4. Ans. $200, and $160.
A man traveled 36 miles in a certain number of hours ; if he had traveled one mile more per hour he would have required 3 hours less to perform his journey. How many miles did he travel per hour?
Let x=number of miles traveled per hour. Then 36/x - 3 = 36/(x+1). Ans. 3 miles.
A person after spending $100 more than ⅓ of his income, had remaining $35 more than ⅕ of it. What was his income ?
Let x=his income. Then x = 100 + x/5 + 35 + x/2. Ans. 450.
A boy engaged to carry 100 glass vessels to a certain place, and to receive 3 cents for every one he de-
Let x=number he broke, and 100—x=number he delivered. Then 3x—9(100—x)=240. Ans. 5.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Parliamentary Rules. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
What are Parliamentary Rules?
A recognized code of systematic regulations, by which the people may be assembled for deliberation, and governed accordingly.
From what source did we derive these recognized rules?
From the British Parliament.
Why taken from the rules governing the British Parliament?

Because of the early relationship between England and the United States. These rules have remained unchanged in the English Parliament for several hundreds of years, and have become a prominent system of parliamentary authority.
Are these rules binding upon the people in their assemblies?
Not binding, although this universal custom becomes a general law, changed only when legislative bodies shall so specify.
Are these changes often made?

Not in the common assembling of the people. In the legislative branches of our government a different system of particular rules have been established, yet founded in and embracing all the essential rules of the common parliamentary law. Differences are adopted even in the two Houses of Congress, while in each state no prescribed rule is used in common. As a consequence of this disagreement, all legislative bodies provide themselves with a Legislative Manual, which becomes the standard authority for the government of that assembly.
When such legislative manual is adopted by any particular legislature, does it become authority for the next body following it?

It does not. Each legislative assembly is free to adopt the rules of its predecessors or to create new ones. In Congress, the same privilege is accepted, which, unlike the standard laws of the British Parliament, are subject to change to suit the particular wants of that body.
Would it be possible to establish a system of governing rules which would agree in every particular with that of Congress or other legislative assemblies?

It would not. As a people, we have no great respect for customs and precedents. This lack of respect for a permanent system is manifested in the endeavor of individuals to publish works which, in many particulars, are a departure from the general law.
Who is recognized as authority on parliamentary practice?
Luther S. Cushing, author of Cushing's Manual.
How is an assembly of the people called into an organized body?

There being no organization, it is effected by some individual in the assembly requesting the meeting to come to order for the transaction of the business calling them together. He also requests that some person be nominated for presiding officer. Such nomination being made, he puts the question for a vote of the assembly. Should the nominee be rejected, other nominations are made, until a choice is effected, whereupon the officer elected takes the chair, and proceeds in the same manner to complete the organization of the assembly, by choice of a secretary and such other officers, if any, as may be deemed necessary.
Does this election of officers constitute the permanent organization of the assembly?

It does not, although in all conventions and meetings where the people are called together for special purposes, no further organization is required; but should a permanency be desired, these officers are regarded as temporary, and to govern until a permanent organization is obtained and other officers elected to take their places.

The year is 1888 and you are in New York, America, sitting an oral examination in Parliamentary Rules. Answer each question put to you. Some questions follow on from earlier ones, so use the preceding questions and answers in this examination as context.
How is the new organization usually taken up and acted upon?

By referring it to a committee, who report such necessary articles for government as may have been suggested by the assembly, by individuals thereof, or upon their own judgment. Such report to be received or rejected, as the assembly deem proper.
Under such circumstances, is it usual to proceed in the election of permanent officers in the same manner as adopted in the election of temporary ones?
It is not. A committee is often selected, who, after consultation, recommend the election of certain individuals as qualified to fill the different offices required.
By what name is the presiding officer recognized?

In organized societies or legislative bodies, president or speaker; in political conventions, school meetings, or any of the numerous calls for an assembling of the people, chairman; in secret societies, by whatever name adopted by that society.
What name is applied to the recording officer?
Secretary or clerk.
Who are entitled to participate in the proceedings of an assembly?

When the assembling is a call for the whole people, all are entitled; in political conventions or caucuses, those who are recognized as electors of that party; in school meetings, electors of that school district; in societies or associations, those who have been duly elected as members.
How are these electors or representatives recognized as lawful members of any assembly?
Usually by a committee being appointed to examine the credentials presented by the different individuals who desire to take part in the assembly.
What are credentials? How obtained?

A certificate of qualification. In the Congress of the United States, certificates of membership are given by the presiding officers of the states where each member is elected; in the legislatures of states, from the county or parish officers where each member is elected; in political conventions, from state or local authority, according to the call.
When are the credentials or certificates of member-ship examined and acted upon?
The proper time for this investigation is after the temporary and before the permanent organization, by the committee appointed for that purpose.
When rival claims are presented, how proceed?
The committee report the claims, and such persons are entitled to a hearing, the decision of the matter being left with those whose rights of membership are not called in question.
Do the contesting members have the privileges of other members during the discussion of rights?
They do not. After being heard, it is their duty to withdraw, or if, by the courtesy of the assembly, they are permitted to remain, they have no right to further discuss or vote upon the subject.
